topics.title: Basics of Computer Graphics and its Applications. Video Display Devices - Refresh Cathode Ray Tubes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT416 - GRAPHICS AND MULTIMEDIA PRINCIPLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basics of Computer Graphics and its Applications. Video Display Devices - Refresh Cathode Ray Tubes # MODULE 1 — BASICS OF COMPUTER GRAPHICS & VIDEO DISPLAY DEVICES ## 1.1 Computer Graphics — Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > **Computer Graphics (CG)** is the discipline of creating, manipulating, storing, and displaying pictorial representations of objects and data using digital computers. It encompasses the mathematical and computational techniques used to convert two-dimensional (2D) or three-dimensional (3D) models of geometric objects into rasterized images, animated sequences, or interactive visual content. The field lies at the intersection of **computer science, mathematics (linear algebra, geometry, calculus), and visual perception**. The KTU 2024 scheme defines CG under the broader umbrella of *multimedia principles*, since the rendering pipeline (input → processing → raster → display) is identical for graphics, image, and video subsystems. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Digital Canvas" Imagine you are an artist, but instead of holding a brush, you are giving instructions to a microscopic robotic arm. The robotic arm has thousands of tiny hammers (called **pixels**), arranged in a strict grid. Each hammer can be told exactly: - **Where** to strike (coordinates: x, y). - **When** to strike (timing — the frame sequence). - **What color** to strike with (RGB intensity values, 0 to 255). Your job as a CG programmer is to write the *choreography* — the sequence of commands — that makes the hammers create meaningful pictures. The "robot arm" is the **Video Display Device (VDD)**, and the "score" is the **frame buffer** in memory. > [!NOTE] > **Core Insight:** Computer Graphics is fundamentally the science of *converting mathematical models into visual output* through a chain of transformations: **Modeling → Transformation → Illumination → Rasterization → Display.** --- ## 1.2 Applications of Computer Graphics (KTU High-Frequency Topic) The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists the following application domains — these appear almost every examination cycle: | **S.No** | **Application Domain** | **Real-World Use Case** | |:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** | Mechanical part design, VLSI chip layout, architectural blueprints | | 2 | **Presentation Graphics** | Pie charts, bar graphs, business dashboards in PowerPoint/Excel | | 3 | **Computer Art & Entertainment** | Films (Pixar, VFX), digital painting, NFT artwork | | 4 | **Entertainment (Games & Movies)** | Real-time 3D rendering, ray tracing, virtual production | | 5 | **Education & Training** | Flight simulators, surgical simulators, virtual labs | | 6 | **Visualization** | Scientific data viz (MRI scans, climate models, CFD airflow) | | 7 | **Image Processing** | Medical imaging (CT/MRI), satellite image enhancement | | 8 | **Graphical User Interfaces (GUI)** | Windows/macOS desktops, mobile UIs, iconography | | 9 | **Virtual Reality (VR) & Augmented Reality (AR)** | Meta Quest, Microsoft HoloLens, industrial training | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** When examiners ask "list the applications of CG," always structure them under the **nine KTU-standardized categories** above, not generic bullet points. This is a **3-mark question standard** in Part A. --- ## 1.3 Video Display Devices — The Output Hardware > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition** > **Video Display Devices** are the physical output hardware units that convert the digital signal (stored in the **frame buffer**) into a visible, human-perceivable image. They are the terminal stage of the CG pipeline. The KTU 2024 module-1 syllabus groups VDDs into two broad families: 1. **Refresh Cathode Ray Tubes (Refresh CRTs)** — the classical, foundational technology. 2. **Raster-Scan Displays & Random-Scan Displays** — the *architectural classifications* applied to CRTs and modern flat panels. This note set covers **(1) and (2)** with primary focus on the **Refresh CRT** as the KTU-mandated core. --- ## 1.4 Refresh Cathode Ray Tube (Refresh CRT) — Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024)** > A **Refresh Cathode Ray Tube (Refresh CRT)** is a vacuum-tube-based video display device that produces images by firing focused beams of **electrons** from one or more **electron guns** onto a phosphor-coated screen. The phosphor emits visible light upon electron impact, and the image must be **redrawn (refreshed)** typically **30 to 75 times per second** to maintain a stable, flicker-free visual due to the rapid decay of phosphor luminescence. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Light-Painter's Wand" Think of the CRT as a darkened room. Inside, there is a phosphor-coated wall (the **screen**) that glows wherever a beam of light hits it. A small electron cannon (the **electron gun**) sits at the back of the room. The cannon fires a single, very thin, very fast beam. Two magnetic "steering mirrors" (the **deflection yoke**) tilt the beam left-right and up-down. The beam sweeps across the wall in a **zig-zag pattern**, line by line, painting pixels one at a time — just like a hand writing a letter in cursive. The whole drawing must be redone **60 times every second**, or the wall goes dark before our eyes can perceive a complete image. That is, fundamentally, a **Refresh CRT**. > [!NOTE] > **Persistence — The Decay Constant:** The brightness of a phosphor pixel does not stay constant — it *decays exponentially* after the electron beam leaves. The time taken for the brightness to fall to **10% of its initial value** is called the **persistence** of the phosphor. Short persistence (≤ 60 ms) demands higher refresh rates; long persistence (> 100 ms) allows lower refresh but causes smearing in animations. # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Architecture of a Refresh CRT — The Six Subsystems The Refresh CRT is built from **six functional subsystems** working in concert. The KTU 2024 examiner expects you to be able to draw and label **all six** in any 14-mark question. ### 2.1.1 The Electron Gun - **Location:** Neck of the CRT, rear of the tube. - **Components inside:** Heated **cathode** (oxide-coated), **control grid**, **accelerating anode**, **focusing anode**. - **Function:** Thermionic emission of electrons from the cathode. The control grid modulates the **beam intensity** (number of electrons per unit time) — this directly controls pixel **brightness**. The accelerating anode (typically at **+25 kV to +50 kV**) accelerates electrons to roughly **0.1 to 0.4 times the speed of light**. > [!NOTE] > The electron gun is *not* a single piece — it is a stack of electrodes that progressively focus and accelerate the electron beam. The cathode emits; the grid modulates; the anode accelerates; the focusing electrodes narrow the beam to a tight spot on the screen. ### 2.1.2 The Focusing System - **Method 1 — Electrostatic Focusing:** A high-voltage electrostatic lens (cylindrical electrode) focuses the divergent electron beam into a tight spot. Cheap and lightweight. - **Method 2 — Magnetic Focusing:** External magnetic coil. Sharper focus, used in high-resolution CRTs. - **Spot Size:** The minimum diameter of the beam on the screen. Determines **resolution limit**. Typical values: **0.2 mm to 0.5 mm**. ### 2.1.3 The Deflection System (Deflection Yoke) - **Type:** Electromagnetic (mounted on the neck, outside the tube). - **Two Coils:** - **Horizontal Deflection Coils (H-DY):** Deflect beam **left–right** (X-axis). - **Vertical Deflection Coils (V-DY):** Deflect beam **up–down** (Y-axis). - **Sawtooth Waveform:** The current through the coils follows a *sawtooth* pattern — slow linear rise (the active sweep) followed by a fast flyback (retrace). The flyback corresponds to the **horizontal retrace** (end of one scanline) and **vertical retrace** (end of one full frame). ### 2.1.4 The Phosphor Screen - **Material:** Phosphor compounds (e.g., P22 — RGB triads; P31, P39 — monochrome green/white). - **Mechanism:** When high-energy electrons strike phosphor atoms, the atoms' electrons jump to a higher energy state and fall back, releasing photons (visible light). - **Color CRTs** use a **shadow-mask** with three electron guns (one each for R, G, B). The mask ensures each gun only hits its corresponding phosphor dot. ### 2.1.5 The Glass Envelope (Vacuum Tube) - Why vacuum? Electrons must travel ballistically — no collisions with air molecules. - Funnel-shaped: narrow neck (electron gun) flares to a wide front (screen). - Front face is slightly curved in older CRTs, nearly flat in later "flat-tube" designs. ### 2.1.6 The Persistence Characteristic - **Definition (revisited):** Time for luminance to decay to 1/10th of its excited value. - **Short persistence (10–60 µs):** Used for fast-moving animation. Requires high refresh. - **Long persistence (100 ms – several seconds):** Used for radar displays, static plots. --- ## 2.2 Refresh Rate and Flicker > [!IMPORTANT] > The image on a CRT screen is **not** static. Phosphor fades in milliseconds. To fool the human eye (which has a *persistence of vision* of approximately **1/16 s ≈ 62.5 ms**), the image must be redrawn faster than the eye can detect the fade-out. - **Critical Flicker Frequency (CFF):** The minimum refresh rate at which flicker is no longer perceived. For a typical CRT with medium-persistence phosphor, the **CFF ≈ 60 Hz**. - **Standard Refresh Rates:** 60 Hz (NTSC), 72 Hz, 75 Hz, 85 Hz, 100 Hz, 120 Hz. --- ## 2.3 Resolution, Aspect Ratio, and Pixel Geometry - **Resolution:** The total number of *addressable* pixels on the screen, expressed as **horizontal × vertical** (e.g., 1920 × 1080 = Full HD). - **Aspect Ratio:** The ratio of screen **width to height**. $$\text{Aspect Ratio} = \frac{W}{H} = \frac{\text{Horizontal Pixels}}{\text{Vertical Pixels}}$$ Common standards: **4:3 (1.333)**, **16:9 (1.778)**, **21:9 (2.333)**. > [!NOTE] > A pixel is *not* required to be square on the screen. The KTU syllabus distinguishes between **screen resolution** (addressable points) and **pixel aspect ratio** (the actual shape of one displayed dot). Modern systems use **square pixels** for simplicity, but the KTU 2024 syllabus asks you to know both. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following six equations are **guaranteed** to be tested across at least one Part A or Part B question in KTU 2024 ESE. | **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Physical Meaning** | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---| | 1 | Total Pixels (N) | $N = N_x \times N_y$ | pixels | Resolution of one full frame | | 2 | Aspect Ratio (AR) | $AR = \dfrac{N_x}{N_y}$ | dimensionless | Width-to-height ratio | | 3 | Frame Time (T_f) | $T_f = \dfrac{1}{f_r}$ | seconds | Time to draw one full frame | | 4 | Horizontal Frequency (f_h) | $f_h = N_y \times f_r$ | Hz | Times per second the beam returns to the start of a new line | | 5 | Time per Pixel (T_p) | $T_p = \dfrac{1}{N_x \times f_h}$ | seconds | Beam dwell time on one pixel | | 6 | **Bandwidth (BW)** | $BW \approx \dfrac{N_x \times N_y \times f_r}{2}$ | Hz | Maximum video signal frequency | > [!WARNING] > **Bandwidth factor of ½:** Many KTU textbooks (Hearn & Baker, Foley et al.) use the factor **0.7** instead of **0.5** because the worst-case square wave is approximated as 70% of a perfect rectangular transition. The "official" KTU simplified formula uses the factor **½** — confirm with your course handout. We present both for completeness: $$BW_{\text{ideal (square wave)}} = \frac{N_x \cdot N_y \cdot f_r}{2} \quad ; \quad BW_{\text{empirical (KTU)}} \approx 0.7 \cdot N_x \cdot N_y \cdot f_r$$ --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Television Engineering (PAL/NTSC/SECAM):** The CRT was the dominant display from 1930 to 2005. TV broadcast standards (e.g., NTSC = 525 lines, 60 Hz) were *literally designed around* CRT physics — not the other way around. - **Medical Imaging (Legacy):** Old ECG/EEG oscilloscopes and ultrasound screens used long-persistence CRTs. - **Air Traffic Control & Military Radar:** Even today, *some* military systems retain CRTs because of their superior **motion-blur characteristics** in tracking fast-moving targets. - **Oscilloscopes & Spectrum Analyzers:** The analog oscilloscope is essentially a *single-beam refresh CRT* with exceptionally high bandwidth (GHz range). - **Historical Computing:** IBM 3270 terminals, Apple Macintosh (pre-1990), Sun workstations — all CRT-based. > [!NOTE] > **Why study CRTs in 2024?** The KTU 2024 syllabus retains CRTs as the *conceptual foundation* for understanding **raster-scan, refresh, frame buffer, interlacing, and pixel clock** — concepts that map directly to LCD, OLED, and MicroLED operation. # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bandwidth of a Video Display System > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the single most-tested derivation in KTU 2024 Module 1. You **must** know it cold. **Given:** A CRT display has a resolution of $N_x \times N_y$ pixels, refreshed at a frequency $f_r$ Hz, with one pixel being either ON (white = 1) or OFF (black = 0). **Find:** The maximum required video signal bandwidth. --- **Step 1 — Identify the Worst-Case Signal Pattern** The *highest-frequency* video signal corresponds to the *fastest* possible alternation between black and white pixels. This happens when **alternating columns** of black and white pixels are displayed: $$\text{Signal: } 1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0, \ldots$$ This is a square wave at the *pixel frequency*. --- **Step 2 — Determine the Time per Pixel** The total number of pixels drawn per second is the **pixel rate**: $$R_{\text{pixel}} = N_x \times N_y \times f_r$$ The time the beam spends on one pixel is: $$T_p = \frac{1}{R_{\text{pixel}}} = \frac{1}{N_x \cdot N_y \cdot f_r}$$ --- **Step 3 — Determine the Period of the Worst-Case Square Wave** One full cycle of the alternating black-white pattern spans **2 pixels** (one black + one white). Therefore: $$T_{\text{cycle}} = 2 \cdot T_p = \frac{2}{N_x \cdot N_y \cdot f_r}$$ --- **Step 4 — Compute the Maximum Frequency** The maximum frequency (the bandwidth) is the reciprocal of the period: $$f_{\max} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{N_x \cdot N_y \cdot f_r}{2}$$ --- **Step 5 — Final Result (Boxed Formula)** $$\boxed{\,BW = \frac{N_x \cdot N_y \cdot f_r}{2}\,}$$ The **factor of ½** arises because one full electrical cycle requires **two pixels** (high then low). The empirical KTU factor of **0.7** accounts for the non-ideal rise/fall times of real amplifiers. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > [!NOTE] > This problem type is a guaranteed 7-mark sub-part in KTU 2024. **Problem:** A CRT monitor has a resolution of **1280 × 1024** pixels and operates at a refresh rate of **85 Hz** (non-interlaced). Compute (a) the horizontal retrace frequency, (b) the time per pixel, and (c) the required video bandwidth. **Given Data:** - $N_x = 1280$ pixels per line - $N_y = 1024$ lines per frame - $f_r = 85$ Hz (refresh rate) - (Assume 5% of horizontal time is retrace — a common KTU extension) --- **Part (a) — Horizontal Retrace Frequency (Horizontal Sync Frequency)** The horizontal sync fires once per scanline, $N_y$ times per frame: $$\begin{aligned} f_h &= N_y \times f_r \\ &= 1024 \times 85 \\ &= 87{,}040 \text{ Hz} \\ f_h &\approx 87.04 \text{ kHz} \end{aligned}$$ --- **Part (b) — Time per Pixel** Total pixels per second: $$\begin{aligned} R_{\text{pixel}} &= N_x \times N_y \times f_r \\ &= 1280 \times 1024 \times 85 \\ &= 111{,}411{,}200 \text{ pixels/s} \\ R_{\text{pixel}} &\approx 1.114 \times 10^{8} \text{ pixels/s} \end{aligned}$$ Time per pixel: $$\begin{aligned} T_p &= \frac{1}{R_{\text{pixel}}} \\ &= \frac{1}{1.114 \times 10^{8}} \\ T_p &\approx 8.976 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} \\ T_p &\approx 8.98 \text{ ns (nanoseconds)} \end{aligned}$$ --- **Part (c) — Video Bandwidth** Using the ideal square-wave formula: $$\begin{aligned} BW_{\text{ideal}} &= \frac{N_x \cdot N_y \cdot f_r}{2} \\ &= \frac{1280 \times 1024 \times 85}{2} \\ &= \frac{111{,}411{,}200}{2} \\ BW_{\text{ideal}} &= 55{,}705{,}600 \text{ Hz} \\ BW_{\text{ideal}} &\approx 55.7 \text{ MHz} \end{aligned}$$ Using the empirical KTU factor of 0.7: $$\begin{aligned} BW_{\text{empirical}} &= 0.7 \times N_x \times N_y \times f_r \\ &= 0.7 \times 1280 \times 1024 \times 85 \\ &\approx 77.99 \text{ MHz} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analog CRT monitors** of that era (e.g., 2002-era Samsung SyncMaster) were specified with **~110 MHz pixel-clock bandwidth** to leave a comfortable engineering margin. The KTU formula gives the *minimum* theoretical requirement. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Aspect Ratio from Resolution **Problem:** A display advertises a resolution of **1600 × 1200** pixels. What is the aspect ratio? $$\begin{aligned} AR &= \frac{N_x}{N_y} \\ &= \frac{1600}{1200} \\ &= \frac{4}{3} \\ AR &= 4{:}3 \quad \text{(Standard XGA / Classic Computer Display)} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > A quick mental check: any resolution $N_x : N_y$ that reduces to **4:3** or **16:9** is a "standard" aspect ratio. Common 16:9 resolutions: 1920×1080, 2560×1440, 3840×2160, 7680×4320 (8K UHD). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common CRT Resolutions and Their Bandwidths (at 60 Hz) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners *frequently* give a table like this and ask you to fill in the blank columns. Memorize the highlighted band. | **Standard Name** | **Resolution $N_x \times N_y$** | **Aspect Ratio** | **Total Pixels (N)** | **BW @ 60 Hz (MHz, ideal)** | **BW @ 60 Hz (MHz, 0.7 factor)** | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | VGA | 640 × 480 | 4:3 | 307,200 | 9.22 | 12.90 | | SVGA | 800 × 600 | 4:3 | 480,000 | 14.40 | 20.16 | | **XGA** | **1024 × 768** | **4:3** | **786,432** | **23.59** | **33.03** | | SXGA | 1280 × 1024 | 5:4 | 1,310,720 | 39.32 | 55.05 | | UXGA | 1600 × 1200 | 4:3 | 1,920,000 | 57.60 | 80.64 | | HD 720p | 1280 × 720 | 16:9 | 921,600 | 27.65 | 38.71 | | **Full HD 1080p** | **1920 × 1080** | **16:9** | **2,073,600** | **62.21** | **87.09** | | QHD / 2K | 2560 × 1440 | 16:9 | 3,686,400 | 110.59 | 154.83 | | **4K UHD** | **3840 × 2160** | **16:9** | **8,294,400** | **248.83** | **348.36** | The **bolded** rows (XGA, Full HD, 4K) are the three highest-priority rows for KTU 2024 Part B questions. # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Refresh CRT > [!NOTE] > This diagram maps the **six subsystems** of the CRT and the **flow of the electron beam** from emission to phosphor impact. Use it as a labeled substitute for the physical cross-sectional drawing that the KTU examiner expects in any 7-mark "explain CRT with diagram" question. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Heated Cathode
Thermionic Emission] B[Control Grid
Intensity Modulation] C[Accelerating Anode
25 to 50 kV] D[Focusing Electrodes
Electrostatic Lens] E[Deflection Yoke H
X Axis Steering] F[Deflection Yoke V
Y Axis Steering] G[Shadow Mask
Color CRTs Only] H[Phosphor Screen
RGB Triad] I[Light Output
Visible Image] J[Flyback Retrace
Horizontal and Vertical] K[Frame Buffer
Digital Image Data] K --> B A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H H --> I F -.-> J J -.-> E ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Raster-Scan Process (KTU-Standard Drawing) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **raster-scan pattern** is the most-tested diagram in KTU Module 1. The flow below shows the **sequencing logic** of the electron beam as it paints one full frame. ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Start of Frame] p1[Set Beam to Top Left
Origin 0,0] p2[Move Beam to First Scanline
Top Row] p3[Sweep Left to Right
Painting Pixels] p4{End of Scanline?} p5[Horizontal Retrace
Right to Left] p6[Move to Next Line Down] p7{All Lines Drawn?} p8[Vertical Retrace
Bottom to Top] p9[Frame Complete] p10[Refresh Next Frame] start --> p1 --> p2 --> p3 --> p4 p4 -- No --> p5 --> p6 --> p3 p4 -- Yes --> p7 p7 -- No --> p2 p7 -- Yes --> p8 --> p9 --> p10 --> p1 ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Computer Graphics Pipeline (End-to-End) > [!NOTE] > This diagram shows how the CRT sits at the *end* of the entire CG pipeline. It is a 14-mark-question-worthy diagram showing the **complete flow** from 3D model to displayed image. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[3D Geometric Model
Vertices and Faces] B[Modeling Transforms
Translate, Rotate, Scale] C[Viewing Transform
Camera Setup] D[Projection Transform
Perspective or Orthographic] E[Clipping and Hidden Surface Removal] F[Illumination and Shading
Phong, Gouraud] G[Rasterization
Convert to Pixels] H[Frame Buffer
Digital Pixel Array] I[Video Controller
Pixel Clock] J[Refresh CRT
Electron Beam Display] A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I --> J ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Comparative Block — CRT vs Modern Flat-Panel Display > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 frequently asks: *"Compare CRT with LCD displays."* The diagram below gives a side-by-side functional block view. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CRT_TECH[Refresh CRT] c1[Frame Buffer] c2[Video Amplifier] c3[Electron Gun] c4[Phosphor Excited] c5[Light from Phosphor] c1 --> c2 --> c3 --> c4 --> c5 end subgraph LCD_TECH[Modern LCD or OLED] l1[Frame Buffer] l2[TFT Matrix Driver] l3[Liquid Crystal Cell or OLED Pixel] l4[Polarized Backlight or Self Emission] l5[Visible Light Output] l1 --> l2 --> l3 --> l4 --> l5 end ``` # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP > [!NOTE] > The following questions follow the **exact mark distribution, internal-choice pattern, and Bloom's-level cognitive tagging** of the KTU 2024 ESE. Model answers follow KTU board valuation key style. --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style] **Define Computer Graphics. List any six major application areas.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (1 mark):** Computer Graphics is the discipline of generating, manipulating, and displaying visual content using digital computers, involving the conversion of mathematical/geometric models into rasterized images. **Six Application Areas (½ mark each = 3 marks):** 1. **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** — Mechanical, civil, electronic design. 2. **Presentation Graphics** — Charts, graphs in business reports. 3. **Computer Art & Entertainment** — Movies, animation, digital art. 4. **Education & Training** — Simulators (flight, surgical, driving). 5. **Visualization** — Scientific, medical, financial data. 6. **Graphical User Interfaces (GUI)** — Operating system desktops and mobile UIs. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style] **What is a Refresh Cathode Ray Tube? Explain the concept of refresh rate.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Refresh CRT Definition (1½ marks):** A Refresh CRT is a vacuum-tube display device that produces images by directing a focused electron beam onto a phosphor-coated screen. The phosphor emits light upon electron impact, but the luminescence decays rapidly. Hence the image must be **redrawn (refreshed)** continuously. **Refresh Rate (1½ marks):** Refresh rate is the number of times per second the entire image (frame) is redrawn on the screen, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. To avoid **flicker**, the refresh rate must exceed the **Critical Flicker Frequency (CFF)** of human vision, which is typically around **60 Hz**. Standard CRT refresh rates are **60 Hz, 72 Hz, 75 Hz, 85 Hz, and 100 Hz**. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU 2024 Mark Split Convention:** Each Part B question is split as **Part (a) = 7 marks + Part (b) = 7 marks**. Internal choice means you answer **EITHER Option A OR Option B**, not both. --- ### Option A — 14 Marks #### Part (a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style] **Explain the working principle of a Refresh Cathode Ray Tube with a neat block diagram. List the major components and their functions.** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **1. Working Principle (2 marks):** A Refresh CRT operates on the principle of **cathode-ray emission and phosphor luminescence**. Electrons emitted from a heated cathode are accelerated, focused into a narrow beam, and directed by electromagnetic deflection coils onto a phosphor-coated screen. The phosphor emits visible light at the point of impact. Because the luminescence decays quickly (typically in milliseconds), the entire image must be **redrawn (refreshed)** 60 to 85 times per second to create the illusion of a stable, continuous image — leveraging the human eye's **persistence of vision** (~62.5 ms). **2. Block Diagram (2 marks):** ``` [Frame Buffer] → [Video Amplifier] → [Electron Gun] → [Focusing System] ↓ [Display] ← [Phosphor Screen] ← [Shadow Mask] ← [Deflection Yoke H+V] (Retrace control feeds back to deflection yoke) ``` **3. Major Components and Functions (3 marks):** | **Component** | **Function** | |:---|:---| | **Electron Gun** | Emits electrons via thermionic emission; modulates beam intensity. | | **Focusing System** | Electrostatic/magnetic lens that narrows the beam to a sharp spot. | | **Deflection Yoke** | Two electromagnetic coils (H, V) that steer the beam across the screen. | | **Phosphor Screen** | Coated with phosphor compounds; emits visible light on electron impact. | | **Shadow Mask** (color CRTs) | Metal plate with holes; aligns beam with R, G, or B phosphor dots. | | **Glass Envelope** | Maintains vacuum; provides structural shape. | --- #### Part (b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style] **A CRT monitor has a resolution of 1024 × 768 pixels and a refresh rate of 75 Hz. Calculate (i) the total number of pixels, (ii) the aspect ratio, (iii) the horizontal sync frequency, (iv) the time per pixel, and (v) the required video bandwidth.** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Given:** $N_x = 1024$, $N_y = 768$, $f_r = 75$ Hz **(i) Total Pixels (1 mark):** $$\begin{aligned} N &= N_x \times N_y \\ &= 1024 \times 768 \\ N &= 786{,}432 \text{ pixels per frame} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Aspect Ratio (1 mark):** $$\begin{aligned} AR &= \frac{N_x}{N_y} \\ &= \frac{1024}{768} \\ &= \frac{4}{3} \\ AR &= 4{:}3 \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Horizontal Sync Frequency (1 mark):** $$\begin{aligned} f_h &= N_y \times f_r \\ &= 768 \times 75 \\ f_h &= 57{,}600 \text{ Hz} = 57.6 \text{ kHz} \end{aligned}$$ **(iv) Time per Pixel (1 mark):** $$\begin{aligned} R_{\text{pixel}} &= N_x \times N_y \times f_r \\ &= 1024 \times 768 \times 75 \\ &= 58{,}982{,}400 \text{ pixels/s} \\ T_p &= \frac{1}{R_{\text{pixel}}} = \frac{1}{5.898 \times 10^7} \\ T_p &\approx 1.695 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s} = 16.95 \text{ ns} \end{aligned}$$ **(v) Video Bandwidth (3 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} BW_{\text{ideal}} &= \frac{N_x \cdot N_y \cdot f_r}{2} \\ &= \frac{1024 \times 768 \times 75}{2} \\ &= \frac{58{,}982{,}400}{2} \\ BW_{\text{ideal}} &= 29{,}491{,}200 \text{ Hz} \approx 29.49 \text{ MHz} \end{aligned}$$ **With empirical KTU factor (alternative):** $$BW_{\text{empirical}} = 0.7 \times 1024 \times 768 \times 75 \approx 41.29 \text{ MHz}$$ --- ### Option B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative) #### Part (a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style] **Discuss the different applications of Computer Graphics in detail with suitable examples for each.** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **1. Computer-Aided Design (CAD) (1 mark):** Used by mechanical, civil, and electrical engineers to design parts, buildings, and circuits. Examples: AutoCAD, SolidWorks, CATIA. CAD allows precise dimensioning, stress simulation, and prototyping before physical manufacturing. **2. Presentation Graphics (1 mark):** Used to display statistical and financial data in the form of bar charts, pie charts, line graphs, and 3D plots. Tools: Microsoft PowerPoint, Excel, Tableau. Common in business reports and academic presentations. **3. Computer Art and Entertainment (1 mark):** Used to create 2D and 3D animated films, video games, and visual effects (VFX). Examples: Pixar films (Toy Story), Unreal Engine games, Adobe After Effects. Involves modeling, texturing, lighting, and rendering. **4. Education and Training (1 mark):** Computer-generated simulations provide risk-free training. Examples: Flight simulators (Boeing, Airbus pilot training), surgical simulators, military combat training (VR-based). Reduces cost and increases safety. **5. Visualization (1 mark):** Used to represent complex scientific, medical, and engineering data visually. Examples: MRI/CT scan rendering, weather simulation (NCMRWF), CFD airflow over aircraft wings, molecular visualization in drug discovery. **6. Graphical User Interfaces (GUI) (1 mark):** The dominant mode of human-computer interaction. Examples: Windows 11 desktop, macOS Finder, Android UI, iOS Springboard. Includes icons, windows, menus, and pointer devices. **7. Virtual Reality and Augmented Reality (1 mark):** VR creates fully immersive 3D environments (Meta Quest, HTC Vive). AR overlays graphics on the real world (Microsoft HoloLens, Pokémon Go, industrial maintenance apps). --- #### Part (b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2022 Style] **Derive the expression for video bandwidth of a refresh CRT display. A display has 1920 × 1080 resolution at 60 Hz refresh. Compute the bandwidth. Why is the empirical factor 0.7 often used?** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Derivation (3 marks):** The video signal is most demanding when alternate black and white pixels are displayed (square wave at pixel frequency). $$\begin{aligned} \text{Pixels per second} &= N_x \cdot N_y \cdot f_r \\ T_p &= \frac{1}{N_x \cdot N_y \cdot f_r} \\ T_{\text{cycle}} &= 2 \cdot T_p \quad \text{(one full square-wave cycle spans 2 pixels)} \\ BW &= \frac{1}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{N_x \cdot N_y \cdot f_r}{2} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{BW = \frac{N_x \cdot N_y \cdot f_r}{2}}$$ **Numerical Calculation for 1920 × 1080 @ 60 Hz (2 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} BW_{\text{ideal}} &= \frac{1920 \times 1080 \times 60}{2} \\ &= \frac{124{,}416{,}000}{2} \\ BW_{\text{ideal}} &= 62{,}208{,}000 \text{ Hz} \approx 62.21 \text{ MHz} \end{aligned}$$ **Empirical Factor 0.7 (2 marks):** The factor **0.5** assumes an *ideal square wave* with **zero rise and fall time**. In real electronic circuits, transitions between black and white pixels are not perfectly sharp — they take a finite time determined by the **amplifier bandwidth** and the **CRT capacitance**. The factor **0.7** (sometimes written as 0.35 per half-cycle) provides a more realistic estimate, accounting for the **Gaussian-shaped pulses** of real video signals. Therefore, the practical bandwidth is: $$BW_{\text{empirical}} \approx 0.7 \cdot N_x \cdot N_y \cdot f_r \approx 87.09 \text{ MHz for Full HD 1080p @ 60 Hz}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of ½** in the bandwidth formula — losing 1 mark per instance. > 2. **Confusing horizontal sync frequency with vertical refresh rate** — $f_h$ is in **tens of kHz**, $f_r$ is in **Hz**. > 3. **Not mentioning the shadow mask** when describing color CRT operation — costs 1 mark. > 4. **Calling the screen "glass"** without saying "phosphor-coated" — the KTU 2024 rubric penalizes vague terminology. > 5. **Skipping the persistence-of-vision reasoning** when explaining why refresh is needed — 1 mark lost. > 6. **Writing `N = Nx + Ny`** instead of `N = Nx × Ny` — major error in any total-pixels calculation. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!NOTE] > **Last-minute revision checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1** - **Computer Graphics (CG):** The science of generating visual output from mathematical models. Pipeline: **Model → Transform → Project → Raster → Display**. - **Nine KTU-standard application areas:** CAD, Presentation Graphics, Computer Art, Entertainment, Education & Training, Visualization, Image Processing, GUI, VR/AR. - **Refresh CRT definition:** Vacuum tube; electron beam strikes phosphor; image redrawn at **30–85 Hz** to overcome phosphor decay and persistence of vision. - **Six components:** Electron gun, focusing system, deflection yoke, phosphor screen, shadow mask (color CRTs), glass envelope. - **Deflection yoke:** Electromagnetic coils; H-coil sweeps horizontally, V-coil sweeps vertically. Driven by **sawtooth waveforms**. - **Aspect Ratio = $N_x / N_y$** — common values: 4:3, 16:9, 21:9. - **Refresh Rate ($f_r$):** Frames per second, measured in Hz. Standard: 60 Hz minimum to avoid flicker. - **Horizontal Sync Frequency ($f_h$):** $f_h = N_y \times f_r$. Typically **30–100 kHz**. - **Time per Pixel ($T_p$):** $T_p = 1 / (N_x \cdot N_y \cdot f_r)$. Typically **5–20 ns**. - **Bandwidth (BW):** $BW = (N_x \cdot N_y \cdot f_r) / 2$ (ideal) or $\approx 0.7 \cdot N_x \cdot N_y \cdot f_r$ (empirical KTU). - **Persistence:** Time for phosphor luminance to decay to 10% of peak. Short persistence for animation, long for radar. - **Critical Flicker Frequency (CFF):** ~60 Hz for medium-persistence phosphors. Below this, the human eye perceives flicker. - **Pixel Aspect Ratio vs Screen Aspect Ratio:** Know the difference; KTU 2024 may ask. - **Interlacing (advanced):** Even lines in one field, odd lines in the next; halves bandwidth but introduces flicker. KTU 2024 may ask for a 1-sentence definition.

topics.title: Random Scan Displays and Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT416 - GRAPHICS AND MULTIMEDIA PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Random Scan Displays and Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Random Scan Display (Vector Display / Stroke-Writing Display / Calligraphic Display):** A CRT-based display technology in which the electron beam is directed (deflected) along *arbitrary, non-sequential paths* on the phosphor screen to directly trace the geometric primitives (lines, curves, characters) of a picture, with the beam switched off (blanked) during repositioning between primitives. The picture is stored in memory as a list of vector drawing commands and is continuously refreshed at a frequency that prevents flicker.

**Course Outcome Alignment (CO Mapping for PEAIT416):**
- **CO1 / CO2:** Understand the architecture, working principles, and comparative performance of various display technologies used in computer graphics and multimedia.

**Standard Performance Metrics (Mandatory KTU 2024 Terminology):**
- **Refresh Rate:** Typically **30 Hz to 60 Hz** (must be ≥ 30 Hz to avoid visible flicker).
- **Resolution:** Up to **4096 × 4096** addressable points (much higher than early raster systems).
- **Beam Deflection:** Electrostatic (faster, for high-precision oscilloscopes) or Electromagnetic (for TV-sized screens).
- **Phosphor Persistence:** Standard P1 / P4 / P31 phosphors, with decay times of **1 ms to 100 ms**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Eternal Chalk Artist" 🖍️

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a blindfolded artist holding a piece of chalk:** Every second, the artist must *redraw the entire picture* on a blackboard before the chalk dust (phosphor glow) fades away. The artist is not allowed to "fill in" regions — they can only draw connected lines by moving the chalk tip across the board.
>
> - The **chalk** = the **electron beam**.
> - The **blackboard** = the **phosphor screen**.
> - The **list of strokes** the artist memorizes = the **Display File / Display List** stored in memory.
> - The **fading chalk dust** = the **phosphor persistence** (why we need to refresh).
> - The **blindfold** = the **beam is invisible** when moving (it must be *blanked*) between strokes so it does not scribble stray marks.
>
> This is exactly how a **Random Scan System** works. The picture is **line-by-line**, redrawn **30–60 times per second**, with the beam *jumping* (not scanning) from one endpoint to the next.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Random Scan Displays

Random scan systems fall into **two principal hardware generations**, both of which are examinable under KTU Module 1:

| Generation | Storage Mechanism | Refresh Hardware | KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type 1** | Requires continuous CPU/Controller refresh from **Display File** | External vector & character generator | **Refreshed Random Scan** |
| **Type 2** | Built-in **storage mesh** behind phosphor retains the image | None — beam is fired only on image change | **Direct-View Storage Tube (DVST)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** DVST is often incorrectly classified as "non-refreshed raster" by students. It is **NOT** raster at all — it is still a **vector/random scan** device, but with intrinsic image storage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of a Random Scan CRT

The random scan system is built on the classical **Cathode Ray Tube (CRT)**. Unlike a TV, the electron beam is *not* locked into horizontal scan lines — it is steered by an **X–Y deflection system**.

### 2.1.1 Operational Sequence (The 5-Step Refresh Loop)

1. **Instruction Fetch:** The Display Controller reads the *next* command from the **Display File (Display List)** stored in dedicated refresh memory.
2. **Coordinate Decoding:** The end-coordinates $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ of the vector are loaded into the **Digital-to-Analog Converters (DACs)** — one DAC for X, one for Y.
3. **Beam Deflection:** The DAC voltages drive the **X-Y deflection plates/coils** to move the electron beam to $(x_1, y_1)$. During this motion the beam is *blanked* (grid voltage cut off).
4. **Stroke Drawing:** The unblanked beam travels from $(x_1, y_1)$ to $(x_2, y_2)$. The **Vector Generator (Stroke Generator)** produces a staircase of analog voltages to approximate the straight line.
5. **Repeat / Cycle:** The cycle repeats for every command in the display file. Once the list ends, the controller loops back to the top — completing one full *refresh cycle*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Random" Scan?** Because the beam can go from coordinate $(0,0)$ to $(4000,3000)$ and *then* to $(100, 200)$ in successive steps — there is **no fixed left-to-right, top-to-bottom order** as in raster. Hence, the *traversal order is random with respect to the screen geometry*.

---

### 2.1.2 Vector Generation Mathematics

A true straight line on a continuous CRT face is physically impossible — the beam is driven by quantized digital values, producing a **staircase approximation**. The quality depends on the **DAC bit-depth**.

For a line from $(x_1, y_1)$ to $(x_2, y_2)$, the deflection voltages are ramped linearly with time $t$:

$$x(t) = x_1 + (x_2 - x_1) \cdot \frac{t}{T}$$

$$y(t) = y_1 + (y_2 - y_1) \cdot \frac{t}{T}$$

where $T$ is the total time taken to draw the stroke.

The **staircase resolution** (the smallest visible step) is:

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{\text{full-scale}}}{2^n - 1}$$

where $n$ = DAC bit-depth and $V_{\text{full-scale}}$ is the screen-deflection voltage.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2-Mark Quick-Fire:** A **10-bit DAC** with $\pm 5\,\text{V}$ full-scale gives a staircase step of $\Delta \approx 9.77\,\text{mV}$ — translating to roughly **1/1024 of the screen width**, i.e. one pixel in a 1024-pixel system.

---

## 2.2 Architecture of a Random Scan System

The full KTU-prescribed block diagram has **six** functional units. Memorize these — they appear almost every year in part (a) of 14-mark questions.

| # | Block | Function | Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Display File Memory** | Stores the application program as a command list (`LINE`, `MOVE`, `CHAR`, `POLY`) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | **Display Controller** | Fetches & interprets commands; coordinates all timing | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | **Vector (Stroke) Generator** | Produces analog X–Y ramps for straight-line drawing | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | **Character Generator** | Decodes ASCII → stroke font (typically 7×9 or 9×12 dot/stroke matrix) | ⭐⭐ |
| 5 | **X-Y Deflection Subsystem** | DACs + X-plates/Y-plates (electrostatic) or X-coils/Y-coils (magnetic) | ⭐⭐ |
| 6 | **CRT with Phosphor Screen** | Visual output device (P1, P4, P31 phosphor) | ⭐ |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every** KTU-testable equation for Module 1 random scan topics. Bookmark this for revision.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Standard KTU Boundary Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minimum Refresh Frequency (flicker-free) | $f_{\text{min}} \geq 1 / t_{\text{persistence}}$ | $t_{\text{persistence}} \leq 1/60\,\text{s}$ |
| 2 | Refresh Cycle Time | $T_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{\text{stroke},i}}{1}$ | Typical: 10–20 ms for $N < 200$ |
| 3 | Beam Spot Velocity (Vector Gen.) | $v = \dfrac{\sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}}{T_{\text{stroke}}}$ | Limited to $\leq 1$ inch/$\mu$s |
| 4 | Staircase Step (Quantization) | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{\text{fs}}}{2^n - 1}$ | $n$ = DAC bits (8/10/12) |
| 5 | Phosphor Decay (exponential) | $I(t) = I_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}$ | $\tau$ = 1 ms (P31), 100 ms (P1) |
| 6 | Vector Memory Required | $M = N_{\text{vectors}} \times \text{bytes/vector}$ | Typical: 8 bytes/vector (16-bit X,Y) |
| 7 | DVST Storage Time | Theoretically **infinite** (no refresh needed) | Up to several hours |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where are Random Scan Displays still relevant in 2025–26 engineering practice?**
>
> 1. **Oscilloscopes (Tektronix, Keysight, Rohde & Schwarz)** — still use *analog random scan CRTs* (or emulated equivalents) for the highest single-shot bandwidth displays.
> 2. **Air Traffic Control Radar (PPI Displays — Plan Position Indicator)** — historical vector displays mapping aircraft as vectors on a sweeping radial.
> 3. **Legacy CAD/CAM systems (Apollo, early SGI IRIS)** — random scan terminals like the *Tektronix 4014* dominated engineering workstations in the 1970s–80s.
> 4. **Avionics Head-Up Displays (HUDs)** — vector graphics drawn on a CRT combiner in fighter aircraft (e.g., F-16, Mirage 2000).
> 5. **Medical Imaging (Early CT, Ultrasound A-mode)** — vector-style traces of signal amplitude vs. time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Number of Vectors for a Flicker-Free Display

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Standard KTU Numericals):** A random scan display uses a CRT with phosphor decay time constant $\tau = 25\,\text{ms}$. Each vector takes $t_s = 15\,\mu\text{s}$ to draw (stroke time). Determine the **maximum number of vectors** that can be drawn per refresh frame without visible flicker, assuming a minimum refresh rate of $f = 40\,\text{Hz}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the physics constraint.**
For flicker-free display, the time between successive illuminations of any one spot must be less than the phosphor persistence time:

$$T_{\text{refresh}} \leq t_{\text{persistence}} = \tau$$

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum allowable refresh period.**

$$T_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{min}}} = \frac{1}{40\,\text{Hz}} = 0.025\,\text{s} = 25\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the time available per vector.**
Since all $N$ vectors must be drawn within one refresh cycle, and each takes $t_s$:

$$T_{\text{total strokes}} = N \cdot t_s$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for $N$.**

$$N \cdot t_s \leq T_{\text{max}}$$

$$N \leq \frac{T_{\text{max}}}{t_s} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}}{15 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s}}$$

$$N \leq \frac{25{,}000}{15} = 1666.67$$

**Step 5 — Take the floor (integer number of vectors).**

$$\boxed{N_{\text{max}} = 1666 \text{ vectors per refresh frame}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Vector Generator Ramp Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Derive the time-varying deflection voltages $V_x(t)$ and $V_y(t)$ required to draw a vector from $(2, 3)$ to $(10, 7)$ in $T = 8\,\mu\text{s}$ on a 12-bit DAC system with full-scale voltage $V_{\text{fs}} = \pm 10\,\text{V}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert pixel coordinates to analog voltages.**
A 12-bit DAC spans a range of $2^{12} - 1 = 4095$ codes.

$$V_{\text{per code}} = \frac{V_{\text{fs}}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{10\,\text{V}}{4095} = 2.442 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V/code}$$

**Step 2 — Compute start and end voltages.**

For $x$: $\; V_x(0) = 2 \times 2.442\,\text{mV} = 4.884\,\text{mV}$ and $\; V_x(T) = 10 \times 2.442\,\text{mV} = 24.42\,\text{mV}$.

For $y$: $\; V_y(0) = 3 \times 2.442\,\text{mV} = 7.326\,\text{mV}$ and $\; V_y(T) = 7 \times 2.442\,\text{mV} = 17.094\,\text{mV}$.

**Step 3 — Apply the linear ramp equation.**

$$V_x(t) = V_x(0) + \left[V_x(T) - V_x(0)\right] \cdot \frac{t}{T}$$

$$V_y(t) = V_y(0) + \left[V_y(T) - V_y(0)\right] \cdot \frac{t}{T}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$V_x(t) = 4.884\,\text{mV} + (19.536\,\text{mV}) \cdot \frac{t}{8\,\mu s}$$

$$V_y(t) = 7.326\,\text{mV} + (9.768\,\text{mV}) \cdot \frac{t}{8\,\mu s}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify.**

$$\boxed{V_x(t) = 4.884 + 2.442\,t \quad [\text{mV},\ t \text{ in }\mu s]}$$

$$\boxed{V_y(t) = 7.326 + 1.221\,t \quad [\text{mV},\ t \text{ in }\mu s]}$$

The **slope ratio** $2.442 : 1.221 = 2 : 1$ correctly reflects $\Delta y / \Delta x = (7-3)/(10-2) = 4/8 = 1/2$. ✔

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Random Scan vs. Raster Scan

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Type:** "Compare and contrast Random Scan and Raster Scan display systems. Discuss their relative merits with suitable diagrams." — *This is a KTU-favourite question, appearing in nearly every December and June session since 2019.*

| Comparison Axis | Random Scan (Vector) | Raster Scan (TV/Modern) |
|---|---|---|
| **Beam Trajectory** | Arbitrary, line-by-line | Fixed left→right, top→bottom |
| **Picture Storage** | Display File (command list) | Frame Buffer (pixel array) |
| **Refresh Source** | Display Controller, cyclic | Video Controller at line rate |
| **Memory Required** | Low (KB range) | High (MB to GB range) |
| **Resolution** | Very high (vector-edge smooth) | Limited by pixel matrix |
| **Color Capability** | Limited (beam intensity only) | Full color via RGB triad |
| **Flicker Sensitivity** | High (depends on file length) | Low (constant refresh) |
| **Draw Time** | Variable, depends on picture | Constant per frame |
| **Line Smoothness** | Smooth (vector drawn at sub-pixel accuracy) | Jagged ("the jaggies"/aliasing) |
| **Cost (1970s)** | Very high | Low |
| **Example Systems** | Tektronix 4014, DVST, early CAD | TV, LCD, modern PC monitor |
| **Modern Relevance** | Oscilloscopes, HUDs, niche CAD | Universal (smartphones, TVs) |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Display File Memory Footprint

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A random scan display file stores each line as: **opcode (1 byte) + X₁ (2 bytes) + Y₁ (2 bytes) + X₂ (2 bytes) + Y₂ (2 bytes) + intensity (1 byte)**. How much memory is required for a picture containing 800 line commands?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute bytes per line entry.**

$$\text{Bytes/entry} = 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 = 10\,\text{bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by the number of entries.**

$$M = N_{\text{lines}} \times \text{Bytes/entry} = 800 \times 10 = 8000\,\text{bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kilobytes.**

$$M = \frac{8000}{1024} \approx 7.81\,\text{KB}$$

$$\boxed{M \approx 7.81\,\text{KB of display file memory}}$$

This very modest memory requirement (vs. several MB for a raster frame buffer) is the key historical advantage of random scan systems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Random Scan Display System

> [!NOTE]
> Below is a Mermaid block-level functional architecture showing the data flow from the host application down to the CRT electron beam. Memorize the direction of arrows for full marks in KTU diagrams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Host Application Program] --> B[Display File in Refresh Memory]
    B --> C[Display Controller]
    C --> D[Vector Stroke Generator]
    C --> E[Character Generator]
    D --> F1[X DAC]
    D --> F2[Y DAC]
    E --> F1
    E --> F2
    F1 --> G1[X Deflection Plates or Coils]
    F2 --> G2[Y Deflection Plates or Coils]
    G1 --> H[CRT Electron Beam]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Phosphor Screen]
    C --> J[Beam Blanking / Z Axis Control]
    J --> H
    C --> K[Intensity DAC]
    K --> I
```

**Reading the Diagram (Exam-Ready Description):**
- The **Host** writes a list of display commands (MOVE, LINE, POLY, CHAR) into the **Display File Memory**.
- The **Display Controller** sequentially fetches these commands at the refresh rate.
- For LINE commands, control is passed to the **Vector Generator** which loads the endpoint coordinates into the **X-DAC** and **Y-DAC**.
- The analog outputs of the DACs drive the **X and Y Deflection Subsystems**, which steer the electron beam on the phosphor screen.
- The **Intensity DAC** and **Blanking (Z-axis) Control** manage the brightness and the "lift-the-chalk" beam cut-off.
- The **Character Generator** is invoked when a CHAR command is decoded, generating the appropriate stroke-font pattern.

---

## 4.2 Random Scan vs. Raster Scan — Beam Trajectory Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **high-yield KTU diagram** (worth up to 4 marks). Practice drawing it manually in the exam hall.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RandomVector["RANDOM SCAN - Beam Path"]
        R1[Start at P1] -->|Draw Line| R2[P2]
        R2 -->|Blank and Jump| R3[P3]
        R3 -->|Draw Line| R4[P4]
        R4 -->|Blank and Jump| R5[P5]
        R5 -->|Draw Line| R6[Back to P1 - Refresh Loop]
    end
    subgraph RasterScan["RASTER SCAN - Beam Path"]
        S1[Start at Top Left] -->|Draw Line| S2[Top Right]
        S2 -->|Flyback - Blank| S3[Next Line Start]
        S3 -->|Draw Line| S4[End of that Line]
        S4 -->|Flyback - Blank| S5[Continue to Bottom Right]
        S5 -->|Vertical Retrace| S6[Back to Top Left - Frame Loop]
    end
```

**Key Visual Takeaway:**
- In **Random Scan**, the arrows are *arbitrary* and the beam *jumps* between endpoints.
- In **Raster Scan**, the arrows follow a **strict zig-zag, top-to-bottom** sequence with regular horizontal flyback blanking.

---

## 4.3 Internal Anatomy of a Random Scan CRT

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Heated Cathode] -->|Emits electrons| B[Control Grid]
    B -->|Acceleration| C[Anode]
    C --> D[Focusing Anode]
    D --> E[X Deflection Plates]
    E --> F[Y Deflection Plates]
    F --> G[Phosphor Coated Screen]
    G --> H[Visible Light Spot]
    subgraph Yoke
        E
        F
    end
```

**KTU Exam Description (Recommended Wording):**
> "A heated cathode emits a stream of electrons via thermionic emission. The control grid regulates beam intensity (Z-axis). The anode accelerates and focuses the beam. Two pairs of mutually perpendicular deflection plates steer the beam on the X and Y axes. The beam strikes the phosphor coating, exciting it to emit visible light, forming the picture spot."

---

## 4.4 DVST (Direct-View Storage Tube) Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> DVST eliminates the need for refresh by using a **storage grid** behind the phosphor. The picture is "written" once and persists for hours.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Writing Gun - High Energy] -->|Floods Flood Guns| B[Storage Grid Mesh]
    B -->|Persistent Charge Pattern| C[Phosphor Screen]
    D[Flood Guns - Low Energy Continuous] -->|Read Floods| B
    E[Collector Mesh] -->|Recycles Electrons| D
```

**Working Principle:** The **writing gun** deposits a pattern of charges on the storage grid. The continuously firing **flood guns** spray low-energy electrons through the grid. Where the grid has stored positive charge, electrons pass through and excite the phosphor; where negative, they are repelled. The picture persists *indefinitely* (hours) until erased by a global reset pulse.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Random Scan Display. Why is it called a "vector" or "stroke-writing" display?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Random Scan Display** is a CRT-based display device in which the electron beam is directly steered (deflected) along the geometric paths of a picture's primitives, with the beam *blanked* (cut off) while moving between primitives. It is called a **"vector"** display because the picture is defined mathematically as a set of *vectors* (directed line segments) stored in a display file. It is called **"stroke-writing"** because the picture appears to be drawn by a pen making individual **strokes** on the screen, in contrast to the line-by-line painting of raster systems. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for vector naming, 1 for stroke-writing analogy.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List any three advantages and three disadvantages of Random Scan Displays.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **High resolution** — lines have mathematically smooth edges, not pixelated.
2. **Low memory requirement** — only the display file (command list) is stored, not full pixel array.
3. **Ideal for line drawings** — wireframe CAD/engineering diagrams are extremely efficient.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Flicker-prone** — for complex pictures with thousands of lines, the refresh cycle becomes too long.
2. **Limited color and shading** — cannot easily fill regions or render photorealistic images.
3. **High system cost historically** — required high-speed analog deflection hardware.

*(3 marks: 1 per pair of advantages/disadvantages, with full credit for three correct points each.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students answer **one full question** of 14 marks per module, with internal choice between two questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Architecture-Focused**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and working of a **Random Scan Display System**. Describe the role of the Display File, Vector Generator, Character Generator, and Display Controller in the refresh cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Random Scan** and **Raster Scan** display systems under the following heads: (i) Beam trajectory, (ii) Memory requirement, (iii) Picture quality, (iv) Suitability for shaded/realistic images, (v) Flicker behaviour, (vi) Cost, (vii) Example applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** A random scan system draws a picture by directing the electron beam to trace each line/curve in the image, refreshing the entire display 30–60 times per second from a stored display file.

2. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid architecture in Section 4.1. The key blocks are:
   - **Display File Memory** (e.g., 8 KB) — stores the command list.
   - **Display Controller** — fetches one command per cycle, decodes it.
   - **Vector Generator** — produces analog X-Y ramps for LINE commands.
   - **Character Generator** — converts ASCII to stroke-font strokes.
   - **X-DAC / Y-DAC** — convert digital coordinates to deflection voltages.
   - **X / Y Deflection Plates** — physically steer the beam.
   - **CRT** — displays the final image on phosphor.

3. **Working — The Refresh Cycle (3 marks):** Step-by-step:
   - Step 1: Display controller reads the first command from the display file.
   - Step 2: If it's a `MOVE` command → beam is positioned (blanked) at the new coordinates.
   - Step 3: If it's a `LINE` command → vector generator is activated, beam is unblanked, and electron beam traces the path from current position to the new endpoint.
   - Step 4: If it's a `CHAR` command → character generator is invoked, which decodes the ASCII code and issues a sequence of stroke sub-commands.
   - Step 5: Controller increments the instruction pointer; when end-of-list is reached, loops back to top, completing one refresh cycle.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

Refer to the comprehensive comparison table in Section 3.3. For full 7 marks, students must present the answer in a **tabular format with at least 7 comparison parameters**, with one mark each (1) and 0 marks deducted for missing table format. Recommended 7 axes:
- **Beam trajectory** (1 mark) — Random: arbitrary, vs. Raster: line-by-line scan.
- **Memory requirement** (1 mark) — KB vs. MB/GB.
- **Picture quality** (1 mark) — Smooth lines vs. pixelated.
- **Shaded images** (1 mark) — Difficult vs. Native support.
- **Flicker behaviour** (1 mark) — Yes, file-length dependent vs. No, fixed rate.
- **Cost** (1 mark) — Higher historically vs. Lower.
- **Applications** (1 mark) — CAD/oscilloscopes vs. TV/monitors.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Numerical & DVST-Focused**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A random scan display system refreshes at **50 Hz**. Each vector requires **20 μs** to draw, and the display file contains **1200 vectors**. Determine:
  (i) The total time taken to refresh the entire display. **(2 marks)**
  (ii) Whether the system will flicker. Justify. **(2 marks)**
  (iii) The maximum number of vectors that can be displayed without flicker. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of a **Direct-View Storage Tube (DVST)**. List its advantages and limitations over a refreshed random scan CRT. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**(i) Total refresh time (2 marks):**

$$T_{\text{total}} = N \times t_s = 1200 \times 20\,\mu s = 24{,}000\,\mu s = 24\,\text{ms}$$

**(ii) Flicker check (2 marks):**

The maximum allowed time between refreshes for 50 Hz is:

$$T_{\text{available}} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{50} = 20\,\text{ms}$$

Since $T_{\text{total}} = 24\,\text{ms} > T_{\text{available}} = 20\,\text{ms}$, the system **WILL FLICKER**. The refresh cycle is too long — by the time the controller finishes drawing the 1200th vector, the first vector's phosphor has already decayed significantly. **(2 marks: 1 for calculation, 1 for conclusion.)**

**(iii) Maximum vectors without flicker (3 marks):**

$$N_{\text{max}} = \frac{T_{\text{available}}}{t_s} = \frac{20\,\text{ms}}{20\,\mu s} = \frac{20{,}000}{20} = 1000 \text{ vectors}$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{max}} = 1000 \text{ vectors per refresh frame}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**1. Construction (2 marks):** A DVST consists of:
- A **writing gun** (high-velocity electron gun) for image generation.
- **Two flood guns** (low-velocity, continuous electron emitters) for image retention.
- A **storage grid/mesh** (fine wire grid) just behind the phosphor screen.
- A **phosphor screen** and **collector mesh** at the front.

**2. Working (3 marks):**
- The **writing gun** draws the picture on the storage grid by depositing a pattern of positive and negative charges corresponding to the picture.
- The **flood guns** continuously spray low-energy electrons across the entire screen.
- Where the storage grid has a **positive charge pattern** (the picture), electrons pass through to the phosphor, exciting it to glow.
- Where the grid has a **negative charge** (background), electrons are repelled and collected by the collector mesh.
- The image **persists** on the storage grid for hours without refresh.
- To erase, a brief **erase pulse** neutralises the entire grid.

**3. Advantages (1 mark):**
- No flicker, no refresh memory, no refresh controller.
- High resolution, smooth lines (not limited by refresh cycle time).

**4. Limitations (1 mark):**
- Cannot selectively erase parts — must erase and redraw the whole picture.
- Slow writing speed.
- Limited brightness/contrast range.
- No animation (since erasing is destructive of the entire image).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Random Scan Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing DVST with Raster:** DVST is *vector-based* with storage, not raster. **(-2 marks typical).**
> 2. **Forgetting to mention "blanking"** while describing beam movement between primitives. This is a key concept. **(-1 mark).**
> 3. **Omitting the Display File** in the architecture diagram. Many students draw only the CRT side and forget the memory/command-list side. **(-2 marks).**
> 4. **In numericals, mixing up ms and μs.** Always re-check the units before dividing. $24{,}000\,\mu s = 24\,\text{ms}$, not $24{,}000\,\text{ms}$.
> 5. **In comparison questions, writing in paragraph form** instead of tabular form. The KTU answer key explicitly awards 1 mark for proper table presentation.
> 6. **Stating "Random Scan has lower resolution"** — this is **FALSE**. Random scan has *higher* effective resolution. The correct phrasing is: *"Random scan has higher resolution for line drawings, but limited color/shading capability."*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Random Scan Displays & Systems (Module 1)**
>
> - ☐ **Definition:** Random scan = beam directly traces vectors; refreshed from a display file at 30–60 Hz.
> - ☐ **Synonyms:** Vector display, stroke-writing display, calligraphic display, line-drawing display.
> - ☐ **Alternative name for display storage:** "Display File" / "Display List" / "Refresh Buffer" (in some texts).
> - ☐ **Core Architecture (6 blocks):** Display File → Display Controller → Vector Generator + Character Generator → X-DAC + Y-DAC + Intensity DAC → Deflection System → CRT.
> - ☐ **Two Types:** Refreshed random scan (uses Display File) and DVST (uses storage grid — no refresh needed).
> - ☐ **DVST Advantages:** No flicker, no refresh, persistent image. **Disadvantages:** No selective erase, no animation, slow.
> - ☐ **Phosphor Persistence:** Must be ≤ 1/60 s to avoid flicker.
> - ☐ **Critical Numerical Formula:** $N_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{min}} \cdot t_s}$ — use this for all "maximum vectors" problems.
> - ☐ **DAC Step Size:** $\Delta = V_{\text{fs}} / (2^n - 1)$ — used in vector generation quality questions.
> - ☐ **Vector Generator Equation:** Linear ramp $V_x(t) = V_{x1} + (V_{x2} - V_{x1}) \cdot t/T$.
> - ☐ **Beam Blanking (Z-axis):** Beam is *cut off* during repositioning between primitives — essential to avoid stray marks.
> - ☐ **Random vs. Raster:** Random = line-drawing efficiency, no shading; Raster = shading, color, animation, pixel-based.
> - ☐ **Real-world systems:** Tektronix 4014, Tektronix oscilloscopes, Apollo/SGI IRIS CAD terminals, avionics HUDs.
> - ☐ **Common 2-Mark Definition:** "Random scan display is a vector display that directly draws the picture as a set of lines, refreshing from a display file, with the beam blanked during repositioning."
> - ☐ **Diagram Tip:** Always show *all six* blocks in the architecture. Always label arrows with data/signal types.
> - ☐ **Examiner's Pet Question:** "Compare Random Scan and Raster Scan" — appears every exam cycle, worth 7+ marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Raster Scan Displays and Systems. Line Drawing Algorithms - DDA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT416 - GRAPHICS AND MULTIMEDIA PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Raster Scan Displays and Systems. Line Drawing Algorithms - DDA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Raster Scan Displays — Formal Definition

A **Raster Scan Display** is a cathode-ray tube (CRT) or solid-state video display technology in which an image is composed of a matrix of discrete picture elements called **pixels** (short for *picture elements*) arranged in horizontal rows called *scan lines*. The electron beam (in CRTs) or pixel-driving circuitry (in LCD/LED panels) sweeps the entire screen systematically from top-to-bottom, left-to-right, refreshing each pixel according to the value stored in a dedicated memory area called the **Frame Buffer** (or **Refresh Buffer**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A *pixel* is the smallest addressable graphic element on a raster display. Each pixel has integer screen coordinates $(x, y)$ and an associated intensity or color value fetched from the frame buffer.

**Key System Components of a Raster Scan System:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Frame Buffer | Stores the intensity/color value of every pixel on the screen |
| Video Controller | Reads frame buffer data and generates synchronization signals |
| Display Processor | Offloads graphics operations (line draw, area fill) from the CPU |
| CRT/LCD Monitor | Visually reproduces the pixel matrix |
| Scan Conversion | The algorithmic process of converting geometric primitives into pixels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Image Quality Metric:** A full-HD raster display has a resolution of **1920 × 1080 = 2,073,600 pixels**, refreshed at **60 Hz** (or higher), requiring the video controller to push roughly **124 million pixels per second**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Raster Scan Like a Seating Chart

Imagine a large auditorium where the seats are arranged in a strict grid of rows and columns. Each seat can either be *lit* (occupied by a passenger) or *dark* (empty). A raster display is essentially this seating chart:
- The **rows** correspond to horizontal scan lines (Y-axis).
- The **columns** correspond to pixel positions on each line (X-axis).
- The **occupancy list** (which seat is filled and with what color) is the **frame buffer**.

To draw anything — a line, a circle, or a character — the graphics system must decide *exactly which seats to fill* and *in what color*. This is the essence of **Scan Conversion**.

### 1.3 Line Drawing Algorithms — Why Are They Needed?

In vector graphics, a line is defined mathematically as $\{(x, y) \mid y = mx + c\}$. But a raster screen only understands discrete pixel grids. The naive approach of computing $y$ as a real number and rounding it produces visible **staircase artifacts** known as **aliasing** or **jaggies**. **Line drawing algorithms** systematically determine the *optimal sequence of pixels* that best approximates a true straight line between two endpoints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Digital Differential Analyzer (DDA Algorithm):** A *sampling-based* line drawing algorithm that increments either $x$ or $y$ by unit steps and computes the other coordinate using the differential equation of a line: $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = m$ (slope).

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — DDA Like a Staircase Builder

Picture yourself walking from one corner of a rectangular garden to the diagonally opposite corner. The shortest path is a straight line, but the garden is paved with square tiles. DDA is like a person who decides: *"For every **one tile** I move forward horizontally, I must move up by exactly $m$ tiles vertically (or its rounded integer equivalent) to stay on the straight-line path."* This produces a clean staircase approximation of the line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> DDA is **incremental** (uses the result of the previous step to compute the next) and **floating-point-intensive**, which historically made it slower than integer-only methods like Bresenham's. However, DDA remains a foundational teaching algorithm in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the DDA Line Algorithm

The DDA algorithm exploits the differential form of the straight-line equation. Given two endpoints $P_1(x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2(x_2, y_2)$, the algorithm proceeds as follows:

**Step 1 — Compute the Geometric Differences (Lengths):**
- $\Delta x = x_2 - x_1$
- $\Delta y = y_2 - y_1$

**Step 2 — Identify the Dominant Axis (Stepping Parameter):**
- The number of interpolation steps is determined by the **larger** of $\vert \Delta x \vert$ and $\vert \Delta y \vert$, denoted as $steps = \max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)$.
- This ensures the line is sampled at least once per pixel column (or row), preventing gaps.

**Step 3 — Compute Per-Step Increments:**
- $X_{inc} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{steps}$
- $Y_{inc} = \dfrac{\Delta y}{steps}$

**Step 4 — Initialize and Iterate:**
- Start at $(x_1, y_1)$.
- Repeat $steps$ times: plot $(\text{round}(x), \text{round}(y))$, then $x \leftarrow x + X_{inc}$, $y \leftarrow y + Y_{inc}$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why use $\max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)$?** If we incremented only by $\Delta x = 1$, a steep line (large $\Delta y$) would have multiple $y$-pixels for a single $x$-pixel, possibly skipping rows and creating disconnected segments. Using the larger magnitude guarantees full coverage.
- **Why round to nearest integer?** Pixel coordinates are integers. The true line may pass *between* pixels; rounding places the closest pixel "on" the line.
- **Why floating-point accumulation?** Each iteration adds a fractional increment. Without rounding, $x$ and $y$ would drift as irrational numbers, eventually exceeding screen bounds.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slope of the line | $m = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$ | Dimensionless; undefined when $\Delta x = 0$ |
| 2 | Number of steps | $steps = \max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)$ | Positive integer; unitless |
| 3 | X-increment per step | $X_{inc} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{steps}$ | Pixels per step (real number) |
| 4 | Y-increment per step | $Y_{inc} = \dfrac{\Delta y}{steps}$ | Pixels per step (real number) |
| 5 | Pixel plot at iteration $k$ | $(x_k, y_k) = \left( \text{round}(x_1 + k \cdot X_{inc}),\ \text{round}(y_1 + k \cdot Y_{inc}) \right)$ | $k = 0, 1, \dots, steps$ |
| 6 | Straight-line equation | $y = m \cdot x + c$ | Where $c = y_1 - m \cdot x_1$ |
| 7 | Symmetry property | DDA handles $\vert m \vert \leq 1$ and $\vert m \vert > 1$ uniformly | The case $m = 0$ (horizontal) and $m = \infty$ (vertical) are also handled |
| 8 | Refresh rate requirement | $f_{refresh} \geq 30\ \text{Hz}$ to avoid flicker | Standard: **60 Hz** to **144 Hz** for modern displays |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Rule:** For lines with $\vert m \vert \leq 1$, DDA typically steps one pixel in $x$ per iteration. For $\vert m \vert > 1$, it steps one pixel in $y$ per iteration. The formula above handles both cases *automatically* because $steps$ is the larger magnitude.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

DDA is the conceptual grandparent of all modern hardware line rasterizers. Today:
- **GPU Rasterization Pipelines** in NVIDIA, AMD, and Intel GPUs use *parallelized, fixed-point* versions of incremental line/edge traversal logic descended from DDA.
- **Plotter and CNC Machine Controllers** use DDA-like interpolation to convert vector paths into coordinated multi-axis motion commands.
- **Embedded LCD Drivers** in medical and automotive displays use incremental DDA logic to draw UI elements (speedometer needles, waveform traces).

### 2.5 Advantages and Disadvantages of DDA

| Aspect | DDA Performance |
|---|---|
| Speed | Slower due to floating-point arithmetic and rounding operations per step |
| Accuracy | Good for most practical resolutions; subject to floating-point accumulation error |
| Simplicity | Very simple to implement and understand — ideal for academic instruction |
| Memory | Uses only a few registers; no lookup tables needed |
| Anti-aliasing | Out of the box, no anti-aliasing (jaggies remain) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the DDA Recurrence Relations

**Starting point:** The Cartesian line equation:

$$y = m \cdot x + c$$

**Differential form (the heart of DDA):** Taking the derivative with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = m$$

This differential equation tells us: *for an infinitesimally small change $dx$ in the horizontal direction, the vertical change is $dy = m \cdot dx$.*

**Discretization for raster display:** Since pixels are unit-spaced, we set $dx = 1$ pixel (or $dy = 1$, whichever is the dominant axis). Therefore:

$$dy = m \cdot dx = m \quad \text{(per unit step in } x\text{)}$$

**Generalization to fractional steps:** To handle slopes $m > 1$ or $m < -1$ without skipping pixels, we re-parameterize by the number of steps $N$:

$$X_{inc} = \frac{\Delta x}{N}, \quad Y_{inc} = \frac{\Delta y}{N}$$

**Recurrence relations** (the iterative engine of DDA):

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + X_{inc}$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k + Y_{inc}$$

**Pixel plot rule** at each iteration $k$:

$$P_k = \left( \text{round}(x_k),\ \text{round}(y_k) \right)$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Full KTU Board Style

> **Problem (KTU-style):** Apply the DDA algorithm to rasterize the line segment from $P_1(0, 0)$ to $P_2(6, 4)$. Plot all intermediate pixels.

**Step 1 — Compute the differences:**

$$\Delta x = x_2 - x_1 = 6 - 0 = 6$$

$$\Delta y = y_2 - y_1 = 4 - 0 = 4$$

**Step 2 — Determine the number of steps:**

$$steps = \max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert) = \max(6, 4) = 6$$

**Step 3 — Compute the per-step increments:**

$$X_{inc} = \frac{\Delta x}{steps} = \frac{6}{6} = 1.000$$

$$Y_{inc} = \frac{\Delta y}{steps} = \frac{4}{6} = 0.6667$$

**Step 4 — Initialize at $(x, y) = (0, 0)$ and iterate.**

We will build a 7-row analytical matrix (one row per iteration including the starting point). Each row shows: iteration index $k$, current real values of $(x, y)$, rounded pixel coordinates, and a graphical marker on the grid.

| $k$ | $x_k$ (real) | $y_k$ (real) | Plotted Pixel $\bigl(\text{round}(x_k), \text{round}(y_k)\bigr)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | (0, 0) |
| 1 | 1.0000 | 0.6667 | (1, 1) |
| 2 | 2.0000 | 1.3333 | (2, 1) |
| 3 | 3.0000 | 2.0000 | (3, 2) |
| 4 | 4.0000 | 2.6667 | (4, 3) |
| 5 | 5.0000 | 3.3333 | (5, 3) |
| 6 | 6.0000 | 4.0000 | (6, 4) |

**Visual pixel plot on a 7 × 5 grid** (X = plotted pixel, · = empty):

```
Y=4  ·  ·  ·  ·  ·  ·  X
Y=3  ·  ·  ·  ·  X  X  ·
Y=2  ·  ·  ·  X  ·  ·  ·
Y=1  ·  X  X  ·  ·  ·  ·
Y=0  X  ·  ·  ·  ·  ·  ·
     X=0 1 2 3 4 5 6
```

**Step 5 — Verification of end point:** At $k = 6$, the rounded coordinates $(6, 4)$ match the target endpoint $P_2$ exactly. **Algorithm converged successfully.**

### 3.3 Second Worked Example — Steep Line (Slope Greater Than 1)

> **Problem:** Apply DDA to draw a line from $A(2, 1)$ to $B(5, 9)$.

**Step 1 — Compute differences:**

$$\Delta x = 5 - 2 = 3, \quad \Delta y = 9 - 1 = 8$$

**Step 2 — Number of steps:**

$$steps = \max(3, 8) = 8$$

This is the **steep-line case**. The dominant axis is $y$, so $y$ will advance by exactly 1.0 per step, while $x$ will advance by a fraction.

**Step 3 — Per-step increments:**

$$X_{inc} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375, \quad Y_{inc} = \frac{8}{8} = 1.000$$

**Step 4 — Iterate from $(2, 1)$:**

| $k$ | $x_k$ | $y_k$ | Plotted Pixel |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 2.000 | 1.000 | (2, 1) |
| 1 | 2.375 | 2.000 | (2, 2) |
| 2 | 2.750 | 3.000 | (3, 3) |
| 3 | 3.125 | 4.000 | (3, 4) |
| 4 | 3.500 | 5.000 | (4, 5) |
| 5 | 3.875 | 6.000 | (4, 6) |
| 6 | 4.250 | 7.000 | (4, 7) |
| 7 | 4.625 | 8.000 | (5, 8) |
| 8 | 5.000 | 9.000 | (5, 9) |

**Observation:** Notice how $y$ advances by exactly **1.0 per step** (the dominant axis dominates cleanly), and $x$ advances by a fractional $0.375$, producing a smooth staircase.

### 3.4 Analytical Edge Case — Negative Slope

For lines with negative slope (descending from left to right), the same algorithm applies directly because $\Delta y < 0$ simply makes $Y_{inc}$ negative. No special branching is needed — a key strength of the unified DDA formulation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Raster Scan Display System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the high-level data flow inside a raster scan display system, mapping the journey of pixel data from CPU command to screen illumination.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpu[CPU] --> op[Graphics Commands]
    op --> dp[Display Processor]
    dp --> fb[Frame Buffer Memory]
    fb --> vc[Video Controller]
    vc --> sync[Sync Signal Generator]
    vc --> pix[Pixel Data Stream]
    sync --> crt[CRT or LCD Monitor]
    pix --> crt
    kb[Keyboard/Mouse Input] --> op
    crt --> eye[Human Eye Perception]
    fb -.refresh cycle.-> vc
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **CPU** issues high-level graphics commands (e.g., "draw line from A to B").
- The **Display Processor** interprets these commands and performs scan-conversion, writing pixel intensities into the **Frame Buffer**.
- The **Video Controller** continuously reads the frame buffer at the refresh rate (e.g., 60 Hz) and converts it to analog (CRT) or digital (LCD) signals for the monitor.
- The **dashed line** between the frame buffer and video controller represents the *constant refresh cycle* that prevents image flicker.

### 4.2 DDA Algorithm — Process Flowchart

The following flowchart shows the exact decision logic and computational flow of the DDA algorithm. Every node uses safe alphanumeric IDs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start DDA Algorithm]) --> in[Input Endpoints P1 x1 y1 and P2 x2 y2]
    in --> diff[Compute dx equals x2 minus x1 and dy equals y2 minus y1]
    diff --> steps[Compute steps equals max of absolute dx and absolute dy]
    steps --> incX[Compute Xinc equals dx divided by steps]
    incX --> incY[Compute Yinc equals dy divided by steps]
    incY --> init[Initialize x equals x1 and y equals y1]
    init --> loop{For k equals 0 to steps}
    loop --> plot[Plot pixel at round of x and round of y]
    plot --> upd[Update x equals x plus Xinc and y equals y plus Yinc]
    upd --> loop
    loop --> fin([End DDA Algorithm])
```

**Flowchart key steps (mapped to the derived formulas):**

| Node Label | Step Reference |
|---|---|
| `Compute dx and dy` | $\Delta x, \Delta y$ |
| `Compute steps` | $steps = \max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)$ |
| `Compute Xinc and Yinc` | $X_{inc}, Y_{inc}$ |
| `Plot pixel` | $(\text{round}(x), \text{round}(y))$ |
| `Update x and y` | $x_{k+1} = x_k + X_{inc}$ and $y_{k+1} = y_k + Y_{inc}$ |

### 4.3 Pixel Grid vs. True Line — Aliasing Visualization

The diagram below illustrates the **staircase aliasing effect** that DDA (and all integer-raster line algorithms) must mitigate or accept.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mathematical Line y equals 0.6667 x] --> B[Sampling at Integer X Values]
    B --> C[Discrete Pixel Approximation]
    C --> D((Visible Jaggies or Staircase))
    A -.continuous.-> B
    B -.discrete.-> C
    C -.aliasing artifact.-> D
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1]**
> **Q1. Differentiate between Raster Scan Display and Random Scan Display.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A *Raster Scan Display* renders images as a matrix of pixels stored in a frame buffer; the electron beam sweeps every scan line sequentially, refreshing the entire screen. It is well-suited for realistic shaded scenes but suffers from staircase aliasing.
