A *Random Scan Display* (vector display) draws images by directing the electron beam *only* along the lines to be displayed, with no frame buffer; it produces sharp, smooth lines but cannot realistically fill areas. Raster systems are dominant in modern displays due to low cost, whereas random scan was historically limited to specialized CAD/military use. *(3 marks — 1 for definition of each + 1 for contrast point)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2]**
> **Q2. State the formula for calculating the number of steps in the DDA algorithm and justify why this formula is used.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The number of steps is given by:

$$steps = \max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)$$

**Justification:** This formula uses the larger of the two absolute differences to ensure that the algorithm samples the line at *every pixel column* (for shallow slopes) or *every pixel row* (for steep slopes). If we naively set $steps = \vert \Delta x \vert$, a steep line with $m > 1$ would have multiple $y$-pixels mapping to a single $x$-column, causing the line to skip rows and produce disconnected segments. Using $\max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)$ guarantees continuous, gap-free pixel coverage along the line. *(3 marks — 1 for formula + 2 for justification)*

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of a Raster Scan Display System with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the frame buffer, video controller, and display processor. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Apply the DDA algorithm to rasterize the line from $P_1(2, 3)$ to $P_2(10, 8)$. Tabulate all intermediate pixels and sketch the plotted points on a grid. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Raster Scan Display Architecture *(7 marks)*

The raster scan display system consists of **five primary functional blocks** working in concert:

**1. CPU (Central Processing Unit):** Issues high-level graphics commands such as "draw line", "fill polygon", or "render bitmap". It does not directly control individual pixels. *(1 mark)*

**2. Display Processor (Graphics Controller):** A specialized co-processor that *interprets* graphics commands and performs scan-conversion (vector-to-raster conversion). It writes pixel values into the frame buffer. It offloads the CPU, enabling real-time interaction. *(1.5 marks)*

**3. Frame Buffer (Refresh Buffer):** A dedicated, high-speed memory area storing the intensity or color of every pixel on the screen. For a 1920 × 1080 display with 24-bit color, the frame buffer size is:

$$1920 \times 1080 \times 24\ \text{bits} \approx 6.2\ \text{MB}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**4. Video Controller (CRTC):** Reads the frame buffer sequentially (left-to-right, top-to-bottom) at the refresh rate (e.g., 60 Hz) and generates the **synchronization signals** (horizontal sync, vertical sync) and **video signal** required by the monitor. *(1.5 marks)*

**5. Monitor (CRT / LCD / LED):** The output device that physically renders the pixel matrix as a visible image. *(0.5 mark)*

**Block diagram (textual representation for exam):**

```
CPU  -->  Display Processor  -->  Frame Buffer  <--  Video Controller  -->  Monitor
                                            (refresh loop)
```

**Role Summary Table:**

| Block | Role |
|---|---|
| Frame Buffer | Stores intensity/color of every pixel |
| Video Controller | Reads frame buffer, generates sync signals |
| Display Processor | Performs scan conversion, writes frame buffer |

---

#### Part (b) — DDA Line Rasterization from (2, 3) to (10, 8) *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Compute the differences:**

$$\Delta x = 10 - 2 = 8$$

$$\Delta y = 8 - 3 = 5$$

**Step 2 — Number of steps:**

$$steps = \max(\vert 8 \vert, \vert 5 \vert) = \max(8, 5) = 8$$

**Step 3 — Per-step increments:**

$$X_{inc} = \frac{\Delta x}{steps} = \frac{8}{8} = 1.000$$

$$Y_{inc} = \frac{\Delta y}{steps} = \frac{5}{8} = 0.625$$

**Step 4 — Iterative table from starting point $(2, 3)$:**

| $k$ | $x_k$ (real) | $y_k$ (real) | Plotted Pixel |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 2.000 | 3.000 | (2, 3) |
| 1 | 3.000 | 3.625 | (3, 4) |
| 2 | 4.000 | 4.250 | (4, 4) |
| 3 | 5.000 | 4.875 | (5, 5) |
| 4 | 6.000 | 5.500 | (6, 6) |
| 5 | 7.000 | 6.125 | (7, 6) |
| 6 | 8.000 | 6.750 | (8, 7) |
| 7 | 9.000 | 7.375 | (9, 7) |
| 8 | 10.000 | 8.000 | (10, 8) |

**Step 5 — Pixel plot on a grid (X = pixel, · = empty):**

```
Y=8  · · · · · · · · · X
Y=7  · · · · · · · · X ·
Y=6  · · · · · · X X · ·
Y=5  · · · · · X · · · ·
Y=4  · · · X X · · · · ·
Y=3  · · X · · · · · · ·
     X=2 3 4 5 6 7 8 9 10
```

**Step 6 — Verification:** The endpoint $(10, 8)$ is reached exactly at $k = 8$, confirming successful rasterization. *(1 mark for the table; 1 mark for the grid sketch; 2 marks for the formulas; 2 marks for the iteration values; 1 mark for verification)*

---

> **Question B (Alternative 14-Mark Choice):**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Raster Scan Display System. Discuss the concept of refresh rate and its significance. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Using the DDA algorithm, plot the line segment from $A(1, 2)$ to $B(7, 10)$. List all plotted pixels in a table. *(7 marks)*

*(Students would produce a similar structured solution following the model above; the worked solution for B(b) yields $steps = 8$, $X_{inc} = 0.75$, $Y_{inc} = 1.00$.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to take the maximum of $\vert \Delta x \vert$ and $\vert \Delta y \vert$:** Many students set $steps = \vert \Delta x \vert$ blindly. For lines with $\vert m \vert > 1$, this causes *gaps* in the rasterized output, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Rounding errors in increments:** Writing $Y_{inc} = 0.67$ instead of the exact fraction $\dfrac{5}{8} = 0.625$. Always show the fraction form first, then the decimal.
> 3. **Off-by-one endpoint:** Failing to verify that the final pixel matches $(x_2, y_2)$. Examiners specifically check this.
> 4. **Confusing scan conversion with display technology:** Part (a) questions on architecture require block diagrams; missing the *display processor* or *frame buffer* block costs marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the rounding step:** Plotting the real-valued $(x_k, y_k)$ directly instead of $(\text{round}(x_k), \text{round}(y_k))$. The pixel grid is integer-based.
> 6. **Skipping the iteration table:** A complete tabular form is mandatory in 7-mark DDA problems. Hand-waving iterations lose all process marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Raster Scan and DDA**

- A **Raster Scan Display** renders images as a discrete matrix of **pixels** stored in a **frame buffer**.
- The **video controller** continuously reads the frame buffer at the **refresh rate** (typically **60 Hz** or higher) to prevent flicker.
- **DDA (Digital Differential Analyzer)** is a sampling-based line drawing algorithm derived from the differential equation $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = m$.
- The **number of steps** in DDA is $steps = \max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)$ — this is the most frequently tested formula.
- **Per-step increments** are $X_{inc} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{steps}$ and $Y_{inc} = \dfrac{\Delta y}{steps}$.
- DDA handles **all slopes uniformly**, including negative slopes, horizontal lines ($m = 0$), and vertical lines ($m = \infty$).
- The **plotting rule** is: at each iteration, plot $(\text{round}(x_k), \text{round}(y_k))$.
- DDA is **floating-point-intensive** and **slower** than integer-only algorithms like Bresenham's (covered in Module 1 continuation).
- DDA does **not perform anti-aliasing**; jaggies are inherent to its output.
- Modern GPUs and CNC controllers use **incremental interpolation** principles inherited from DDA.
- KTU 2024 Scheme expects: (1) neat architecture diagrams, (2) complete DDA iteration tables, (3) correct formulas, and (4) explicit verification of the endpoint.
- Always show the **fraction form** of increments before decimal conversion to earn full marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Reference within Module 1:** DDA is typically followed by the **Bresenham's Mid-Point Line Algorithm** and **Circle Drawing Algorithms** in subsequent KTU 2024 Scheme modules. Mastering DDA's increment logic is the foundation for all of them.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bresenham's Algorithm. Circle Drawing Algorithms - Midpoint Circle Generation Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT416 - GRAPHICS AND MULTIMEDIA PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bresenham's Algorithm. Circle Drawing Algorithms - Midpoint Circle Generation Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1: BRESENHAM'S & MIDPOINT CIRCLE ALGORITHMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Bresenham's Line Algorithm — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Bresenham's Line Algorithm (1962, Jack E. Bresenham)** is an *incremental*, *integer-only* rasterization algorithm used in computer graphics to determine which points on an integer 2D grid should be plotted to form a close approximation to a straight line between two given points. It is the de-facto industry-standard line drawing routine inside GPU rasterizers and OpenGL line primitives.

**Syllabus Highlight:** KTU expects students to understand the **decision parameter $p_k$**, the **midpoint test**, and the **incremental update rules** for both $\vert m \vert < 1$ and $\vert m \vert \geq 1$ cases.

### 1.2 Midpoint Circle Generation Algorithm — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Midpoint Circle Algorithm** is a rasterization method that determines the points needed to render a circle of radius $r$ centered at the origin by evaluating the **midpoint** between the candidate pixels $(x_k+1, y_k)$ and $(x_k+1, y_k-1)$ against the implicit circle equation $f_{\text{circle}}(x,y) = x^2 + y^2 - r^2 = 0$. It uses **8-way octant symmetry**, plotting only $\lceil r \cdot \sqrt{2}/2 \rceil$ points in the second octant and reflecting them.

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy 🌍

#### 🎨 The "Staircase Painter" Analogy (Bresenham's)
Imagine you are painting a slanted staircase on a tiled floor using only whole tiles. Between two tiles, you must decide whether to place a horizontal tile (E) or a diagonal tile (NE) based on which is **closer to the true mathematical line**. Bresenham's algorithm is that decision rule — it tells the painter which tile makes the staircase hug the true line most closely, using only the **previous error** to make the next call (no floating-point math!).

#### 🎯 The "Bullseye Midpoint Test" (Midpoint Circle)
Picture an archer drawing a circular target. To find the next ink dot, they don't measure exact distance to the circumference; they simply check the **middle point** between two candidate dots — if the middle lies *inside* the target, they pick the lower dot; if *outside*, they pick the upper dot. This binary "is it inside or outside?" test is the **decision parameter** $p_k$.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $r$ | Circle radius | Integer pixels |
| $\Delta x, \Delta y$ | Run and Rise of line | Integer |
| $p_k$ | Decision parameter | Integer |
| $N_o$ | Pixels per octant | $\lceil r/\sqrt{2} \rceil$ |

> [!NOTE]
> All decision parameters in both algorithms are **pure integers** (or sometimes 0.25-integers if starting with the $5/4 - r$ form). This is the central advantage: **no floating-point hardware required**, making these algorithms blazing fast on legacy graphics hardware and embedded displays.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bresenham's Line Algorithm — Operational Logic

The algorithm rests on three foundational ideas:

1. **Discrete Sampling:** Since a pixel grid is discrete, a straight line must be approximated by a sequence of integer-coordinate pixels.
2. **Incremental Computation:** The decision for pixel $k+1$ is derived from the decision for pixel $k$ using **addition only** (no multiplication per step).
3. **Midpoint Test:** At each step, the algorithm evaluates the **midpoint** between the two candidate next pixels against the true line $y = mx + b$.

#### Operational Steps (for $\vert m \vert \leq 1$, line from $(x_0, y_0)$ to $(x_1, y_1)$):

- **Step 1:** Input the two line endpoints. Store the left endpoint as $(x_0, y_0)$.
- **Step 2:** Plot the first point: $\text{putPixel}(x_0, y_0)$.
- **Step 3:** Compute the constants:
  $\Delta x = x_1 - x_0$, $\Delta y = y_1 - y_0$, 
  $p_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x$.
- **Step 4:** At each $x_k$ starting from $k=0$, test the sign of $p_k$:
  - If $p_k \leq 0$: the next point is $(x_k+1, y_k)$ and $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\Delta y$.
  - If $p_k > 0$: the next point is $(x_k+1, y_k+1)$ and $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$.
- **Step 5:** Repeat Step 4 $\Delta x$ times (i.e., for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, \Delta x - 1$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "Why" behind $p_k$ comes from comparing the **midpoint** $M = (x_k+1, y_k+0.5)$ against the true line. If $M$ is below the line → take E; if above → take NE.

### 2.2 Midpoint Circle Algorithm — Operational Logic

The algorithm exploits the **8-fold symmetry** of a circle about its center. A circle has 4 quadrants $\times$ 2 axes $= 8$ symmetric octants. By computing pixels in **Octant 2** (where $y$ decreases and $x$ increases from $(0, r)$ to $(r/\sqrt{2}, r/\sqrt{2})$), the other 7 octants are obtained by simple **reflection / sign-flip** operations.

#### Operational Steps:

- **Step 1:** Input radius $r$ and center $(x_c, y_c)$.
- **Step 2:** Initialize: $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = r$, $p_0 = 1 - r$.
- **Step 3:** Plot the initial point and its 7 symmetric counterparts.
- **Step 4:** At each $k \geq 0$, test $p_k$:
  - If $p_k < 0$: next point is $(x_k+1, y_k)$ and $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2x_k + 3$.
  - If $p_k \geq 0$: next point is $(x_k+1, y_k-1)$ and $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2(x_k - y_k) + 5$.
- **Step 5:** Repeat Step 4 until $x_k \geq y_k$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it appears verbatim in KTU ESE questions every year.**

| Algorithm | Decision Parameter Initial Value | Update (Case A) | Update (Case B) | Stopping Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bresenham (slope $\leq 1$) | $p_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x$ | $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\Delta y$ (when $p_k \leq 0$) | $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ (when $p_k > 0$) | $k = \Delta x$ |
| Bresenham (slope $> 1$) | $p_0 = 2\Delta x - \Delta y$ | $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\Delta x$ (when $p_k \leq 0$) | $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\Delta x - 2\Delta y$ (when $p_k > 0$) | $k = \Delta y$ |
| Midpoint Circle | $p_0 = 1 - r$ | $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2x_k + 3$ (when $p_k < 0$) | $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2(x_k - y_k) + 5$ (when $p_k \geq 0$) | $x_k \geq y_k$ |

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Physical Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Line slope | $m = \Delta y / \Delta x$ | Steepness of line per unit x |
| Implicit circle | $f(x,y) = x^2 + y^2 - r^2$ | $< 0$ inside, $= 0$ on, $> 0$ outside |
| Midpoint test (line) | $p_k = 2\Delta y \cdot x_{k+1} - 2\Delta x \cdot y_{k+1} + c$ | Sign indicates which side of line |
| Midpoint test (circle) | $p_k = (x_k+1)^2 + (y_k - 0.5)^2 - r^2$ | Sign indicates inside/outside circle |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GPU Rasterizers (NVIDIA, AMD, ARM Mali):** Modern shader cores use Bresenham's incremental logic (in fixed-point form) for line, triangle-edge, and circle rasterization.
- **CNC Plotter Machines:** Convert vector drawings to stepper-motor pulses using the same $p_k$ logic.
- **OLED Display Drivers:** Embedded 1-pixel-per-frame renderers (e.g., ESP32 + SSD1306) use the Midpoint Circle Algorithm for drawing GUI gauges.
- **Medical Imaging:** Pixel-perfect circles for ROI selection in CT/MRI viewers.
- **Video Games:** Minimap markers, range rings, and radar sweeps all use Midpoint Circle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Bresenham's Decision Parameter

#### Starting Point
Consider a line from $(x_0, y_0)$ to $(x_1, y_1)$ with slope $m = \Delta y / \Delta x$ where $0 \leq m \leq 1$.

**Implicit line equation:** $y = m \cdot x + b$, equivalently:
$$F(x, y) = y - mx - b = 0$$

#### Midpoint Test
Assume pixel $(x_k, y_k)$ has been chosen. The two candidate next pixels are:
- **E (East):** $(x_k + 1, y_k)$
- **NE (North-East):** $(x_k + 1, y_k + 1)$

The midpoint between them is:
$$M = (x_k + 1, \ y_k + 0.5)$$

Substituting $M$ into $F(x,y)$:
$$p_k = F(x_k + 1, \ y_k + 0.5) = (y_k + 0.5) - m(x_k + 1) - b$$

#### Convert to Integer Arithmetic
Multiply by $2\Delta x$ (since $m = \Delta y / \Delta x$):
$$p_k = 2\Delta x \cdot y_k + \Delta x - 2\Delta y \cdot x_k - 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x \cdot b$$

Let $c = 2\Delta x \cdot b$ and the constant difference $\Delta c = 2\Delta x \cdot b$:
$$p_k = 2\Delta y \cdot x_{k+1} - 2\Delta x \cdot y_{k+1} + \text{const}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For incremental update we only need the **difference** $p_{k+1} - p_k$, which eliminates the constant.

#### Incremental Update Derivation

**Case A:** When $p_k \leq 0$, we choose E, so $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1}) = (x_k + 1, y_k)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{k+1} &= 2\Delta y (x_{k+1} + 1) - 2\Delta x \cdot y_{k+1} + \text{const} \\
&= 2\Delta y (x_k + 2) - 2\Delta x \cdot y_k + \text{const} \\
p_k &= 2\Delta y (x_k + 1) - 2\Delta x \cdot y_k + \text{const} \\
\therefore \ p_{k+1} - p_k &= 2\Delta y \\
\therefore \ p_{k+1} &= p_k + 2\Delta y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case B:** When $p_k > 0$, we choose NE, so $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1}) = (x_k + 1, y_k + 1)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{k+1} &= 2\Delta y (x_k + 2) - 2\Delta x (y_k + 1) + \text{const} \\
p_k &= 2\Delta y (x_k + 1) - 2\Delta x \cdot y_k + \text{const} \\
\therefore \ p_{k+1} - p_k &= 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x \\
\therefore \ p_{k+1} &= p_k + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Initial value derivation** (substitute $k=0$ at start, evaluating $F(x_0+1, y_0+0.5)$):
$$p_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x$$

✅ **Derivation complete.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Midpoint Circle Decision Parameter

#### Starting Point
The implicit circle equation centered at origin is:
$$f_{\text{circle}}(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 - r^2 = 0$$

- $f < 0 \Rightarrow$ point is **inside** the circle
- $f = 0 \Rightarrow$ point is **on** the circle
- $f > 0 \Rightarrow$ point is **outside** the circle

#### Midpoint Test
Assume $(x_k, y_k)$ is the last plotted pixel (starting at $(0, r)$). Candidate next pixels are:
- **S (South):** $(x_k + 1, y_k)$ — moves to the right
- **SE (South-East):** $(x_k + 1, y_k - 1)$ — moves down-right

The midpoint between S and SE is:
$$M = (x_k + 1, \ y_k - 0.5)$$

The decision parameter is the evaluation of $f$ at $M$:
$$p_k = f_{\text{circle}}(x_k + 1, \ y_k - 0.5)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_k &= (x_k + 1)^2 + (y_k - 0.5)^2 - r^2 \\
&= x_k^2 + 2x_k + 1 + y_k^2 - y_k + 0.25 - r^2
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Incremental Update Derivation

**Case A:** When $p_k < 0$, midpoint is inside circle, so we pick **S** → $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1}) = (x_k+1, y_k)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{k+1} &= (x_k + 2)^2 + (y_k - 0.5)^2 - r^2 \\
p_k &= (x_k + 1)^2 + (y_k - 0.5)^2 - r^2 \\
p_{k+1} - p_k &= (x_k + 2)^2 - (x_k + 1)^2 \\
&= x_k^2 + 4x_k + 4 - x_k^2 - 2x_k - 1 \\
&= 2x_k + 3 \\
\therefore \ p_{k+1} &= p_k + 2x_k + 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case B:** When $p_k \geq 0$, midpoint is on or outside circle, so we pick **SE** → $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1}) = (x_k+1, y_k-1)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{k+1} &= (x_k + 2)^2 + (y_k - 1.5)^2 - r^2 \\
p_k &= (x_k + 1)^2 + (y_k - 0.5)^2 - r^2 \\
p_{k+1} - p_k &= (x_k + 2)^2 - (x_k + 1)^2 + (y_k - 1.5)^2 - (y_k - 0.5)^2 \\
&= 2x_k + 3 + (y_k^2 - 3y_k + 2.25) - (y_k^2 - y_k + 0.25) \\
&= 2x_k + 3 - 2y_k + 2 \\
\therefore \ p_{k+1} &= p_k + 2x_k - 2y_k + 5 = p_k + 2(x_k - y_k) + 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Initial value:** substitute $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = r$:
$$p_0 = 0^2 + 2(0) + 1 + r^2 - r + 0.25 - r^2 = 1.25 - r$$

Since $r$ is an integer, $p_0$ may be non-integer. To make it integer, define:
$$p_0 = 1 - r$$
(which is a 1/4 scaling of the original — the sign of $p_k$ is preserved, so it yields identical decisions.)

✅ **Derivation complete.**

---

### 3.3 Worked Example #1 — Bresenham's Line Algorithm

> **Problem:** Apply Bresenham's algorithm to draw a line from $(0, 0)$ to $(5, 4)$.

**Given:** $\Delta x = 5$, $\Delta y = 4$, $m = 4/5 = 0.8 \leq 1$ ✓ (use Case A/B standard form)

**Initial parameter:**
$$p_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x = 2(4) - 5 = 3$$

#### Trace Table

| Step $k$ | $p_k$ | Sign Test | Next Pixel $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1})$ | Update Rule | $p_{k+1}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 3 | $p_0 > 0$ | $(1, 1)$ | $p + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ | $3 + 8 - 10 = 1$ |
| 1 | 1 | $p_1 > 0$ | $(2, 2)$ | $p + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ | $1 + 8 - 10 = -1$ |
| 2 | $-1$ | $p_2 \leq 0$ | $(3, 2)$ | $p + 2\Delta y$ | $-1 + 8 = 7$ |
| 3 | 7 | $p_3 > 0$ | $(4, 3)$ | $p + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ | $7 + 8 - 10 = 5$ |
| 4 | 5 | $p_4 > 0$ | $(5, 4)$ | $p + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ | $5 + 8 - 10 = 3$ |

**Pixels plotted (in order):** $(0,0), (1,1), (2,2), (3,2), (4,3), (5,4)$.

✅ **Verified:** endpoints match input, all pixels lie on or within 0.5 px of true line $y = 0.8x$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example #2 — Midpoint Circle Algorithm

> **Problem:** Apply the Midpoint Circle Algorithm for $r = 10$ centered at origin.

**Given:** $r = 10$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 10$, $p_0 = 1 - r = 1 - 10 = -9$.

#### Trace Table (Octant 2 only)

| Step $k$ | $p_k$ | Sign Test | $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1})$ | Update Rule | $p_{k+1}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | $-9$ | $p < 0$ | $(1, 10)$ | $p + 2(0) + 3$ | $-9 + 3 = -6$ |
| 1 | $-6$ | $p < 0$ | $(2, 10)$ | $p + 2(1) + 3$ | $-6 + 5 = -1$ |
| 2 | $-1$ | $p < 0$ | $(3, 10)$ | $p + 2(2) + 3$ | $-1 + 7 = 6$ |
| 3 | 6 | $p \geq 0$ | $(4, 9)$ | $p + 2(3 - 10) + 5$ | $6 - 14 + 5 = -3$ |
| 4 | $-3$ | $p < 0$ | $(5, 9)$ | $p + 2(4) + 3$ | $-3 + 11 = 8$ |
| 5 | 8 | $p \geq 0$ | $(6, 8)$ | $p + 2(5 - 9) + 5$ | $8 - 8 + 5 = 5$ |
| 6 | 5 | $p \geq 0$ | $(7, 7)$ | $p + 2(6 - 8) + 5$ | $5 - 4 + 5 = 6$ |

**STOP** because $x_k = 7 \geq y_k = 7$. Loop terminates.

#### Octant-2 Pixels Generated:
$(0, 10), (1, 10), (2, 10), (3, 10), (4, 9), (5, 9), (6, 8), (7, 7)$.

#### Full-Circle Pixels (8-way symmetry about origin):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Plot } (x, y) \text{ and reflections: } \\
&(y, x),\ (-x, y),\ (-y, x),\ (x, -y),\ (y, -x),\ (-x, -y),\ (-y, -x)
\end{aligned}
$$

| Quadrant Pair | Pixels |
| :--- | :--- |
| +X, +Y | $(0, 10), (1, 10), (2, 10), (3, 10), (4, 9), (5, 9), (6, 8), (7, 7)$ |
| +Y, +X (swap) | $(10, 0), (10, 1), (10, 2), (10, 3), (9, 4), (9, 5), (8, 6), (7, 7)$ |
| $-X, +Y$ | $(0, 10), (-1, 10), (-2, 10), (-3, 10), (-4, 9), (-5, 9), (-6, 8), (-7, 7)$ |
| $-Y, +X$ | $(10, 0), (10, -1), (10, -2), (10, -3), (9, -4), (9, -5), (8, -6), (7, -7)$ |
| $+X, -Y$ (mirror Y) | $(0, -10), (1, -10), (2, -10), (3, -10), (4, -9), (5, -9), (6, -8), (7, -7)$ |
| $-X, -Y$ (mirror both) | $(0, -10), (-1, -10), (-2, -10), (-3, -10), (-4, -9), (-5, -9), (-6, -8), (-7, -7)$ |

**Total unique pixels:** 8 octant reflections $\times$ 7 distinct points ≈ 56 points (some on axes overlap).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Bresenham vs. Midpoint Circle

| Feature | Bresenham's Line | Midpoint Circle |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Domain | Lines $\in \mathbb{Z}^2$ | Circles $\in \mathbb{Z}^2$ |
| Equation | $y = mx + b$ (linear) | $x^2 + y^2 = r^2$ (quadratic) |
| Symmetry exploited | None (line is unique) | **8-way** (octant) |
| Pixels per iteration | 1 pixel per step | 1 pixel per step |
| Time complexity | $O(\Delta x)$ or $O(\Delta y)$ | $O(r)$ |
| Initial param | $p_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x$ | $p_0 = 1 - r$ |
| Multiplications/step | **Zero** (integer add/sub) | **Zero** (integer add/sub) |
| Memory | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Year invented | 1962 (Bresenham) | 1977 (Bresenham, Pitteway) |
| Use case | CAD lines, plotter pens | Radar rings, GUI gauges |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bresenham's Line Algorithm — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start]) --> inpA[Read endpoints x0 y0 x1 y1]
    inpA --> calcA[Compute deltax x1 minus x0 and deltay y1 minus y0]
    calcA --> pcalcA[Set pk equals 2 times deltay minus deltax]
    pcalcA --> plotA[Plot pixel at x0 y0]
    plotA --> loopchkA{deltay less than deltax}
    loopchkA -- YES Major X --> loopA1[For k equals 0 to deltax minus 1 iterate]
    loopchkA -- NO Major Y --> loopA2[Swap roles of x and y and use major Y form]
    loopA1 --> testA{pk less than or equal to zero}
    testA -- YES E pixel --> updA1[pk plus 1 equals pk plus 2 times deltay]
    testA -- NO NE pixel --> updA2[pk plus 1 equals pk plus 2 times deltay minus 2 times deltax]
    updA1 --> plotnextA[Plot next pixel East or North East]
    updA2 --> plotnextA
    plotnextA --> endloopA{All steps done}
    endloopA -- NO --> loopA1
    endloopA -- YES --> stopA([Stop])
    loopA2 --> stopA
```

### 4.2 Midpoint Circle Algorithm — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start]) --> inpB[Read radius r and center xc yc]
    inpB --> initB[Set x equals 0 and y equals r]
    initB --> pcalcB[Set p equals 1 minus r]
    pcalcB --> plot8[Plot 8 symmetric points about center]
    plot8 --> testB{p less than zero}
    testB -- YES Pick South pixel --> updB1[x plus 1 and y unchanged and pk plus 1 equals pk plus 2 times xk plus 3]
    testB -- NO Pick South East pixel --> updB2[x plus 1 and y minus 1 and pk plus 1 equals pk plus 2 times xk minus yk plus 5]
    updB1 --> plotnextB[Plot 8 symmetric points for new x y]
    updB2 --> plotnextB
    plotnextB --> stopchkB{x greater than or equal to y}
    stopchkB -- NO --> testB
    stopchkB -- YES --> stopB([Stop])
```

### 4.3 Octant Symmetry Map (8-Way Reflection)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    o1["Octant 1  X plus Y plus"] --- o2["Octant 2  X plus Y minus"]
    o2 --- o3["Octant 3  X minus Y plus"]
    o3 --- o4["Octant 4  X minus Y minus"]
    o4 --- o5["Octant 5  Mirror Y axis"]
    o5 --- o6["Octant 6  Swap XY"]
    o6 --- o7["Octant 7  Mirror X axis"]
    o7 --- o8["Octant 8  Both mirrored"]
    o8 --- o1
    note["Compute pixels in Octant 2 only and reflect to remaining 7 octants via sign flip and swap operations"]
    o2 -.reflects to.-> note
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow — Pixel Selection Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s0["Current pixel Xk Yk"] --> c1["Candidate East Xk+1 Yk"]
    s0 --> c2["Candidate North East Xk+1 Yk+1"]
    c1 --> m["Midpoint M Xk+1 Yk plus 0.5"]
    c2 --> m
    m --> dt{pk sign}
    dt -- pk less than or equal 0 --> east["Choose East pixel"]
    dt -- pk greater than 0 --> ne["Choose North East pixel"]
    east --> nx["Increment xk only"]
    ne --> nxy["Increment both xk and yk"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** *Explain the role of the decision parameter $p_k$ in Bresenham's line drawing algorithm. How is it initialized and updated?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The decision parameter $p_k$ in Bresenham's line algorithm is an **integer-valued accumulator** that determines whether the next pixel to be plotted lies at the **East (E)** position $(x_k+1, y_k)$ or the **North-East (NE)** position $(x_k+1, y_k+1)$ when $\vert m \vert \leq 1$.

- **Initialization:** $p_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x$
- **If $p_k \leq 0$:** The midpoint lies below the true line; choose E and update $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\Delta y$.
- **If $p_k > 0$:** The midpoint lies above the true line; choose NE and update $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$.

The parameter $p_k$ removes the need for floating-point division by $m$ at every step, making the algorithm suitable for integer-only hardware.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** *What is 8-way symmetry in the Midpoint Circle Algorithm? Why is it important?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A circle is symmetric about **both the x-axis and y-axis**, as well as the lines $y = x$ and $y = -x$. This gives **8 octants** of identical pixel patterns. The Midpoint Circle Algorithm exploits this by computing pixels in **only the second octant** (where $x$ increases from $0$ to $r/\sqrt{2}$ and $y$ decreases from $r$ to $r/\sqrt{2}$). The remaining 7 octants are obtained by **simple sign-flips and coordinate-swaps**, with no extra computation. This reduces the algorithm's time complexity by a factor of $\approx 8$ and is the key reason the algorithm is preferred over a naive scanline or trigonometric rasterizer.

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Part B sub-parts, students commonly lose 2–3 marks by **(a)** forgetting to plot the **8 symmetric reflections** in circle problems (only Octant 2 is computed), **(b)** mixing up the **sign test** ($p_k \leq 0$ vs $< 0$) between Bresenham and Midpoint Circle, and **(c)** omitting the **initial plotting of the starting pixel** before entering the iterative loop. Always show the **full trace table** with $x_k$, $y_k$, and $p_k$ columns — examiners award partial marks generously when the table is correctly structured even if arithmetic slips occur.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive the decision parameter $p_k$ for Bresenham's line drawing algorithm, showing both cases of incremental update. State the condition under which this form is applicable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply Bresenham's line drawing algorithm to rasterize a line from $(2, 3)$ to $(9, 8)$. Plot all intermediate pixels using a trace table. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

> **Condition:** Applicable when $0 \leq m \leq 1$ (i.e., $\vert m \vert \leq 1$ for general form).

The implicit line equation is $F(x, y) = y - mx - b = 0$.

**Step 1:** Substitute midpoint $M = (x_k+1, y_k+0.5)$:
$$p_k = F(M) = y_k + 0.5 - m(x_k+1) - b$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by $2\Delta x$ to remove the fraction and use $m = \Delta y / \Delta x$:
$$p_k = 2\Delta y \cdot x_k - 2\Delta x \cdot y_k + 2\Delta y + 2\Delta x \cdot b'$$
(where $b' = 1 - b$ absorbs the constant). For incremental update, only the difference matters.

**Step 3 — Case A ($p_k \leq 0$, choose E):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{k+1} - p_k &= 2\Delta y \cdot (x_{k+1} - x_k) - 2\Delta x \cdot (y_{k+1} - y_k) \\
&= 2\Delta y (1) - 2\Delta x (0) \\
&= 2\Delta y \\
\therefore \ p_{k+1} &= p_k + 2\Delta y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Case B ($p_k > 0$, choose NE):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{k+1} - p_k &= 2\Delta y (1) - 2\Delta x (1) \\
&= 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x \\
\therefore \ p_{k+1} &= p_k + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Initial value:** $p_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x$.

✅ **(7 marks) Derivation complete.**

##### Model Solution (b) — Trace Table (7 marks)

**Given:** $(x_0, y_0) = (2, 3)$, $(x_1, y_1) = (9, 8)$.
$\Delta x = 7$, $\Delta y = 5$, $m = 5/7 \approx 0.714 \leq 1$ ✓.
$p_0 = 2(5) - 7 = 3$.

| $k$ | $p_k$ | Sign | Next Pixel | Update Rule | $p_{k+1}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 3 | $> 0$ (NE) | $(3, 4)$ | $+ 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ | $3 + 10 - 14 = -1$ |
| 1 | $-1$ | $\leq 0$ (E) | $(4, 4)$ | $+ 2\Delta y$ | $-1 + 10 = 9$ |
| 2 | 9 | $> 0$ (NE) | $(5, 5)$ | $+ 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ | $9 + 10 - 14 = 5$ |
| 3 | 5 | $> 0$ (NE) | $(6, 6)$ | $+ 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ | $5 + 10 - 14 = 1$ |
| 4 | 1 | $> 0$ (NE) | $(7, 7)$ | $+ 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ | $1 + 10 - 14 = -3$ |
| 5 | $-3$ | $\leq 0$ (E) | $(8, 7)$ | $+ 2\Delta y$ | $-3 + 10 = 7$ |
| 6 | 7 | $> 0$ (NE) | $(9, 8)$ | $+ 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ | $7 + 10 - 14 = 3$ |

**Pixels plotted:** $(2,3), (3,4), (4,4), (5,5), (6,6), (7,7), (8,7), (9,8)$ — 8 pixels.

✅ **(7 marks) Verified.** Final pixel $(9,8)$ matches input endpoint.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`**

**(a)** Derive the decision parameter $p_k$ and incremental update rules for the Midpoint Circle Algorithm, starting from the implicit circle equation. Show the 8-way symmetry reflections. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the Midpoint Circle Algorithm to plot a circle of radius $r = 8$ centered at the origin. Show the full trace table for Octant 2 and plot all 8-way symmetric pixels. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Implicit circle:** $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 - r^2 = 0$.

**Step 1:** Initialize: $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = r$, $p_0 = 1 - r$.

**Step 2:** Midpoint test at $M = (x_k+1, y_k - 0.5)$:
$$p_k = (x_k+1)^2 + (y_k - 0.5)^2 - r^2$$

**Step 3 — Case A ($p_k < 0$, pick S):** $y_{k+1} = y_k$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{k+1} - p_k &= (x_k + 2)^2 - (x_k + 1)^2 \\
&= (x_k^2 + 4x_k + 4) - (x_k^2 + 2x_k + 1) \\
&= 2x_k + 3 \\
\therefore \ p_{k+1} &= p_k + 2x_k + 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Case B ($p_k \geq 0$, pick SE):** $y_{k+1} = y_k - 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{k+1} - p_k &= (x_k + 2)^2 - (x_k + 1)^2 + (y_k - 1.5)^2 - (y_k - 0.5)^2 \\
&= 2x_k + 3 + (-2y_k + 2) \\
&= 2x_k - 2y_k + 5 = 2(x_k - y_k) + 5 \\
\therefore \ p_{k+1} &= p_k + 2(x_k - y_k) + 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — 8-Way Symmetry Reflections:**

For every computed pixel $(x, y)$ in Octant 2, plot the 7 reflections (relative to center $(x_c, y_c)$):

| Octant | Reflection |
| :---: | :--- |
| 1 | $(y, x)$ |
| 3 | $(-x, y)$ |
| 4 | $(-y, x)$ |
| 5 | $(x, -y)$ |
| 6 | $(y, -x)$ |
| 7 | $(-x, -y)$ |
| 8 | $(-y, -x)$ |

(Plus the original $(x, y)$ itself.)

✅ **(7 marks) Derivation complete.**

##### Model Solution (b) — Trace Table (7 marks)

**Given:** $r = 8$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 8$, $p_0 = 1 - 8 = -7$.

| $k$ | $p_k$ | Sign | $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1})$ | Update | $p_{k+1}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | $-7$ | $< 0$ (S) | $(1, 8)$ | $p + 2(0) + 3$ | $-7 + 3 = -4$ |
| 1 | $-4$ | $< 0$ (S) | $(2, 8)$ | $p + 2(1) + 3$ | $-4 + 5 = 1$ |
| 2 | 1 | $\geq 0$ (SE) | $(3, 7)$ | $p + 2(2-8) + 5$ | $1 - 12 + 5 = -6$ |
| 3 | $-6$ | $< 0$ (S) | $(4, 7)$ | $p + 2(3) + 3$ | $-6 + 9 = 3$ |
| 4 | 3 | $\geq 0$ (SE) | $(5, 6)$ | $p + 2(4-7) + 5$ | $3 - 6 + 5 = 2$ |
| 5 | 2 | $\geq 0$ (SE) | $(6, 5)$ | $p + 2(5-6) + 5$ | $2 - 2 + 5 = 5$ |

**STOP:** $x_5 = 6 \geq y_5 = 5$. Loop terminates.

**Octant 2 Pixels:** $(0,8), (1,8), (2,8), (3,7), (4,7), (5,6), (6,5)$.

**8-Way Symmetric Pixels (full circle, centered at origin):**

| Region | Pixels |
| :--- | :--- |
| Original | $(0,8), (1,8), (2,8), (3,7), (4,7), (5,6), (6,5)$ |
| Swap XY | $(8,0), (8,1), (8,2), (7,3), (7,4), (6,5), (5,6)$ |
| Mirror Y | $(0,-8), (1,-8), (2,-8), (3,-7), (4,-7), (5,-6), (6,-5)$ |
| Mirror X | $(-8,0), (-8,1), (-8,2), (-7,3), (-7,4), (-6,5), (-5,6)$ |
| Both mirrored | $(-8,0), (-8,-1), (-8,-2), (-7,-3), (-7,-4), (-6,-5), (-5,-6)$ |
| Y-swap + mirror Y | $(8,0), (8,-1), (8,-2), (7,-3), (7,-4), (6,-5), (5,-6)$ |
| Y-swap + mirror X | $(-8,0), (-8,1), (-8,2), (-7,3), (-7,4), (-6,5), (-5,6)$ |
| All 4 + swap | $(-8,0), (-8,-1), (-8,-2), (-7,-3), (-7,-4), (-6,-5), (-5,-6)$ |

✅ **(7 marks) Verified.** Total ≈ 49–56 unique symmetric pixels (axis overlaps reduce count).

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- ⭐ **Bresenham's Algorithm** uses only **integer addition/subtraction** — never multiplication per step.
- ⭐ **Initial $p_0$ for line (slope $\leq 1$):** $p_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x$.
- ⭐ **Initial $p_0$ for line (slope $> 1$):** $p_0 = 2\Delta x - \Delta y$ (swap roles of x and y).
- ⭐ **Sign test for line:** $p_k \leq 0 \Rightarrow$ East; $p_k > 0 \Rightarrow$ North-East.
- ⭐ **Initial $p_0$ for circle:** $p_0 = 1 - r$ (integer form, $1.25 - r$ is the half-pixel form).
- ⭐ **Sign test for circle:** $p_k < 0 \Rightarrow$ South (y unchanged); $p_k \geq 0 \Rightarrow$ South-East (y decreases).
- ⭐ **Stopping condition for circle:** Loop terminates when $x_k \geq y_k$ (Octant 2 boundary).
- ⭐ **8-way symmetry formulas** for circle (center at $(x_c, y_c)$):
  $(x_c \pm x, \ y_c \pm y)$, $(x_c \pm y, \ y_c \pm x)$.
- ⭐ **Algorithm complexity:** Both are $O(n)$ where $n$ is the number of pixels in the major axis.
- ⭐ **Examiner's tip:** Always include the **starting pixel plot** and the **8-symmetric pixels** in circle solutions.
- ⭐ **Common pitfall:** Using $\leq$ vs $<$ — the sign test differs between Bresenham (uses $\leq 0$) and Midpoint Circle (uses $< 0$).
- ⭐ **Real-world use:** GPU rasterizers, CNC plotters, OLED displays, medical imaging, and radar HUDs.
- ⭐ **Year invented:** Bresenham 1962 (line); Pitteway & Bresenham 1977 (circle midpoint form).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bresenham's Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT416 - GRAPHICS AND MULTIMEDIA PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bresenham's Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bresenham's Line Algorithm** is an *incremental scan-conversion algorithm* that determines which points on a 2D raster grid should be plotted to form a close approximation of a straight line between two given endpoints, using only **integer arithmetic** (addition, subtraction, and bit-shifting).

Unlike the **Digital Differential Analyzer (DDA)** algorithm, which requires floating-point multiplication and rounding, Bresenham's algorithm was developed by **Jack E. Bresenham (1962)** at IBM to overcome the computational bottleneck of floating-point operations on early hardware. It is one of the earliest algorithms in the field of computer graphics and remains a fundamental topic under KTU's *Raster Scan Graphics* syllabus.

### Key Assumptions (KTU Syllabus Standard Form)
The algorithm is most easily expressed for lines where the **slope $m$** satisfies $0 \le m \le 1$. For other octants, symmetry transformations are applied:

$$
0 \le \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} \le 1
$$

The two endpoints are $(x_0, y_0)$ and $(x_{end}, y_{end})$, with $\Delta x = x_{end} - x_0$ and $\Delta y = y_{end} - y_0$.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🪜 The Staircase Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are building a staircase from point A to point B on a graph paper. You must place a "step" at every integer column ($x = 0, 1, 2, \dots$). At each step, you have only **two choices**:
> 1. Place a tile at the **current row** (no vertical change), OR
> 2. Place a tile at the **next row up** (one unit up).
>
> The "perfect mathematical line" passes somewhere *between* these two choices. Bresenham's clever trick is that he does not need to compute the exact line at every step. He only computes a **decision variable** — a single integer — that asks: *"Is the true line closer to the upper tile, or to the lower tile?"* This decision is made using a very fast integer add/subtract, making the algorithm blazingly fast.

### Why This Matters in Engineering
Bresenham's algorithm is the **de-facto standard** inside GPU rasterizers, laser plotters, CNC machines, and embedded displays. Because it avoids floating-point arithmetic, it is deterministic and **robust on low-power hardware** (no FPU required), making it indispensable in firmware and real-time systems.

> [!TIP]
> **Constants you should commit to memory:**
> - Decision parameter: $P_k$
> - Increments: $2\Delta y$ and $2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$
> - Initial value: $P_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x$

---

## Comparison Snapshot (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Property | DDA Algorithm | Bresenham's Algorithm |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Arithmetic Type | Floating-point | **Integer only** |
| Speed | Slower | **Faster** |
| Accuracy | Rounding errors possible | **More accurate** |
| Complexity | $O(n)$ with mul/div | $O(n)$ with add/sub |
| Operations per step | 2 float add + 1 round | 1 integer add + 1 compare |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Geometry

For a line $y = mx + b$ with slope $m = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$, at integer column $x_k$, the **true mathematical y-coordinate** is:

$$
y = m \cdot x_k + b
$$

The two candidate pixels on the screen are:

- **Lower pixel:** $(x_k + 1, y_k)$  →  distance $d_{lower} = y - y_k$
- **Upper pixel:** $(x_k + 1, y_k + 1)$  →  distance $d_{upper} = (y_k + 1) - y$

The choice depends on the sign of the difference:

$$
d = d_{lower} - d_{upper} = 2m(x_k + 1) - 2y_k + 2b - 1
$$

Multiplying through by $\Delta x$ (which is positive in our assumed octant) to **eliminate the fraction** $m$:

$$
P_k = \Delta x \cdot (d_{lower} - d_{upper}) = 2 \Delta y \cdot x_k - 2 \Delta x \cdot y_k + c
$$

where $c = 2 \Delta y + \Delta x(2b - 1)$ is a constant. This $P_k$ is the **decision parameter**.

---

## 2.2 Recurrence Relations — The Heart of Bresenham's Algorithm

The entire algorithm is built on a beautiful insight: we don't compute $P_k$ from scratch each time. We compute $P_{k+1}$ from $P_k$ by simple addition.

$$
P_{k+1} - P_k = 2 \Delta y (x_{k+1} - x_k) - 2 \Delta x (y_{k+1} - y_k)
$$

Since $x_{k+1} = x_k + 1$ always, this simplifies dramatically. We get the **two-case update rule**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Case 1:** If $P_k \le 0$  (line is closer to, or exactly on, the lower pixel)
> - $y_{k+1} = y_k$  (no vertical step)
> - $P_{k+1} = P_k + 2 \Delta y$
>
> **Case 2:** If $P_k > 0$  (line is closer to the upper pixel)
> - $y_{k+1} = y_k + 1$  (take a vertical step)
> - $P_{k+1} = P_k + 2 \Delta y - 2 \Delta x$

And the **initial decision parameter** (at $k = 0$, $x_0, y_0$):

$$
P_0 = 2 \Delta y - \Delta x
$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Bookmark this table — every KTU Part B question on this topic is solved using ONLY these formulas.**

| Quantity | Formula | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $x$-increment | $x_{k+1} = x_k + 1$ | Always taken in assumed octant |
| Initial $P$ | $P_0 = 2 \Delta y - \Delta x$ | Compute once at start |
| Case 1: $P_k \le 0$ | $y_{k+1} = y_k$ | No vertical step |
| Case 1: Update $P$ | $P_{k+1} = P_k + 2 \Delta y$ | Add only |
| Case 2: $P_k > 0$ | $y_{k+1} = y_k + 1$ | Take vertical step |
| Case 2: Update $P$ | $P_{k+1} = P_k + 2 \Delta y - 2 \Delta x$ | Add and subtract |
| Termination | Stop when $x_k = x_{end}$ | Total $= \Delta x$ pixels |
| Octant 1 condition | $0 \le \Delta y \le \Delta x$ | If violated, swap roles |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **GPU Rasterization:** Every modern graphics card (NVIDIA, AMD, Intel) uses a Bresenham-derived algorithm in its fragment/pixel pipelines.
- **LCD/OLED Display Drivers:** Embedded display controllers use Bresenham to draw lines on tiny microcontrollers **without an FPU**.
- **CNC & 3D Printing:** Tool-path generation often relies on Bresenham-style incremental stepping for axis motors.
- **Plotter Hardware:** Legacy HP/pen plotters used Bresenham's algorithm in their firmware for speed.
- **Game Engines:** Line-of-sight and raycasting in classic games (Wolfenstein 3D) used Bresenham's logic.

---

## 2.5 Standard Pseudocode (Reference Only)

The KTU board typically asks for this in algorithm form. **This is a generic algorithm description, not source code:**

```
ALGORITHM BresenhamLine(x0, y0, x1, y1)
    dx  = x1 - x0
    dy  = y1 - y0
    P0  = 2*dy - dx
    x   = x0
    y   = y0
    plot(x, y)
    WHILE x < x1
        x = x + 1
        IF P < 0
            P = P + 2*dy
        ELSE
            y = y + 1
            P = P + 2*dy - 2*dx
        ENDIF
        plot(x, y)
    ENDWHILE
END
```

> [!NOTE]
> All variables in the pseudocode are **integers**. No floating-point types appear. This is the defining feature of Bresenham's method.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Decision Parameter $P_k$

**Step 1 — Start with the implicit line equation.**
The ideal line between $(x_0, y_0)$ and $(x_1, y_1)$ in implicit form is:

$$
F(x, y) = \Delta y \cdot x - \Delta x \cdot y + \Delta x \cdot b = 0
$$

where $b = y_0 - m \cdot x_0$. We can rewrite this as $y = m \cdot x + b$ with $m = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$.

**Step 2 — Evaluate $F$ at the two candidate pixel midpoints.**
For column $x_k + 1$, the midpoint between the two candidate pixels is:

$$
M = (x_k + 1, \; y_k + 0.5)
$$

Plugging $M$ into the line equation:

$$
F(M) = \Delta y (x_k + 1) - \Delta x (y_k + 0.5) + \Delta x \cdot b
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by 2 to clear the half.**
This is the key trick — multiplying by 2 yields a **decision parameter that is always an integer** if $\Delta x, \Delta y$ are integers:

$$
P_k = 2 F(M) = 2 \Delta y (x_k + 1) - 2 \Delta x (y_k + 0.5) + 2 \Delta x \cdot b
$$

Simplifying the $- 2 \Delta x (y_k + 0.5)$ term:

$$
P_k = 2 \Delta y (x_k + 1) - \Delta x (2 y_k + 1) + 2 \Delta x \cdot b
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the recurrence.**
Let us now compute $P_{k+1}$ at $(x_k + 1, y_{k+1})$:

$$
P_{k+1} = 2 \Delta y (x_k + 2) - \Delta x (2 y_{k+1} + 1) + 2 \Delta x \cdot b
$$

**Step 5 — Subtract to get the update rule.**

$$
P_{k+1} - P_k = 2 \Delta y - 2 \Delta x (y_{k+1} - y_k)
$$

**Step 6 — Branch the recurrence on the sign of $P_k$.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The sign of $P_k$ tells us where the midpoint $M$ lies relative to the ideal line. If $P_k \le 0$, the line is below or on the midpoint, so the **lower pixel** is closer → $y$ does not change. If $P_k > 0$, the line is above the midpoint, so the **upper pixel** is closer → $y$ increments by 1.

This gives the two cases listed in the formula sheet in Section 2.3.

**Step 7 — Compute the initial value $P_0$.**
Substitute $k = 0$, $x_0$, $y_0$ into the formula from Step 3:

$$
P_0 = 2 \Delta y (x_0 + 1) - \Delta x (2 y_0 + 1) + 2 \Delta x \cdot b
$$

Using $b = y_0 - \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} \cdot x_0$, so $2 \Delta x \cdot b = 2 \Delta x \cdot y_0 - 2 \Delta y \cdot x_0$:

$$
P_0 = 2 \Delta y (x_0 + 1) - \Delta x (2 y_0 + 1) + 2 \Delta x \cdot y_0 - 2 \Delta y \cdot x_0
$$

Expanding $2 \Delta y (x_0 + 1) = 2 \Delta y \cdot x_0 + 2 \Delta y$:

$$
P_0 = 2 \Delta y \cdot x_0 + 2 \Delta y - 2 \Delta x \cdot y_0 - \Delta x + 2 \Delta x \cdot y_0 - 2 \Delta y \cdot x_0
$$

The $2 \Delta y \cdot x_0$ terms cancel. The $2 \Delta x \cdot y_0$ terms cancel. We are left with:

$$
P_0 = 2 \Delta y - \Delta x
$$

This is the standard initial decision parameter. ✅

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — End-to-End Trace

> [!NOTE]
> **Question:** Use Bresenham's Algorithm to draw a line from $(0, 0)$ to $(5, 3)$. Plot all intermediate pixels and tabulate the decision parameter.

**Given:**
- $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 0$
- $x_1 = 5$, $y_1 = 3$
- $\Delta x = 5 - 0 = 5$
- $\Delta y = 3 - 0 = 3$

**Pre-computed constants:**
- $2 \Delta y = 6$
- $2 \Delta y - 2 \Delta x = 6 - 10 = -4$
- $P_0 = 2 \Delta y - \Delta x = 6 - 5 = 1$

**Iteration Table:**

| Step $k$ | $P_k$ | Sign | $x_{k+1}$ | $y_{k+1}$ | Plot |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | **1** | $> 0$ | 1 | 1 | $(1, 1)$ |
| 1 | $1 + (-4) = -3$ | $\le 0$ | 2 | 1 | $(2, 1)$ |
| 2 | $-3 + 6 = 3$ | $> 0$ | 3 | 2 | $(3, 2)$ |
| 3 | $3 + (-4) = -1$ | $\le 0$ | 4 | 2 | $(4, 2)$ |
| 4 | $-1 + 6 = 5$ | $> 0$ | 5 | 3 | $(5, 3)$ |

**Verification of the logic at Step 0:**
- We are at $(0,0)$ and $P_0 = 1 > 0$, so we step to $(1, 1)$.
- Update: $P_1 = 1 + (2 \Delta y - 2 \Delta x) = 1 + (-4) = -3$. ✅

**Verification of the logic at Step 1:**
- We are at $(1,1)$ and $P_1 = -3 \le 0$, so we step horizontally to $(2, 1)$.
- Update: $P_2 = -3 + 2 \Delta y = -3 + 6 = 3$. ✅

**Verification of the logic at Step 2:**
- We are at $(2,1)$ and $P_2 = 3 > 0$, so we step diagonally to $(3, 2)$.
- Update: $P_3 = 3 + (-4) = -1$. ✅

**Verification of the logic at Step 3:**
- We are at $(3,2)$ and $P_3 = -1 \le 0$, so we step horizontally to $(4, 2)$.
- Update: $P_4 = -1 + 6 = 5$. ✅

**Verification of the logic at Step 4:**
- We are at $(4,2)$ and $P_4 = 5 > 0$, so we step diagonally to $(5, 3)$.
- This is the endpoint, so we stop. ✅

**Final Pixels Plotted (in order):**

$$
(0,0) \to (1,1) \to (2,1) \to (3,2) \to (4,2) \to (5,3)
$$

This produces **6 pixels** total, which is exactly $\Delta x + 1 = 6$. ✅

> [!TIP]
> **Valuator's trick:** The number of pixels plotted must always equal $\Delta x + 1$ for the assumed octant. If your answer gives a different count, you have made a sign error in the update rule.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — All Octants at a Glance

| Octant | Slope $m$ | $x$-step | $y$-step | When $P_k \le 0$ | When $P_k > 0$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | $0 \le m \le 1$ | $x+1$ | $y$ or $y+1$ | $y_{k+1} = y_k$ | $y_{k+1} = y_k + 1$ |
| 2 | $m > 1$ | $y+1$ | $x$ or $x+1$ | $x_{k+1} = x_k$ | $x_{k+1} = x_k + 1$ |
| 3 | $-1 \le m < 0$ | $x+1$ | $y-1$ or $y$ | $y_{k+1} = y_k$ | $y_{k+1} = y_k - 1$ |
| 4 | $m < -1$ | $y-1$ | $x$ or $x+1$ | $x_{k+1} = x_k$ | $x_{k+1} = x_k + 1$ |
| 5–8 | (Symmetric to 1–4) | (Swap roles of $x$ and $y$, reverse signs as needed) |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU board problems, **Octant 1 is by far the most common (≈ 80% of past papers).** The other octants are conceptually identical — they just swap the role of $x$ and $y$, and adjust the sign of $\Delta y$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid flow chart captures the **complete control flow** of Bresenham's algorithm. Each decision branch is labeled with the integer arithmetic operation it performs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: x0, y0, x1, y1]) --> B["dx = x1 - x0  dy = y1 - y0  2dy, 2dym2dx precompute"]
    B --> C["P0 = 2dy - dx"]
    C --> D["x = x0  y = y0  plot x, y"]
    D --> E{"x is less than x1"}
    E -- No --> Z([End: All Pixels Plotted])
    E -- Yes --> F["x = x + 1"]
    F --> G{"P less than or equal 0"}
    G -- Yes --> H["y stays same  P = P + 2dy"]
    G -- No --> I["y = y + 1  P = P + 2dy - 2dx"]
    H --> J["plot x, y"]
    I --> J
    J --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every node is a *state* (values of $x$, $y$, $P$). Every edge is a *transition* triggered by a comparison. The **diamond nodes** G and E are the only conditional checks — exactly what makes this algorithm so fast on hardware.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following diagram explains the **hardware-software division of responsibility** in a typical raster display system where Bresenham's algorithm operates.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Application Layer] --> B[Geometry Engine]
    B --> C[Line Rasterizer Module]
    C --> D[Decision Logic Unit]
    C --> E[Integer ALU]
    C --> F[Pixel Plot Buffer]
    D --> G[Frame Buffer Memory]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Display Refresh Controller]
    H --> I[Screen Pixels]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-by-block explanation:**
> - **A. Application Layer:** The user-level program requests a line to be drawn, providing two endpoints.
> - **B. Geometry Engine:** Performs viewport transforms, clipping, and lighting.
> - **C. Line Rasterizer Module:** Executes **Bresenham's Algorithm** — the heart of this entire block diagram.
> - **D. Decision Logic Unit:** Compares $P_k$ with 0. It is a single comparator circuit.
> - **E. Integer ALU:** Performs only **add** and **subtract** — no FPU is needed.
> - **F. Pixel Plot Buffer:** Temporarily stores pixels before flushing to the frame buffer.
> - **G. Frame Buffer Memory:** A 2D array of pixel values in VRAM.
> - **H. Display Refresh Controller:** Reads the frame buffer at 60 Hz and pushes pixels to the monitor.

---

## 4.3 Pixel Plotting Visualization (Worked Example)

For the example in Section 3.2, here is a **textual raster grid** showing the plotted pixels (marked with `█`) versus the empty cells (marked with `·`).

```
y=3  · · · · █
y=2  · · █ █ ·
y=1  · █ █ · ·
y=0  █ · · · ·
     0 1 2 3 4 5  x
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to verify visually:** The pixel staircase must rise from bottom-left to top-right. The line should have a **gradient of $3/5 = 0.6$**, meaning for every 5 steps right, the line should rise 3 steps. Indeed, we went up at $x = 1, 3, 5$ — exactly three vertical steps over five horizontal columns. ✅
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**Q: Differentiate between DDA algorithm and Bresenham's line drawing algorithm. List any two advantages of Bresenham's algorithm over DDA.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> | Aspect | DDA | Bresenham |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Arithmetic | Floating-point | **Integer only** |
> | Operations/step | Addition + Round-off | Addition + Comparison |
> | Speed | Slower | **Faster** |
> | Hardware Suitability | Needs FPU | Runs on bare ALU |

**Two Advantages of Bresenham's Algorithm:**
1. It uses **only integer arithmetic**, eliminating the need for a floating-point unit and producing **no rounding errors**.
2. It is **computationally faster** as it uses only addition, subtraction, and comparison — no multiplication or division per step.

*(3 marks: 1 for table, 1 per advantage with valid justification)*

---

### **Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**Q: What is the role of the decision parameter $P_k$ in Bresenham's line algorithm? Write the formula for $P_0$.**

**Model Answer:**

The decision parameter $P_k$ is an **integer quantity** computed at each step that determines whether the next pixel to be plotted lies on the **lower diagonal** or the **upper diagonal** of the ideal mathematical line. Its sign directly drives the next $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1})$ coordinate, replacing expensive floating-point division with a single integer compare-and-branch.

The initial value is:

$$
P_0 = 2 \Delta y - \Delta x
$$

*(3 marks: 2 for the role of $P_k$, 1 for the formula with correct symbols)*

---

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)**, typically carrying **7 marks each**. The examiner provides an internal choice — attempt **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`**

**(a)** Explain Bresenham's line drawing algorithm with the help of a flowchart. Derive the initial decision parameter $P_0 = 2 \Delta y - \Delta x$.

**(b)** Using Bresenham's line drawing algorithm, rasterize the line from $(2, 2)$ to $(8, 5)$. Tabulate the decision parameter and plot the pixels.

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Bresenham's algorithm is an incremental scan-conversion method that determines which pixels to light up to approximate a straight line, using only integer arithmetic. It is significantly faster than the DDA algorithm because it avoids floating-point operations.

**Assumed Octant (1 mark):**
We consider lines with slope $0 \le m \le 1$, i.e., $0 \le \Delta y \le \Delta x$. For other slopes, the roles of $x$ and $y$ are swapped (see Section 3.3).

**Derivation of $P_0$ (3 marks):**
Starting from the line $y = mx + b$ with $m = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$, we evaluate the implicit form at the midpoint $M(x_k+1, y_k+0.5)$ between candidate pixels:

$$
F(x,y) = \Delta y \cdot x - \Delta x \cdot y + \Delta x \cdot b
$$

Multiplying by 2 to clear the half:

$$
P_k = 2\Delta y(x_k+1) - \Delta x(2y_k+1) + 2\Delta x \cdot b
$$

Substituting $b = y_0 - \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} x_0$ and simplifying (see Section 3.1, Steps 4–7) yields:

$$
P_0 = 2 \Delta y - \Delta x
$$

**Update Rule (1 mark):**

- If $P_k \le 0$: $\quad P_{k+1} = P_k + 2 \Delta y$
- If $P_k > 0$: $\quad P_{k+1} = P_k + 2 \Delta y - 2 \Delta x$

**Flowchart (1 mark):**
See the flowchart in Section 4.1.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $x_0 = 2$, $y_0 = 2$, $x_1 = 8$, $y_1 = 5$

**Pre-computation:**

- $\Delta x = 8 - 2 = 6$
- $\Delta y = 5 - 2 = 3$
- $2 \Delta y = 6$
- $2 \Delta y - 2 \Delta x = 6 - 12 = -6$
- $P_0 = 2(3) - 6 = 0$

**Iteration Table:**

| Step $k$ | $P_k$ | Sign | $x_{k+1}$ | $y_{k+1}$ | Plot |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | **0** | $\le 0$ | 3 | 2 | $(3, 2)$ |
| 1 | $0 + 6 = 6$ | $> 0$ | 4 | 3 | $(4, 3)$ |
| 2 | $6 + (-6) = 0$ | $\le 0$ | 5 | 3 | $(5, 3)$ |
| 3 | $0 + 6 = 6$ | $> 0$ | 6 | 4 | $(6, 4)$ |
| 4 | $6 + (-6) = 0$ | $\le 0$ | 7 | 4 | $(7, 4)$ |
| 5 | $0 + 6 = 6$ | $> 0$ | 8 | 5 | $(8, 5)$ |

**Plot Visualization:**

```
y=5  · · · · · · █
y=4  · · · · █ █ ·
y=3  · · █ █ · · ·
y=2  · █ · · · · ·
y=1  · · · · · · ·
y=0  · · · · · · ·
     2 3 4 5 6 7 8   x
```

The algorithm correctly produces 7 pixels (count = $\Delta x + 1 = 7$ ✅), and the staircase rises from $(2,2)$ to $(8,5)$ with a gradient of $3/6 = 0.5$. ✅

---

### **Question B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`** *(ALTERNATIVE)*

**(a)** Write the Bresenham's line drawing algorithm. Explain the significance of the decision parameter with a neat sketch of the geometry used.

**(b)** Apply Bresenham's algorithm to plot a line from $(0, 0)$ to $(6, 4)$. Tabulate the values of $P_k$, $x_k$, $y_k$ for all intermediate points. Also state the advantages and limitations of the algorithm.

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Algorithm (3 marks):** See the pseudocode in Section 2.5. Briefly: input endpoints, compute $\Delta x, \Delta y$, initialize $P_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x$, plot starting pixel, then iterate $\Delta x$ times — comparing $P_k$ with 0 and updating coordinates and $P_k$ using the two-case rule.

**Geometry Sketch — The Midpoint Test (2 marks):**
For column $x_k+1$, two candidate pixels exist: the lower $(x_k+1, y_k)$ and the upper $(x_k+1, y_k+1)$. Bresenham's insight is to evaluate the **implicit line equation** at the **midpoint** $M = (x_k+1, y_k+0.5)$ between these candidates:

- If $F(M) < 0$: midpoint lies *below* the line → pick the **upper pixel** ($y$ increments)
- If $F(M) \ge 0$: midpoint lies *on or above* the line → pick the **lower pixel** ($y$ stays)

Multiplying $F(M)$ by $2 \Delta x$ (to clear the fractional $m$ and the 0.5) yields the **integer decision parameter** $P_k$ used in the algorithm.

**Significance of Decision Parameter (2 marks):**
$P_k$ collapses three operations (fractional $y$, multiplication, comparison) into a **single integer add and a single integer compare per pixel**. It is the variable that carries the "memory" of where the line is, allowing the algorithm to be incremental and O(1) per pixel.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 0$, $x_1 = 6$, $y_1 = 4$

**Pre-computation:**

- $\Delta x = 6$, $\Delta y = 4$
- $2 \Delta y = 8$, $2 \Delta y - 2 \Delta x = 8 - 12 = -4$
- $P_0 = 8 - 6 = 2$

**Iteration Table:**

| Step $k$ | $P_k$ | Sign | $x_{k+1}$ | $y_{k+1}$ | Plot |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | **2** | $> 0$ | 1 | 1 | $(1, 1)$ |
| 1 | $2 + (-4) = -2$ | $\le 0$ | 2 | 1 | $(2, 1)$ |
| 2 | $-2 + 8 = 6$ | $> 0$ | 3 | 2 | $(3, 2)$ |
| 3 | $6 + (-4) = 2$ | $> 0$ | 4 | 3 | $(4, 3)$ |
| 4 | $2 + (-4) = -2$ | $\le 0$ | 5 | 3 | $(5, 3)$ |
| 5 | $-2 + 8 = 6$ | $> 0$ | 6 | 4 | $(6, 4)$ |

**Plot Visualization:**

```
y=4  · · · · · · █
y=3  · · · █ █ · ·
y=2  · · █ · · · ·
y=1  · █ █ · · · ·
y=0  █ · · · · · ·
     0 1 2 3 4 5 6   x
```

**Total pixels plotted:** 7 (matches $\Delta x + 1$ ✅)

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. Uses only **integer arithmetic** — fast, no rounding, no FPU required.
2. **Incrementally updates** $P_k$ via simple addition, making it $O(n)$ with $O(1)$ work per pixel.
3. **Highly accurate** for raster lines — produces the closest pixel at every step.
4. Easily extends to **circles** (Bresenham's circle algorithm) and other conics.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
1. Designed primarily for lines in **Octant 1**; extending to other slopes requires swapping roles of $x$ and $y$, increasing code complexity.
2. Produces visible **staircase aliasing** — this is a property of rasterization itself, not a flaw of the algorithm.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in $P_0$:** The most common mistake is writing $P_0 = 2\Delta x - \Delta y$ (swapped). Always derive from the implicit form: **$P_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x$**.
> 2. **Wrong Update Rule:** Students often use $P_{k+1} = P_k + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ in BOTH cases. The correct rule is:
>    - Case $P_k \le 0$: $\quad P_{k+1} = P_k + 2\Delta y$  *(only add)*
>    - Case $P_k > 0$: $\quad P_{k+1} = P_k + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$  *(add and subtract)*
> 3. **Forgetting the Plot at $(x_0, y_0)$:** The starting pixel must be plotted before the loop. Examiners explicitly check for this (1 mark lost if missed).
> 4. **Off-by-one termination:** Loop must run exactly $\Delta x$ times. Running $\Delta x + 1$ times or $\Delta x - 1$ times is a deduction.
> 5. **Missing the condition $0 \le m \le 1$:** Always state the octant assumption at the start of your answer — 1 mark is reserved for this.
> 6. **Not showing the derivation of $P_0$ in Part (a):** Simply stating the formula without derivation costs 2 of the 7 marks. See Section 3.1 for the full derivation chain.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam**

- ✅ **Bresenham's Algorithm** is an *integer-only* line rasterization technique — its defining feature.
- ✅ It assumes **Octant 1** by default: $0 \le \Delta y \le \Delta x$ and $\Delta x > 0$.
- ✅ The **initial decision parameter** is $P_0 = 2 \Delta y - \Delta x$.
- ✅ The **update rule has TWO cases**:
  - $P_k \le 0 \Rightarrow P_{k+1} = P_k + 2 \Delta y$ (no $y$ step)
  - $P_k > 0 \Rightarrow P_{k+1} = P_k + 2 \Delta y - 2 \Delta x$ (one $y$ step)
- ✅ $x$ **always increments by 1** per iteration in Octant 1.
- ✅ Total pixels drawn = $\Delta x + 1$ (this is your self-check).
- ✅ Loop terminates when $x = x_{end}$ (or after $\Delta x$ iterations).
- ✅ **Compare with DDA:** DDA uses floating-point; Bresenham uses only integers — Bresenham is faster, more accurate, and FPU-free.
- ✅ The **midpoint geometry** (evaluating the implicit line equation at the midpoint between candidate pixels) is the elegant foundation of the algorithm.
- ✅ Bresenham's algorithm extends to **circles** (Midpoint Circle Algorithm) — a frequent follow-up question in KTU Module 1.
- ✅ The algorithm is $O(n)$ with $O(1)$ work per pixel, where $n$ is the number of pixels.
- ✅ **Hardware impact:** Used in GPUs, LCD controllers, plotters, and CNC machines — no floating-point hardware required.
- ✅ Examiners' hot topics: derivation of $P_0$, the two-case update rule, the worked-example table, and the comparison with DDA.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Filled Area Primitives - Scan Line Polygon Filling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT416 - GRAPHICS AND MULTIMEDIA PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Filled Area Primitives - Scan Line Polygon Filling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Scan Line Polygon Filling** is an *image-space* (also called *pixel-space*) algorithm used in computer graphics to rasterize the interior of a polygon. The algorithm sweeps a horizontal line (the **scan line**) from $y = y_{min}$ to $y = y_{max}$ of the polygon. For every scan line, it computes the set of **parity-bit intersections** with the polygon's edges, sorts them by $x$, and then fills the pixels lying strictly between each successive pair of intersection points.

The technique relies on the **Odd-Even Rule** (Parity Rule): a point $(x, y)$ is *inside* the polygon if a horizontal ray cast from that point crosses the polygon boundary an **odd** number of times. Pixels at odd–even transition boundaries are turned ON (filled) and pixels at even–odd transitions are turned OFF (left untouched).

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Scan Line** | An imaginary horizontal line, $y = c$, used to sample the polygon one row of pixels at a time. |
| **Edge Table (ET)** | A bucket-sorted data structure indexed by $y_{min}$ of each edge, holding all non-horizontal edges. |
| **Active Edge List (AEL)** | A dynamic, sorted list of edges currently intersected by the active scan line. |
| **y_max Bucket** | A list of edges whose lowest vertex lies on the current scan line $y$. |
| **Shorthand / Vertex Counting** | A rule to avoid double-counting an intersection at a polygon vertex. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Painting a Staircase Wall:** Imagine you are painting the silhouette of a mountain on a giant wall. You have a horizontal laser level (the *scan line*) that you slowly drop from the top of the mountain to the bottom. Each time the laser crosses the boundary, you shout "**in**" or "**out**". Between the first "in" and the first "out", the wall is mountain, so you paint it. Between the first "out" and the next "in", the wall is sky, so you leave it. By the time the laser reaches the floor, you have painted the entire mountain in horizontal stripes — without ever needing to know the polygon's full mathematical equation.

The algorithm turns this physical intuition into a deterministic, frame-buffer-friendly procedure. Two clever data structures — the **Edge Table (ET)** and the **Active Edge List (AEL)** — make the procedure run in $O(N \cdot M)$ time, where $N$ is the number of scan lines and $M$ is the number of edges, an order of magnitude faster than naïve per-pixel testing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Topic is Exam-Critical (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Scan line polygon filling is a *high-yield* concept that bridges the gap between **geometric primitives** and **rasterization hardware**. It directly tests the Course Outcomes (COs) related to *filling algorithms*, *data structures for graphics*, and *interior testing*. Almost every KTU End-Semester paper (2019–2024) features at least one 7-mark sub-question on the construction of the Edge Table / AEL for a hand-drawn polygon.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Three-Phase Pipeline

The algorithm decomposes into three logically distinct phases:

**Phase A — Static Pre-processing (Build the Edge Table):**
1. Traverse every pair of consecutive polygon vertices $\{(x_i, y_i), (x_{i+1}, y_{i+1})\}$.
2. **Skip horizontal edges** (where $y_i = y_{i+1}$). They contribute no scan-line intersection but cause vertex double-counting bugs.
3. For each non-horizontal edge, normalize it so that $y_{max} > y_{min}$ (i.e., the lower vertex comes first).
4. Insert the edge into bucket $\text{ET}[y_{min}]$ with three stored fields:
   - $y_{max}$ — the last scan line on which the edge is active.
   - $x_{y_{min}}$ — the $x$-coordinate at the lower endpoint (used as the initial intersection).
   - $1/m$ — the *inverse slope* along $x$ per unit $y$.

**Phase B — Per-Scan-Line Maintenance (Drive the AEL):**
For each integer $y$ from $y_{min}^{polygon}$ to $y_{max}^{polygon}$:
1. **Add:** Insert every edge from $\text{ET}[y]$ into the AEL.
2. **Remove:** Drop every edge whose $y_{max} = y$ (it no longer intersects the *next* scan line).
3. **Sort:** Reorder the AEL by the current $x$ intersection values.
4. **Fill:** Walk the sorted AEL in pairs $(x_1, x_2), (x_3, x_4), \ldots$ and set $\text{FrameBuffer}[x_1 \ldots x_2, y] = \text{fillColor}$.

**Phase C — Stepwise Update (Propagate the AEL):**
1. For every edge still in the AEL, advance its $x$ by $1/m$: $x_{new} = x_{old} + (1/m)$.
2. Increment $y$ by 1 and return to Phase B until the AEL is empty and all buckets are drained.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the *only* formulas you need to memorize for scan line polygon filling in the KTU 2024 ESE.

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{1. Inverse Slope:} \quad \frac{1}{m} = \frac{x_2 - x_1}{y_2 - y_1} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{2. Stepwise } x \textbf{ Update:} \quad x_{i+1} = x_i + \frac{1}{m} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{3. Edge Bucket Index:} \quad \text{Bucket} = y_{min} = \min(y_1, y_2) \\[4pt]
&\textbf{4. Active Span Rule:} \quad \text{Fill } [x_{2k-1}, x_{2k}] \text{ for } k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \\[4pt]
&\textbf{5. Termination:} \quad y = y_{max}^{polygon} \;\;\wedge\;\; \text{AEL} = \varnothing
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bar symbol $\vert$ (used for absolute value or set cardinality) is rendered with the LaTeX command `\vert` to remain compatible with markdown tables. Never write a raw `|` inside a table cell — it breaks the table parser.

**Notation Table for the Edge Record**

| Field | Mathematical Symbol | Stored At | Updated When |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lower $y$ | $y_{min}$ | Build time | Never changes |
| Upper $y$ | $y_{max}$ | Build time | Compared each step for removal |
| Current $x$ | $x$ | Build: $x_{y_{min}}$; Step: $x + 1/m$ | Every scan line increment |
| Inverse slope | $1/m$ | Build time | Never changes (constant per edge) |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where This Algorithm is Used

> [!TIP]
> - **Graphics Processing Units (GPUs):** The fixed-function rasterizers in legacy GPU pipelines (pre-2006) used scan-line–like traversal before tile-based renderers took over.
> - **Laser Cutting & CNC Toolpaths:** Industrial CAM software converts 2D vector outlines into scan-line "on/off" bitmaps to drive $CO_2$ laser tubes and plasma cutters.
> - **3D Printing Slicers:** The first stage of every FDM/FFF slicer (Cura, PrusaSlicer) is *exactly* a scan-line polygon fill over each layer to generate per-pixel toolpath decisions.
> - **Inkjet & Photo Printing:** Raster Image Processors (RIPs) treat each page as a polygon composite and fill the CMYK channels scan-line by scan-line.

### 2.4 Coherence Properties — The Secret of its Efficiency

Scan line filling exploits four kinds of coherence to amortize computation across neighbouring pixels:

1. **Scan-line coherence:** The interior/exterior pattern of scan line $y$ is almost identical to scan line $y - 1$. Only at edge-crossings does it flip.
2. **Edge coherence:** The $x$ intersection of an edge changes by a *constant* amount $1/m$ per scan line. No per-pixel re-derivation of the edge equation is required.
3. **Span coherence:** Long horizontal runs of interior pixels exist on each scan line — once we know the left/right boundaries, every pixel between them is filled by a single `memset`-style operation.
4. **Area coherence:** The interior of a connected region shares the same fill colour/pattern. This is exploited by *seed-fill* and *boundary-fill* algorithms (related topics in Module 2).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Inverse-Slope Derivation (Why 1/m and not m?)

The classical line equation in point-slope form is:

$$
y - y_1 = m \, (x - x_1)
$$

For a polygon edge, we are given the two endpoints $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ and we want to know, given the current $x$ at scan line $y$, what the $x$ will be at scan line $y + 1$.

We can solve for the *change in $x$ per unit change in $y$* by inverting the slope:

$$
\frac{dx}{dy} = \frac{1}{m} = \frac{x_2 - x_1}{y_2 - y_1}
$$

**Why store $1/m$ instead of $m$?** Because during the scan, we increment $y$ by 1 and need the corresponding $\Delta x$. Storing $1/m$ turns the per-step update into a single addition:

$$
x_{y+1} = x_y + \frac{1}{m}
$$

If we had stored $m$ instead, we would have to perform a division on every edge on every scan line — drastically slower.

**Edge-case handling:**
- If the edge is **vertical** ($x_1 = x_2$), then $1/m = 0$ and the $x$ coordinate never changes.
- If the edge is **horizontal** ($y_1 = y_2$), we skip the edge from the ET entirely. It contributes zero intersections on a single scan line, but it can cause vertex double-counting. Skipping it cleanly avoids the issue.

### 3.2 Vertex-Counting Pitfall — The Shorthand Rule

> [!WARNING]
> **The "double-counting vertex" trap:** When two edges meet at a local extremum (a vertex where the polygon changes direction relative to the scan line), naive intersection counting will record *two* intersections at the same $x$ — flipping parity twice and accidentally leaving the polygon "outside".

**Example of the problem (with a triangle):**

Consider the triangle $(2, 1), (8, 1), (5, 6)$. The vertex $(8, 1)$ is a *local minimum* in $x$ — both edges meeting here have $y$ values on both sides of the scan line $y = 1$. Naive counting gives 4 intersections on $y = 1$ (two from each edge), making the parity *even* outside the intended interior.

**The Shorthand Solution:** At any vertex where an edge has $y_{min}$ equal to the vertex $y$ (i.e., the edge is starting), **shorten the edge by one pixel** by incrementing $y_{min}$ to $y_{min} + 1$. Equivalently, when two edges share a vertex, count them as 1 intersection (not 2) if the scan line passes exactly through that vertex and the two edges lie on *opposite* sides of the scan line.

The decision rule:
- If the two edges are on **opposite sides** of the scan line (one going up, one going down) — count as **1** intersection.
- If both edges are on the **same side** of the scan line (e.g., both going up, as in a "V" shape) — count as **2** intersections.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Pentagon (2,1) → (6,1) → (8,4) → (6,7) → (2,7)

This example will be carried through end-to-end so you can see every numerical step. We will trace scan lines $y = 1$ through $y = 7$.

**Polygon vertices in order:** $P_1(2,1), \; P_2(6,1), \; P_3(8,4), \; P_4(6,7), \; P_5(2,7)$.

**Step 1 — Identify and classify each edge:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &: P_1 \to P_2 = (2,1) \to (6,1) \quad &&\text{Horizontal, SKIP} \\
E_2 &: P_2 \to P_3 = (6,1) \to (8,4) \quad &&\text{Non-horizontal, } y_{min}=1,\; y_{max}=4 \\
E_3 &: P_3 \to P_4 = (8,4) \to (6,7) \quad &&\text{Non-horizontal, } y_{min}=4,\; y_{max}=7 \\
E_4 &: P_4 \to P_5 = (6,7) \to (2,7) \quad &&\text{Horizontal, SKIP} \\
E_5 &: P_5 \to P_1 = (2,7) \to (2,1) \quad &&\text{Vertical, } y_{min}=1,\; y_{max}=7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $1/m$ and $x_{y_{min}}$ for each non-horizontal edge:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_2 &: \frac{1}{m} = \frac{x_3 - x_2}{y_3 - y_2} = \frac{8 - 6}{4 - 1} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667 \quad ; \quad x_{y_{min}} = 6 \\
E_3 &: \frac{1}{m} = \frac{x_4 - x_3}{y_4 - y_3} = \frac{6 - 8}{7 - 4} = \frac{-2}{3} \approx -0.667 \quad ; \quad x_{y_{min}} = 8 \\
E_5 &: \text{Vertical} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{m} = 0 \quad ; \quad x_{y_{min}} = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Construct the Edge Table:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Bucket } y & \textbf{Edge} & (x_{y_{min}}, y_{max}, 1/m) & \textbf{Stored as} \\
\hline
1 & E_2 & (6,\; 4,\; 2/3) & (6, 4, 0.667) \\
1 & E_5 & (2,\; 7,\; 0) & (2, 7, 0.000) \\
\hline
4 & E_3 & (8,\; 7,\; -2/3) & (8, 7, -0.667) \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Step 4 — Execute the scan-line loop:**

**Scan line $y = 1$:**
- Add $E_2(6, 4, 0.667)$ and $E_5(2, 7, 0.000)$ from ET[1].
- No removals (no $y_{max}$ equals 1).
- Sort by $x$: AEL $= [(2, 7, 0), (6, 4, 0.667)]$.
- Fill span: pixels from $x = 2$ to $x = 6$ on row $y = 1$.

**Scan line $y = 2$:**
- No additions, no removals.
- Update $x$: $E_5.x = 2 + 0 = 2$; $E_2.x = 6 + 0.667 = 6.667$.
- AEL $= [(2, 7, 0), (6.667, 4, 0.667)]$.
- Fill span: $x = 2$ to $x = 6.667$ on row $y = 2$.

**Scan line $y = 3$:**
- No additions, no removals.
- Update $x$: $E_5.x = 2$; $E_2.x = 6.667 + 0.667 = 7.333$.
- AEL $= [(2, 7, 0), (7.333, 4, 0.667)]$.
- Fill span: $x = 2$ to $x = 7.333$ on row $y = 3$.

**Scan line $y = 4$:**
- Add $E_3(8, 7, -0.667)$ from ET[4].
- Remove $E_2$ (its $y_{max} = 4$).
- Update $x$ for remaining edges: $E_5.x = 2 + 0 = 2$.
- AEL $= [(2, 7, 0), (8, 7, -0.667)]$.
- Fill span: $x = 2$ to $x = 8$ on row $y = 4$.

**Scan line $y = 5$:**
- No additions, no removals.
- Update $x$: $E_5.x = 2$; $E_3.x = 8 + (-0.667) = 7.333$.
- AEL $= [(2, 7, 0), (7.333, 7, -0.667)]$.
- Fill span: $x = 2$ to $x = 7.333$ on row $y = 5$.

**Scan line $y = 6$:**
- No additions, no removals.
- Update $x$: $E_5.x = 2$; $E_3.x = 7.333 + (-0.667) = 6.667$.
- AEL $= [(2, 7, 0), (6.667, 7, -0.667)]$.
- Fill span: $x = 2$ to $x = 6.667$ on row $y = 6$.

**Scan line $y = 7$:**
- Remove $E_5$ (its $y_{max} = 7$); remove $E_3$ (its $y_{max} = 7$).
- AEL becomes empty.
- **Algorithm terminates.**

### 3.4 Summary Table of AEL States & Filled Spans

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Scan Line } y & \textbf{AEL (sorted by } x) & \textbf{Fill Span } [x_{left}, x_{right}] & \textbf{Pixels Filled} \\
\hline
1 & [(2,\; 7,\; 0), \;(6,\; 4,\; 0.667)] & [2,\; 6] & 5 \\
\hline
2 & [(2,\; 7,\; 0), \;(6.667,\; 4,\; 0.667)] & [2,\; 6.667] & 4.667 \\
\hline
3 & [(2,\; 7,\; 0), \;(7.333,\; 4,\; 0.667)] & [2,\; 7.333] & 5.333 \\
\hline
4 & [(2,\; 7,\; 0), \;(8,\; 7,\; -0.667)] & [2,\; 8] & 6 \\
\hline
5 & [(2,\; 7,\; 0), \;(7.333,\; 7,\; -0.667)] & [2,\; 7.333] & 5.333 \\
\hline
6 & [(2,\; 7,\; 0), \;(6.667,\; 7,\; -0.667)] & [2,\; 6.667] & 4.667 \\
\hline
7 & \text{AEL} = \varnothing & \text{None} & 0 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Notice how the maximum fill width occurs at $y = 4$ — the "widest" scan line of the pentagon. This is also the scan line that triggers the **vertex handling** logic (where $E_2$ ends and $E_3$ begins). Forgetting the vertex rule on this scan line will either double-fill or miss-fill the rightmost column — a classic 2-mark deduction in KTU valuation.

### 3.5 Complexity Analysis

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n, m) &= O(n_{\text{edges}} \cdot m_{\text{scanlines}}) \\
&= O(N) \text{ build time for the Edge Table} \\
&\quad + O(N) \text{ per scan line for AEL sorting (insertion sort on small lists)} \\
&\quad + O(1) \text{ per pixel for fill} \\
\text{Overall} &\approx O(N_{y} \cdot \log N_{y}) \text{ where } N_{y} = y_{max} - y_{min} + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, because the AEL is typically very short (often 2–10 edges), an $O(N_y \cdot N_{edges}^2)$ implementation with simple insertion sort actually *outperforms* a sophisticated $O(N_y \cdot N_{edges} \log N_{edges})$ heap-based sort in cache terms. This is why scan line filling remains in real-time systems.
