## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Algorithm Flowchart

The following Mermaid block diagrams the exact control flow of the scan line polygon filling algorithm, including all five sub-routines (Build ET, Update AEL, Sort AEL, Fill Spans, Termination Check).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([START])
    init1[Set AEL = empty]
    init2[Build Edge Table ET from polygon vertices]
    init3[Normalize all edges: y_max greater than y_min]
    init4[Skip horizontal edges from ET]
    init5[Compute 1 over m and x at y_min for each edge]
    yLoop{Y = Y_min of polygon}
    addStep[ADD edges from ET Y to AEL]
    remStep[REMOVE edges with y_max equals Y from AEL]
    sortStep[SORT AEL by current X value ascending]
    fillStep[FILL pixels in spans between pairs of X intersections]
    updStep[UPDATE X for every edge: X = X + 1 over m]
    incY[Y = Y + 1]
    termCheck{AEL empty AND ET empty}
    stopNode([END])
    
    startNode --> init1
    init1 --> init2
    init2 --> init3
    init3 --> init4
    init4 --> init5
    init5 --> yLoop
    yLoop --> addStep
    addStep --> remStep
    remStep --> sortStep
    sortStep --> fillStep
    fillStep --> updStep
    updStep --> incY
    incY --> termCheck
    termCheck -->|NO| yLoop
    termCheck -->|YES| stopNode
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

The algorithm is best implemented as a pipeline of four cooperating modules. The block diagram below shows the data path and the feedback loop that updates the AEL.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1[POLYGON VERTICES] --> m1
    m1[EDGE TABLE BUILDER] --> et[(EDGE TABLE ET)]
    et --> m2
    m2[AEL MAINTAINER] --> ael[(ACTIVE EDGE LIST AEL)]
    ael --> m3
    m3[X INTERSECTION COMPUTER] --> fb[(FRAME BUFFER MEMORY)]
    ael --> m4
    m4[SPAN FILLER ODD EVEN PARITY] --> fb
    fb --> m5
    m5[SCAN LINE COORDINATOR Y] --> m2
    m5 --> m4
```

**Description of the data flow:**

- The **Polygon Vertices** enter the **Edge Table Builder** once, at setup time. This module also normalises the edges and skips horizontal ones.
- The **ET** is a static, random-access structure (a bucket-sorted array indexed by $y$).
- On every clock tick (every increment of $y$), the **AEL Maintainer** pulls new edges from the ET, drops expired ones, and re-sorts.
- The **X-Intersection Computer** uses the $1/m$ field on each AEL record to update the $x$ coordinate by one addition.
- The **Span Filler** walks the AEL in pairs, applying the odd-even rule, and writes directly into the **Frame Buffer Memory**.
- The **Scan Line Coordinator** increments $y$ and triggers the maintainer + filler at every step until both lists are empty.

### 4.3 Worked Pentagon (Visual Schematic)

The pentagon example from §3.3 is rendered below as a Mermaid block diagram, showing the polygon outline and the scan-line-by-scan-line filling pattern.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph POLYGON_OUTLINE
        v1((P1: 2,1))
        v2((P2: 6,1))
        v3((P3: 8,4))
        v4((P4: 6,7))
        v5((P5: 2,7))
        v1 --- v2
        v2 --- v3
        v3 --- v4
        v4 --- v5
        v5 --- v1
    end
    subgraph SCAN_LINE_FILL_PATTERN
        s1["Y=1: FILL X 2 to 6"]
        s2["Y=2: FILL X 2 to 6.667"]
        s3["Y=3: FILL X 2 to 7.333"]
        s4["Y=4: FILL X 2 to 8"]
        s5["Y=5: FILL X 2 to 7.333"]
        s6["Y=6: FILL X 2 to 6.667"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The polygon outline (top block) is the static geometry. The scan-line fill pattern (bottom block) is the dynamic output of the algorithm — one filled span per row. Note that the widest span ($y = 4$, span $[2, 8]$) corresponds exactly to the **rightmost vertex** $P_3(8, 4)$, where the algorithm switches from edge $E_2$ to edge $E_3$.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q1.** Define **scan line polygon filling** algorithm. Mention any two advantages of using an Edge Table-based implementation over a naïve per-pixel inside/outside test.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Scan line polygon filling is an *image-space* rasterization technique that fills the interior of a polygon by sweeping a horizontal line — called the *scan line* — from $y = y_{min}$ to $y = y_{max}$. For each integer $y$, the algorithm computes the intersections of the scan line with the polygon edges, sorts the intersection $x$-values, and uses the **odd-even parity rule** to fill the spans between pairs of intersections.

**Two advantages over a naïve per-pixel test:**
1. **Time complexity is decoupled from resolution** — it depends on the number of *edges* and *scan lines*, not the number of *pixels*. A 4K monitor and a 1080p monitor take nearly the same time to fill the same polygon.
2. **Cache locality** — the algorithm fills entire horizontal spans with sequential memory writes, exploiting CPU/DMA burst transfers.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q2.** What is the **Active Edge List (AEL)**? Why is it sorted by $x$-coordinate at every step?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The AEL is a dynamic, sorted list of all polygon edges that are currently intersected by the active scan line $y = c$. An edge enters the AEL when the scan line first reaches its lower endpoint ($y = y_{min}$) and leaves the AEL immediately after the scan line passes its upper endpoint ($y = y_{max}$).

The AEL must be sorted by $x$ at every step because the **odd-even parity rule** requires filling pixels between *consecutive pairs* of $x$-values — i.e., $(x_1, x_2), (x_3, x_4), \ldots$. Without sorting, the parity check would pair the wrong intersections, producing garbled output (self-intersections, holes, over-fills).

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and provides an internal choice (OR). The standard sub-division is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The model answers below are written in full, step-by-step, to mimic the official KTU scoring key.

---

#### **Question A** *(14 Marks Total)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a) Explain the scan line polygon filling algorithm in detail with a neat flowchart. Discuss the role of the Edge Table and Active Edge List.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The scan line polygon filling algorithm rasterizes a polygon by processing it one horizontal row of pixels at a time. It is divided into **three logical phases**.

**Phase 1 — Build the Edge Table (ET):**
The ET is a bucket-sorted array indexed by the integer scan-line coordinate $y$. For each polygon edge, we:
- **Skip** horizontal edges ($y_1 = y_2$).
- **Normalize** the edge so that $y_{min}$ is the lower endpoint.
- Compute the **inverse slope** $1/m = (x_2 - x_1) / (y_2 - y_1)$.
- Insert the edge into $\text{ET}[y_{min}]$ with the record $(x_{y_{min}}, y_{max}, 1/m)$.

**Phase 2 — Drive the AEL scan-line by scan-line:**
For each integer $y$ from $y_{min}^{polygon}$ to $y_{max}^{polygon}$, in order:
- **Add** every edge from $\text{ET}[y]$ into the AEL.
- **Remove** every edge whose $y_{max} = y$ (it expires at the end of this scan line).
- **Sort** the AEL by the current $x$ value.
- **Fill** spans: pair the sorted $x$-values as $(x_1, x_2), (x_3, x_4), \ldots$ and colour every pixel in the inclusive range between each pair.

**Phase 3 — Update the AEL and increment $y$:**
After filling, advance the $x$ coordinate of every edge in the AEL by $1/m$. Increment $y$ by 1 and return to Phase 2.

**Role of ET:** The ET is a *static* data structure that holds every edge in the polygon, pre-sorted by the scan line on which it becomes active. It is built once and never modified — only read. This amortizes the cost of edge organization across the entire execution.

**Role of AEL:** The AEL is a *dynamic* data structure that holds only the edges crossing the *current* scan line. It is the working set — short, frequently updated, and sorted on every iteration. The AEL enables the algorithm to be $O(1)$ per pixel for filling.

**Flowchart:** Refer to §4.1 of these notes for the full Mermaid flowchart (5 marks are typically awarded for the correct flowchart in KTU valuation; the remaining 2 marks for the narrative description).

---

**(b) Given a polygon with vertices $(3,1), (10,1), (10,5), (14,5), (14,8), (3,8)$, perform scan line polygon filling. Show the Edge Table, the AEL evolution, and the fill spans for $y = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Classify edges:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &: (3,1) \to (10,1) \quad \text{Horizontal, SKIP} \\
E_2 &: (10,1) \to (10,5) \quad \text{Vertical, } x=10, \; 1/m = 0 \\
E_3 &: (10,5) \to (14,5) \quad \text{Horizontal, SKIP} \\
E_4 &: (14,5) \to (14,8) \quad \text{Vertical, } x=14, \; 1/m = 0 \\
E_5 &: (14,8) \to (3,8) \quad \text{Horizontal, SKIP} \\
E_6 &: (3,8) \to (3,1) \quad \text{Vertical, } x=3, \; 1/m = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Build the Edge Table:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Bucket } y & \textbf{Edge} & (x_{y_{min}},\; y_{max},\; 1/m) \\
\hline
1 & E_2 & (10,\; 5,\; 0) \\
1 & E_6 & (3,\; 8,\; 0) \\
\hline
5 & E_4 & (14,\; 8,\; 0) \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Step 3 — Drive the scan lines:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Y} & \textbf{AEL (sorted by X)} & \textbf{Removed} & \textbf{Added} & \textbf{Fill Span} \\
\hline
1 & [(3, 8, 0), (10, 5, 0)] & \text{None} & E_2, E_6 & [3, 10] \\
\hline
2 & [(3, 8, 0), (10, 5, 0)] & \text{None} & \text{None} & [3, 10] \\
\hline
3 & [(3, 8, 0), (10, 5, 0)] & \text{None} & \text{None} & [3, 10] \\
\hline
4 & [(3, 8, 0), (10, 5, 0)] & \text{None} & \text{None} & [3, 10] \\
\hline
5 & [(3, 8, 0), (14, 8, 0)] & E_2 & E_4 & [3, 14] \\
\hline
6 & [(3, 8, 0), (14, 8, 0)] & \text{None} & \text{None} & [3, 14] \\
\hline
7 & [(3, 8, 0), (14, 8, 0)] & \text{None} & \text{None} & [3, 14] \\
\hline
8 & \text{AEL} = \varnothing & E_6, E_4 & \text{None} & \text{None} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Final visual interpretation:** For $y = 1$ through $y = 4$, the polygon is a narrow rectangle from $x = 3$ to $x = 10$. At $y = 5$, the polygon steps out to $x = 14$ (a "stepped" or "L-shaped" polygon). The algorithm correctly produces the step transition at $y = 5$.

---

#### **Question B** *(Alternative Choice for the same 14 Marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**(a) Explain the various types of coherence exploited in scan line polygon filling. How do these coherence properties contribute to the algorithm's efficiency?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Coherence refers to the property that *similar* information is required to compute *adjacent* values, allowing shared computation across pixels. Scan line filling exploits four distinct types of coherence:

1. **Scan-line coherence:** Consecutive scan lines (e.g., $y$ and $y+1$) tend to have almost identical intersection patterns with the polygon. Only at *edge crossings* does the pattern change (a span starts, ends, or shifts). Without this property, we would have to re-test every pixel against the polygon — the naïve $O(P)$ per scan line approach, where $P$ is the pixel count.

2. **Edge coherence:** The $x$-coordinate of an edge intersection changes by a *constant* $1/m$ from one scan line to the next. This means we can update the intersection with a single addition, not a full line equation evaluation. This is the mathematical reason the AEL update step is $O(1)$ per edge per scan line.

3. **Span coherence:** Within a single scan line, the polygon interior is composed of *long horizontal runs* of pixels (spans). Once we know the left and right $x$-boundaries of a span, every pixel in between can be filled by a single hardware burst write (e.g., `memset`, GPU span-fill). Without this, we would test every pixel individually against the polygon interior.

4. **Area coherence:** All pixels in a connected region of a polygon share the same fill colour/pattern. This is exploited not by scan-line filling itself, but by its *cousin* algorithms — *seed-fill* and *boundary-fill* — which use a single seed pixel and flood outward, stopping at the boundary.

**Contribution to efficiency:** Together, these four coherence properties reduce the per-pixel cost of filling from $O(E)$ (testing against $E$ edges) to $O(1)$ amortized. For a polygon with $E$ edges and $P$ pixels, the complexity drops from $O(E \cdot P)$ (naïve) to $O(E \cdot Y + P)$ (scan line), where $Y$ is the height in scan lines. For high-resolution images, this is a 100–1000x speedup.

---

**(b) Construct the Edge Table for the polygon with vertices $(2,2), (10,2), (10,6), (6,10), (2,6)$ and demonstrate the AEL and fill spans for $y = 2, 3, 4, 5$.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Classify edges:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &: (2,2) \to (10,2) \quad \text{Horizontal, SKIP} \\
E_2 &: (10,2) \to (10,6) \quad \text{Vertical, } x=10, \; 1/m = 0 \\
E_3 &: (10,6) \to (6,10) \quad \text{Non-horiz., } y_{min}=6, \; y_{max}=10, \; 1/m = (6-10)/(10-6) = -1 \\
E_4 &: (6,10) \to (2,6) \quad \text{Non-horiz., } y_{min}=6, \; y_{max}=10, \; 1/m = (2-6)/(6-10) = 1 \\
E_5 &: (2,6) \to (2,2) \quad \text{Vertical, } x=2, \; 1/m = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Build the Edge Table:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Bucket } y & \textbf{Edge} & (x_{y_{min}},\; y_{max},\; 1/m) \\
\hline
2 & E_2 & (10,\; 6,\; 0) \\
2 & E_5 & (2,\; 6,\; 0) \\
\hline
6 & E_3 & (10,\; 10,\; -1) \\
6 & E_4 & (6,\; 10,\; 1) \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Step 3 — AEL evolution for $y = 2, 3, 4, 5$:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Y} & \textbf{AEL (sorted by X)} & \textbf{Fill Span} \\
\hline
2 & [(2, 6, 0), (10, 6, 0)] & [2, 10] \\
\hline
3 & [(2, 6, 0), (10, 6, 0)] & [2, 10] \\
\hline
4 & [(2, 6, 0), (10, 6, 0)] & [2, 10] \\
\hline
5 & [(2, 6, 0), (10, 6, 0)] & [2, 10] \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

For $y = 2$ through $y = 5$, the polygon behaves as a simple vertical rectangle with a constant fill span of $[2, 10]$. The non-vertical edges $E_3$ and $E_4$ only become active at $y = 6$, which is beyond the requested range.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in scan line polygon filling questions (typical deduction pattern observed in 2022–2024 answer scripts):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to skip horizontal edges** (–2 marks). A horizontal edge inserted into the ET will cause a divide-by-zero on $1/m$ computation.
> 2. **Wrong vertex-order normalization** (–1 mark). Always store the edge so that $y_{min}$ is the lower endpoint. Storing it "upside down" causes the edge to expire one scan line too early.
> 3. **Missing the vertex double-counting rule** (–2 marks). At a vertex where two edges meet the scan line, apply the *shorthand* rule — count as 1 intersection if the edges are on opposite sides of the scan line, 2 intersections if they are on the same side.
> 4. **Wrong fill span when AEL has odd number of edges** (–1 mark). If after sorting the AEL has an odd count (e.g., 3, 5, 7 intersections), it indicates a polygon error or missing edge. Do not silently pair the last one with a phantom boundary.
> 5. **Not updating $x$ by $1/m$** (–2 marks). The $x$ value must be advanced by $1/m$ *after* filling, *before* the next scan line. This is the most common code-level bug.
> 6. **Confusing scan line $y$ with vertex $y$** (–1 mark). All scan lines are integer $y$ values. The intersection $x$ may be fractional, but $y$ is always an integer.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density revision checklist (KTU 2024 module-internal weightage ≈ 15–20%):**
>
> - **Definition:** Scan line polygon filling is an *image-space*, *row-by-row* rasterization algorithm that uses the **odd-even parity rule** to determine pixel interior/outside status.
> - **Data structures used:** (1) **Edge Table (ET)** — static, bucket-sorted by $y_{min}$. (2) **Active Edge List (AEL)** — dynamic, sorted by current $x$, updated every scan line.
> - **Key formula:** $x_{i+1} = x_i + (1/m)$, where $1/m = (x_2 - x_1) / (y_2 - y_1)$.
> - **Edge record fields:** $(x_{y_{min}},\; y_{max},\; 1/m)$.
> - **Skip horizontal edges** during ET construction to avoid $1/m$ undefined (division by zero) and to avoid vertex double-counting.
> - **Per-step routine:** Add → Remove (if $y_{max} = y$) → Sort (by $x$) → Fill (pairwise spans) → Update $x$ by $1/m$ → Increment $y$.
> - **Termination:** Algorithm halts when AEL is empty AND ET has no more buckets to process.
> - **Coherence types:** Scan-line, edge, span, area — these are the *theoretical* reason the algorithm is fast.
> - **Shorthand rule:** At a vertex, count intersections as 1 or 2 based on whether the two edges lie on opposite or same sides of the scan line, respectively.
> - **Complexity:** $O(N_{edges} + N_{scanlines} \cdot N_{edges} \log N_{edges})$ worst case; in practice, $O(N_{scanlines} \cdot N_{edges})$ since AEL is small.
> - **Real-world analogues:** GPU rasterizers, laser CNC toolpaths, 3D-printer slicers, photo-printing RIPs.
> - **Common KTU questions:** "Build the ET and trace the AEL for a given polygon" (14 marks); "Explain the four coherence types" (7 marks); "Compare scan line with boundary fill" (3 marks).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 30-Second Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"**E**dge **T**able at **T**op, **A**ctive **E**dge **L**ist at **L**ive y, **S**ort by **x**, **F**ill **S**pans, **U**pdate **x**, **I**ncrement **y**"* → **ET-T, AEL-L, Sx, FS, Ux, Iy** — sounds like a chant; in practice, it is the exact 6-step loop body of the algorithm.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boundary Filling and Flood Filling. Two Dimensional Transformations - Translation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT416 - GRAPHICS AND MULTIMEDIA PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boundary Filling and Flood Filling. Two Dimensional Transformations - Translation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Boundary Fill Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Boundary Fill):**
> The **Boundary Fill Algorithm** is a region-filling technique in computer graphics that starts from an interior point (called the *seed point* or *interior point*) and recursively fills the connected area outward, **terminating when it encounters pixels of the specified boundary color**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are standing in the middle of a closed room with white tiled floor and a dark black border near the walls. You start painting the floor red from where you stand. You keep painting every adjacent white tile, but the moment you reach a black tile (the boundary), you stop in that direction. This is exactly how Boundary Fill works — it spreads from a seed until it hits the predefined wall.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Boundary Fill is a *closed-curve* filler. It requires a **single, uniform boundary color** that surrounds the region. If the boundary is broken or has gaps, the fill will "leak" out of the region.

---

### 1.2 Flood Fill Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Flood Fill):**
> The **Flood Fill Algorithm** is a region-filling algorithm that recolors all pixels of a connected region which share the **same original color** as the seed point, replacing them with a new fill color. It does **not** require a boundary — it relies only on color matching.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Picture spilling a bucket of blue paint on a white canvas. The blue spreads across all the *connected* white area but stops wherever it meets a different color (say, a black line). Unlike Boundary Fill, the limit here is set by a *color change*, not a pre-marked wall. This is the algorithm behind the **"Paint Bucket" tool** in MS Paint, Photoshop, and GIMP.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Flood Fill is a *color-based* filler. It is used in image editing, puzzle games (Minesweeper, Go), and connected-component labeling.

---

### 1.3 Two-Dimensional Translation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2D Translation):**
> **Translation** is a rigid-body geometric transformation that repositions every point $(x, y)$ of an object by a constant displacement $(t_x, t_y)$ along the x-axis and y-axis respectively, **without** altering the size, shape, or orientation of the object.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine pushing a book across a flat table by **5 cm to the right** and **3 cm up**. The book itself hasn't changed — it hasn't rotated, hasn't grown, hasn't shrunk — it has just *moved*. That rigid sliding is **Translation**. Every point on the book (every corner, every pixel) undergoes the same shift.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Translation is the *only* basic 2D transformation that is an **affine transformation but NOT a linear transformation** (because it cannot be expressed as matrix multiplication without using homogeneous coordinates).

---

### 1.4 4-Connected vs 8-Connected Pixel Adjacency

| Property | 4-Connected | 8-Connected |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Neighbors considered | North, South, East, West | All 4 above + Diagonals (NE, NW, SE, SW) |
| Speed | Faster | Slower |
| Risk of leaks | Higher (can leak through diagonal gaps) | Lower (more thorough) |
| KTU Preferred for | Boundary Fill (with strong boundary) | Flood Fill (general) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Boundary Fill — Algorithmic Logic Steps

1. **Accept** the seed pixel coordinates $(x, y)$ and the fill color $F_c$.
2. **Accept** the boundary color $B_c$ (e.g., WHITE for background, BLACK for outline).
3. **Get the current color** of the seed pixel using `getpixel(x, y)`.
4. **Check the color test condition:** The current pixel color must be:
   - **Not equal** to the boundary color $B_c$
   - **Not equal** to the fill color $F_c$
5. If both conditions are satisfied → **set the pixel** to the fill color using `putpixel(x, y, F_c)`.
6. **Recursively call** the algorithm for the 4 (or 8) neighbors of the current pixel.
7. **Termination:** The recursion unwinds naturally when all reachable interior pixels have been filled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Boundary Fill **fails** if the seed point is placed on the boundary, or outside the closed region, or if the region has a boundary pixel that shares the fill color.

### 2.2 Flood Fill — Algorithmic Logic Steps

1. **Accept** the seed pixel coordinates $(x, y)$, the target color $T_c$ (the color to be replaced), and the replacement color $R_c$.
2. **Get the current color** at $(x, y)$.
3. **Check the condition:** Current color must **equal** the target color $T_c$.
4. If true → **set the pixel** to the replacement color $R_c$.
5. **Recursively call** for the 4 (or 8) neighbors.
6. **Termination:** Stops when no connected pixel matches the target color.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Flood Fill safer than Boundary Fill in real applications?**
> It does not require a closed boundary. It only needs a *connected* region of the same color. This makes it ideal for editing real-world raster images where the boundary may be anti-aliased or fuzzy.

### 2.3 2D Translation — Operational Concept

Translation is mathematically described by:

$$P'(x', y') = P(x, y) + T(t_x, t_y)$$

Where:
- $P(x, y)$ is the **original point**
- $P'(x', y')$ is the **translated point**
- $T(t_x, t_y)$ is the **translation vector**
- $t_x$ = displacement along the X-axis
- $t_y$ = displacement along the Y-axis

**Component form:**

$$x' = x + t_x$$

$$y' = y + t_y$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For an object with $n$ vertices, each vertex is translated independently. There is **no interaction** between vertices during translation — making it computationally the *cheapest* of all 2D transformations: $O(n)$ time for $n$ points.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the complete formula reference for Module 2 — Boundary/Flood Fill + Translation. Memorize it for KTU ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation Used |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Translation (point form) | $x' = x + t_x, \quad y' = y + t_y$ | $(t_x, t_y)$ = translation vector |
| 2 | Translation (vector form) | $P' = P + T$ | $P, P' \in \mathbb{R}^2, \; T \in \mathbb{R}^2$ |
| 3 | Homogeneous Translation Matrix | $T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $3 \times 3$ matrix |
| 4 | Inverse Translation | $t_x' = -t_x, \quad t_y' = -t_y$ | $T^{-1}$ undoes the move |
| 5 | Boundary Fill Condition | $C_{current} \neq B_c \; \land \; C_{current} \neq F_c$ | $B_c$=boundary color, $F_c$=fill color |
| 6 | Flood Fill Condition | $C_{current} = T_c$ | $T_c$=target color to replace |
| 7 | 4-Neighbor Set (4-Connected) | $\{(x\!+\!1,y), (x\!-\!1,y), (x,y\!+\!1), (x,y\!-\!1)\}$ | Cardinal directions |
| 8 | 8-Neighbor Set (8-Connected) | 4-Set $\cup$ $\{(x\!+\!1,y\!+\!1), (x\!+\!1,y\!-\!1), (x\!-\!1,y\!+\!1), (x\!-\!1,y\!-\!1)\}$ | Cardinal + Diagonal |
| 9 | Transformed Point (Homogeneous) | $P' = T \cdot P$ | $P = [x, y, 1]^{T}$, $P' = [x', y', 1]^{T}$ |
| 10 | Composite Translation (Sequential) | $T_{net} = T_2 \cdot T_1$ | $t_{x,net} = t_{x1} + t_{x2}$ |

> **Real-world Engineering Utility:**
> - **Boundary/Flood Fill** → GIS mapping, medical image segmentation (tumor boundary detection), PCB design verification, paint software, Minesweeper game logic.
> - **2D Translation** → CAD systems, robotics path planning (end-effector offset), video game sprite movement, UI element repositioning in responsive web design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of 2D Translation in Homogeneous Coordinates

**Step 1: The Problem with Standard 2D Vectors**
In a regular 2D Cartesian plane, a point is represented as the column vector:

$$P = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$$

To translate $P$ by $(t_x, t_y)$, we want:

$$P' = P + T = \begin{bmatrix} x + t_x \\ y + t_y \end{bmatrix}$$

This is a *vector addition* — which is fine, **but it cannot be expressed as a matrix multiplication** $P' = M \cdot P$ using a $2 \times 2$ matrix. This is the mathematical motivation for **homogeneous coordinates**.

**Step 2: Augmenting to 3D with Homogeneous Coordinates**
We embed the 2D point into 3D by appending a third coordinate equal to 1:

$$P_h = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Now we construct a $3 \times 3$ translation matrix $T$:

$$T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Performing the Multiplication**
We compute $P'_h = T \cdot P_h$:

$$P'_h = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Breaking this into explicit row-by-column operations:

$$P'_h = \begin{bmatrix} (1)(x) + (0)(y) + (t_x)(1) \\ (0)(x) + (1)(y) + (t_y)(1) \\ (0)(x) + (0)(y) + (1)(1) \end{bmatrix}$$

Simplifying each component:

$$P'_h = \begin{bmatrix} x + t_x \\ y + t_y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Verifying the Result**
The third component is preserved as 1, confirming a valid homogeneous representation. The first two components are:

$$x' = x + t_x \quad \text{and} \quad y' = y + t_y$$

Which exactly matches the original definition of translation. ∎

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Translating a Triangle

**Problem (KTU ESE Style):**
A triangle has vertices $A(2, 1)$, $B(5, 1)$, $C(3.5, 4)$. Apply a translation of $t_x = 4$ and $t_y = 3$. Find the new coordinates and draw both triangles.

**Step 1: Identify the Translation Vector**

$$T = (t_x, t_y) = (4, 3)$$

**Step 2: Construct the Homogeneous Translation Matrix**

$$T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Transformation to Each Vertex**

**For Vertex A(2, 1):**

$$A' = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (1)(2) + (0)(1) + (4)(1) \\ (0)(2) + (1)(1) + (3)(1) \\ (0)(2) + (0)(1) + (1)(1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

So $A' = (6, 4)$.

**For Vertex B(5, 1):**

$$B' = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (1)(5) + (0)(1) + (4)(1) \\ (0)(5) + (1)(1) + (3)(1) \\ (0)(5) + (0)(1) + (1)(1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 9 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

So $B' = (9, 4)$.

**For Vertex C(3.5, 4):**

$$C' = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 3.5 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (1)(3.5) + (0)(4) + (4)(1) \\ (0)(3.5) + (1)(4) + (3)(1) \\ (0)(3.5) + (0)(4) + (1)(1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7.5 \\ 7 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

So $C' = (7.5, 7)$.

**Step 4: Final Summary of Translated Vertices**

| Vertex | Original | Translated |
|--------|----------|------------|
| A | (2, 1) | (6, 4) |
| B | (5, 1) | (9, 4) |
| C | (3.5, 4) | (7.5, 7) |

---

### 3.3 Composite Translation — Analytical Proof

**Problem:** Show that two sequential translations $(t_{x1}, t_{y1})$ followed by $(t_{x2}, t_{y2})$ are equivalent to a single translation by $(t_{x1}+t_{x2}, t_{y1}+t_{y2})$.

**Step 1: Write the Two Individual Translation Matrices**

$$T_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_{x1} \\ 0 & 1 & t_{y1} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad T_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_{x2} \\ 0 & 1 & t_{y2} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Composite $T_2 \cdot T_1$**

$$T_{composite} = T_2 \cdot T_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_{x2} \\ 0 & 1 & t_{y2} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_{x1} \\ 0 & 1 & t_{y1} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Expanding element by element:

- Row 1, Col 1: $(1)(1) + (0)(0) + (t_{x2})(0) = 1$
- Row 1, Col 2: $(1)(0) + (0)(1) + (t_{x2})(0) = 0$
- Row 1, Col 3: $(1)(t_{x1}) + (0)(t_{y1}) + (t_{x2})(1) = t_{x1} + t_{x2}$
- Row 2, Col 1: $(0)(1) + (1)(0) + (t_{y2})(0) = 0$
- Row 2, Col 2: $(0)(0) + (1)(1) + (t_{y2})(0) = 1$
- Row 2, Col 3: $(0)(t_{x1}) + (1)(t_{y1}) + (t_{y2})(1) = t_{y1} + t_{y2}$
- Row 3, Col 1: $(0)(1) + (0)(0) + (1)(0) = 0$
- Row 3, Col 2: $(0)(0) + (0)(1) + (1)(0) = 0$
- Row 3, Col 3: $(0)(t_{x1}) + (0)(t_{y1}) + (1)(1) = 1$

**Step 3: Write the Resulting Matrix**

$$T_{composite} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_{x1} + t_{x2} \\ 0 & 1 & t_{y1} + t_{y2} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is exactly a single translation matrix with $t_x = t_{x1} + t_{x2}$ and $t_y = t_{y1} + t_{y2}$. ∎

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Translations are **commutative** — the order of multiplication does not change the final result. $T_1 \cdot T_2 = T_2 \cdot T_1$ (because the off-diagonal blocks commute and the lower row is invariant).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Boundary Fill Algorithm — Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START Boundary Fill]) --> inputA[/Read seed x, y, fill color F_c, boundary color B_c/]
    inputA --> checkA{Current color NOT equal to B_c AND NOT equal to F_c?}
    checkA -- Yes --> paintA[Set pixel x, y to F_c]
    paintA --> recurseA[Recurse on 4 neighbors: x+1 y, x-1 y, x y+1, x y-1]
    recurseA --> checkA
    checkA -- No --> skipA[Skip / Return]
    skipA --> endA([END])
```

### 4.2 Flood Fill Algorithm — Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([START Flood Fill]) --> inputB[/Read seed x, y, target color T_c, replacement R_c/]
    inputB --> checkB{Current color EQUAL to T_c?}
    checkB -- Yes --> paintB[Set pixel x, y to R_c]
    paintB --> recurseB[Recurse on 4 or 8 neighbors]
    recurseB --> checkB
    checkB -- No --> skipB[Skip / Return]
    skipB --> endB([END])
```

### 4.3 Boundary Fill vs Flood Fill — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BF["BOUNDARY FILL"]
        BF1[Seed Point] --> BF2{Check: Pixel != Boundary color}
        BF2 -- Yes --> BF3[Apply Fill Color]
        BF2 -- No --> BF4[STOP at wall]
    end
    subgraph FF["FLOOD FILL"]
        FF1[Seed Point] --> FF2{Check: Pixel == Target color}
        FF2 -- Yes --> FF3[Apply Replacement Color]
        FF2 -- No --> FF4[STOP at different color]
    end
    BF3 --> BF5[Recurse 4-Neighbors]
    FF3 --> FF5[Recurse 4 or 8-Neighbors]
    BF5 -.Requires closed boundary.-> BFnote[NOTE: Boundary-based]
    FF5 -.No boundary needed.-> FFnote[NOTE: Color-based]
```

### 4.4 2D Translation — Geometric Effect Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN["INPUT"]
        Pt[Original Point P at x, y]
        TV[Translation Vector T at t_x, t_y]
    end
    subgraph PROC["HOMOGENEOUS OPERATION"]
        H1[Embed P as x, y, 1]
        H2[Build 3x3 Matrix with t_x, t_y]
        H3[Matrix Multiply T dot P_h]
    end
    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT"]
        NewPt[Translated Point P prime at x prime, y prime]
    end
    Pt --> H1
    TV --> H2
    H1 --> H3
    H2 --> H3
    H3 --> NewPt
```

### 4.5 4-Connected vs 8-Connected Pixel Neighborhood

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NEIGH["PIXEL ADJACENCY FROM CENTER X, Y"]
        direction TB
        N1["X-1, Y+1"]:::diag
        N2["X, Y+1"]:::card
        N3["X+1, Y+1"]:::diag
        N4["X-1, Y"]:::card
        CX["CENTER X, Y"]:::center
        N5["X+1, Y"]:::card
        N6["X-1, Y-1"]:::diag
        N7["X, Y-1"]:::card
        N8["X+1, Y-1"]:::diag
    end
    classDef card fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef diag fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#663300,color:#000
    classDef center fill:#90ee90,stroke:#003300,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Blue cells** = 4-connected neighbors (cardinal directions only) → used in 4-connected Boundary/Flood Fill.
- **Orange cells** = Diagonal neighbors (additionally used in 8-connected Flood Fill).
- **Green cell** = the current/seed pixel.

### 4.6 Translation of a Triangle — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["A(2,1)"] --> MAT["3x3 Translation Matrix T with t_x=4, t_y=3"]
    B1["B(5,1)"] --> MAT
    C1["C(3.5,4)"] --> MAT
    MAT --> A2["A prime(6,4)"]
    MAT --> B2["B prime(9,4)"]
    MAT --> C2["C prime(7.5,7)"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** Each of the three original vertices is independently fed into the same $3 \times 3$ translation matrix. The output yields three new translated vertices. The triangle retains its shape — only its position shifts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Boundary Fill and Flood Fill algorithms.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | Boundary Fill | Flood Fill |
|-----------|---------------|------------|
| Basis of termination | Boundary color $B_c$ | Original color $T_c$ |
| Requires closed boundary? | **Yes** (mandatory) | **No** |
| Seed placement | Must be strictly *inside* the region | Anywhere on the target color |
| Color matching | Pixel must **not** match boundary/fill | Pixel must **match** target color |
| Typical application | Logo coloring, region tagging | Image editing, paint-bucket tool |
| Failure mode | Leaks through broken boundary | Stops at color discontinuity |

> *Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for each valid point, maximum 3.*

---

**Q2. Why do we use homogeneous coordinates for 2D translation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Uniform matrix representation:** Homogeneous coordinates allow translation to be expressed as a $3 \times 3$ matrix multiplication, the same format as scaling, rotation, and shearing. *(1 mark)*
2. **Enables composition:** Multiple transformations (translate → rotate → scale) can be combined via a single composite matrix $M = T \cdot R \cdot S$. *(1 mark)*
3. **GPU/Hardware compatibility:** Modern graphics hardware (GPUs, OpenGL, DirectX) is fundamentally built around $4 \times 4$ homogeneous matrices. Using them keeps the pipeline consistent. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the Boundary Fill algorithm with a 4-connected approach. State its condition for filling a pixel. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A triangle has vertices $P(1, 2)$, $Q(4, 2)$, $R(2.5, 5)$. Apply a 2D translation with $t_x = 5$, $t_y = 4$. Compute the new coordinates using the homogeneous transformation matrix. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Boundary Fill Algorithm — 4-Connected Approach**

The 4-connected Boundary Fill starts from a seed point inside a closed region and fills the region by checking the 4 cardinal neighbors (up, down, left, right) of each pixel.

**Condition for Filling a Pixel:**
A pixel at $(x, y)$ is filled if and only if its current color $C(x, y)$ satisfies:

$$C(x, y) \neq B_c \quad \text{AND} \quad C(x, y) \neq F_c$$

Where $B_c$ = boundary color, $F_c$ = fill color.

**Algorithm (Pseudo-logic):**

```
Procedure BoundaryFill4(x, y, F_c, B_c):
    current = getpixel(x, y)
    if (current != B_c) AND (current != F_c) then
        putpixel(x, y, F_c)
        BoundaryFill4(x+1, y, F_c, B_c)   // East
        BoundaryFill4(x-1, y, F_c, B_c)   // West
        BoundaryFill4(x, y+1, F_c, B_c)   // South
        BoundaryFill4(x, y-1, F_c, B_c)   // North
    end if
End Procedure
```

**Explanation of Logic Steps:**
1. The function `getpixel(x, y)` retrieves the current color from the framebuffer. *(1 mark)*
2. The double condition (not boundary, not already fill) prevents the algorithm from spilling outside or from infinite recursion. *(2 marks)*
3. Four recursive calls expand the fill in 4 cardinal directions, ensuring complete coverage of the connected interior. *(2 marks)*
4. Base case: recursion unwinds naturally when a pixel is either on the boundary or already filled. *(1 mark)*
5. The algorithm is **recursive in nature** and has time complexity $O(N)$ for a region of $N$ pixels. *(1 mark)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:** Vertices $P(1, 2)$, $Q(4, 2)$, $R(2.5, 5)$. Translation: $t_x = 5, t_y = 4$.

**Step 1: Construct the Homogeneous Translation Matrix** *(2 marks)*

$$T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 5 \\ 0 & 1 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Convert each vertex to homogeneous form** *(1 mark)*

$$P = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad Q = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 \\ 5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Apply Transformation to P** *(1 mark)*

$$P' = T \cdot P = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 5 \\ 0 & 1 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 + 0 + 5 \\ 0 + 2 + 4 \\ 0 + 0 + 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 6 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

So $P' = (6, 6)$.

**Step 4: Apply Transformation to Q** *(1 mark)*

$$Q' = T \cdot Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 5 \\ 0 & 1 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 + 0 + 5 \\ 0 + 2 + 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 9 \\ 6 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

So $Q' = (9, 6)$.

**Step 5: Apply Transformation to R** *(1 mark)*

$$R' = T \cdot R = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 5 \\ 0 & 1 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 \\ 5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 + 0 + 5 \\ 0 + 5 + 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7.5 \\ 9 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

So $R' = (7.5, 9)$.

**Step 6: Final Answer Tabulation** *(1 mark)*

| Vertex | Original Coordinates | Translated Coordinates |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| P | (1, 2) | (6, 6) |
| Q | (4, 2) | (9, 6) |
| R | (2.5, 5) | (7.5, 9) |

The shape, size, and orientation of the triangle remain unchanged — only its position has shifted by 5 units right and 4 units up.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the Flood Fill algorithm using both 4-connected and 8-connected approaches. Compare their advantages. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A square has vertices $A(0, 0)$, $B(4, 0)$, $C(4, 4)$, $D(0, 4)$. Apply the following sequence of translations: first $t_1 = (2, 3)$, then $t_2 = (-1, 5)$. Find the final position of all four vertices and the net translation vector. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Flood Fill Algorithm — Conceptual Explanation**

The Flood Fill algorithm replaces all connected pixels of a *target color* $T_c$ with a *replacement color* $R_c$, starting from a seed point.

**Flood Fill Condition:**
A pixel at $(x, y)$ is filled if and only if:

$$C(x, y) = T_c$$

*(Note: No boundary check is needed — only color matching.)*

**4-Connected Approach:**

```
Procedure FloodFill4(x, y, T_c, R_c):
    if getpixel(x, y) == T_c then
        putpixel(x, y, R_c)
        FloodFill4(x+1, y, T_c, R_c)
        FloodFill4(x-1, y, T_c, R_c)
        FloodFill4(x, y+1, T_c, R_c)
        FloodFill4(x, y-1, T_c, R_c)
    end if
End Procedure
```

**8-Connected Approach:**

```
Procedure FloodFill8(x, y, T_c, R_c):
    if getpixel(x, y) == T_c then
        putpixel(x, y, R_c)
        FloodFill8(x+1, y, T_c, R_c)
        FloodFill8(x-1, y, T_c, R_c)
        FloodFill8(x, y+1, T_c, R_c)
        FloodFill8(x, y-1, T_c, R_c)
        FloodFill8(x+1, y+1, T_c, R_c)
        FloodFill8(x+1, y-1, T_c, R_c)
        FloodFill8(x-1, y+1, T_c, R_c)
        FloodFill8(x-1, y-1, T_c, R_c)
    end if
End Procedure
```

**Comparison Table — Advantages:**

| Aspect | 4-Connected | 8-Connected |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| Number of recursive calls per pixel | 4 | 8 |
| Speed | Faster | Slower (more calls) |
| Diagonal connectivity | Ignored | Included |
| Use case | Images with thick strokes | Thin or diagonal regions |
| Risk of "leak" | High at thin gaps | Low |
| Recommended for KTU | Standard default | When diagonal fill is needed |

*(2 marks for algorithm explanation, 2 marks for code/recursion structure, 2 marks for comparison table, 1 mark for application example.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:** Square with $A(0,0)$, $B(4,0)$, $C(4,4)$, $D(0,4)$. Sequential translations: $t_1 = (2, 3)$, $t_2 = (-1, 5)$.

**Step 1: Compute Net Translation Vector** *(2 marks)*

By the additive property of translations (proved in Section 3.3):

$$t_{net} = t_1 + t_2 = (2 + (-1), \; 3 + 5) = (1, 8)$$

**Step 2: Construct Composite Translation Matrix** *(2 marks)*

$$T_{net} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 8 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Apply to Each Vertex** *(2 marks)*

Using $x' = x + 1, \; y' = y + 8$:

| Vertex | Original | Final |
|--------|----------|-------|
| A | (0, 0) | (1, 8) |
| B | (4, 0) | (5, 8) |
| C | (4, 4) | (5, 12) |
| D | (0, 4) | (1, 12) |

**Step 4: Verify by Sequential Application** *(1 mark)*

- After $t_1 = (2, 3)$: $A' = (2, 3)$, $B' = (6, 3)$, $C' = (6, 7)$, $D' = (2, 7)$.
- After $t_2 = (-1, 5)$: $A'' = (1, 8)$, $B'' = (5, 8)$, $C'' = (5, 12)$, $D'' = (1, 12)$. ✓

**Net Translation Vector:** $\boxed{(1, 8)}$ — the square is moved 1 unit right and 8 units up.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the homogeneous coordinate:** Students often write $P' = T \cdot P$ using a $2 \times 2$ matrix — this is **mathematically invalid** and costs 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Mixing up Boundary and Flood Fill conditions:** Boundary Fill requires `current != boundary`, Flood Fill requires `current == target`. Examiners *deliberately* test this confusion.
> 3. **Skipping the third row in the translation matrix:** The bottom row of the homogeneous matrix must always be `[0 0 1]`. Omitting it loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Applying translation to a single vertex instead of all:** In composite object problems, every vertex must be transformed.
> 5. **Not stating "translation preserves shape, size, and orientation"** in 2-mark definition questions — examiners expect this explicit statement.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this before the exam!)**

- ✅ **Boundary Fill:** Requires a *closed boundary of uniform color*. Fills interior outward. Condition: $C \neq B_c \land C \neq F_c$.
- ✅ **Flood Fill:** Requires a *connected region of the same target color*. Fills color-matched area. Condition: $C = T_c$.
- ✅ **4-Connected:** 4 neighbors (N, S, E, W). Faster, but can leak diagonally.
- ✅ **8-Connected:** 8 neighbors (4 cardinal + 4 diagonal). Slower, more thorough.
- ✅ **Translation Formula:** $x' = x + t_x, \quad y' = y + t_y$.
- ✅ **Homogeneous Translation Matrix:** $3 \times 3$ matrix with $t_x$ and $t_y$ in the top-right column.
- ✅ **Inverse Translation:** $t_x' = -t_x, \; t_y' = -t_y$.
- ✅ **Composite Translation:** Net $t_x = \sum t_{xi}$, Net $t_y = \sum t_{yi}$. Translations are **commutative**.
- ✅ **Translation is rigid:** Distance between any two points is preserved.
- ✅ **Boundary Fill fails** if the boundary has gaps; **Flood Fill fails** if the region is not connected.
- ✅ **Practical use:** Boundary Fill → vector-style fill; Flood Fill → raster/paint-bucket style fill.
- ✅ **GPU Reality:** Real graphics pipelines use $4 \times 4$ matrices (for 3D); the $3 \times 3$ version is a 2D subset.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT416 - GRAPHICS AND MULTIMEDIA PRINCIPLES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Rotation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Rotation** in Computer Graphics (CG) is a *rigid-body geometric transformation* that repositions every point $(x, y)$ of an object along a circular arc around a fixed reference point (the **pivot** or **center of rotation**) by a specified angle $\theta$, while preserving the shape, size, and inter-point distances of the object. The mathematical operator for 2D rotation is the orthogonal matrix: $$R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Rotation is treated as an *isometric (rigid-body) transformation*. The object retains its geometry; only its *orientation* in the coordinate space is altered. This is a direct consequence of the property $\det(R(\theta)) = +1$ (preserves area and handedness). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the **second hand of a wall clock**. - The *center of the clock face* is the **pivot point** (origin of rotation). - Every point on the hand traces a perfect circle. - The angle the hand moves in a given time is $\theta$. - The length of the hand (distance from center to tip) is the *radial distance $r$*, which never changes. In computer graphics, the same principle is applied to a *digital object*. The object is "frozen" as a set of vertices $(x_i, y_i)$. Each vertex is moved along its own invisible circular track. Since every vertex rotates by the *same* angle $\theta$ around the *same* pivot, the object rotates as a single, rigid body — no stretching, no distortion. ### 1.3 Convention: Positive vs. Negative Rotation > [!NOTE] > **Right-Hand Rule / KTU Standard Convention:** > - **Positive angle ($+\theta$):** Counter-Clockwise (CCW) rotation. Visualize: the point moves from the positive X-axis toward the positive Y-axis. > - **Negative angle ($-\theta$):** Clockwise (CW) rotation. > - This convention is fixed in KTU examination problems unless explicitly stated otherwise. ### 1.4 Physical / Mathematical Constants and Metrics - **$\theta$ (Theta):** Rotation angle, measured in **radians** (for computation) or **degrees** (for problem statements). Conversion: $1^\circ = \pi/180$ radians $\approx 0.01745$ rad. - **Pivot Point $(x_r, y_r)$:** The reference coordinate about which rotation occurs. The default pivot in canonical derivations is the **origin $(0, 0)$**. - **Rotation is an Orthogonal Transform:** $R^{-1}(\theta) = R^T(\theta) = R(-\theta)$. The inverse is just a rotation by $-\theta$. - **$\det(R(\theta)) = \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$**, confirming area-preservation. ### 1.5 Categories of Rotation (KTU Module 2 Scope) 1. **2D Rotation about the Origin** 2. **2D Rotation about an Arbitrary Pivot $(x_r, y_r)$** 3. **3D Rotation about Coordinate Axes ($X$, $Y$, $Z$)** 4. **General 3D Rotation about an Arbitrary Axis (Rodrigues' Formula)** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — How Rotation Works Point-by-Point Rotation is a *point-mapping function*. For every input point $P$, there is exactly one output point $P'$. **Step-by-step logic of a 2D rotation about the origin:** - **Step 1 — Polar Decomposition:** Any point $P(x, y)$ can be expressed in polar form as $(r\cos\alpha, r\sin\alpha)$, where $r$ is its distance from the origin and $\alpha$ is its angle from the positive X-axis. - **Step 2 — Angular Displacement:** Apply a rotation of $\theta$. The new angle becomes $(\alpha + \theta)$. - **Step 3 — Reconstruct Cartesian:** $P'(r\cos(\alpha + \theta), r\sin(\alpha + \theta))$. - **Step 4 — Apply Trig Identities:** Expand using $\cos(\alpha + \theta) = \cos\alpha\cos\theta - \sin\alpha\sin\theta$ and $\sin(\alpha + \theta) = \sin\alpha\cos\theta + \cos\alpha\sin\theta$. - **Step 5 — Matrix Form:** This collapses cleanly into a $2\times 2$ matrix multiplication. ### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step - *Why polar form?* Rotation naturally occurs in polar space (angular change around a center). Cartesian coordinates make the algebra look linear, but conceptually it is a *circular motion*. - *Why trig identities?* They are the algebraic bridge that converts "angle addition" into a linear combination of $x$ and $y$, which is what a matrix requires. - *Why orthogonal matrix?* It guarantees **no distortion** — the most important property in graphics where shapes must be visually preserved. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **TABLE 1 — Master Rotation Formulas (KTU 2024 Scheme)** — use `\vert` instead of $\vert$ to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Transformation Type | Matrix Representation | Resulting Coordinates | |---|---------------------|-----------------------|------------------------| | 1 | 2D Rotation about Origin (CCW) | $\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta\\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}$ | $x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta$
$y' = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$ | | 2 | 2D Rotation about Pivot $(x_r, y_r)$ | $T(x_r, y_r) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_r, -y_r)$ | $x' = x_r + (x - x_r)\cos\theta - (y - y_r)\sin\theta$
$y' = y_r + (x - x_r)\sin\theta + (y - y_r)\cos\theta$ | | 3 | 3D Rotation about X-axis | $\begin{bmatrix}1 & 0 & 0\\ 0 & \cos\theta & -\sin\theta\\ 0 & \sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}$ | $x' = x$
$y' = y\cos\theta - z\sin\theta$
$z' = y\sin\theta + z\cos\theta$ | | 4 | 3D Rotation about Y-axis | $\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta\\ 0 & 1 & 0\\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}$ | $x' = x\cos\theta + z\sin\theta$
$y' = y$
$z' = -x\sin\theta + z\cos\theta$ | | 5 | 3D Rotation about Z-axis | $\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0\\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0\\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$ | $x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta$
$y' = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$
$z' = z$ | | 6 | Homogeneous 2D Rotation | $\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0\\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0\\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$ | Enables composition with translation/scaling | | 7 | Inverse 2D Rotation | $R(-\theta) = R^T(\theta)$ | Used for un-rotation, view transformations | | 8 | General 3D Axis (Rodrigues) | $R = \cos\theta \cdot I + (1-\cos\theta)\hat{u}\hat{u}^T + \sin\theta \cdot [\hat{u}]_\times$ | Rotation by $\theta$ about unit vector $\hat{u}$ | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **CAD/CAM Software (AutoCAD, SolidWorks):** Rotating components for assembly analysis. - **Video Games & VR:** Camera rotation, character turning, weapon swinging — all are real-time rotation matrices applied to vertex buffers. - **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT):** Rotating volumetric data to align anatomical slices. - **Robotics:** Forward kinematics — joint angles in a robotic arm correspond to rotation matrices. - **Aerospace:** Euler-angle and quaternion-based attitude control of aircraft/satellites. - **Augmented Reality (AR):** Marker-based tracking computes rotation to overlay virtual objects in the real world. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — 2D Rotation About the Origin **Given:** A point $P(x, y)$ in the 2D plane. **Goal:** Derive the formula for the rotated point $P'(x', y')$ after a CCW rotation by angle $\theta$ about the origin. **Step 1:** Express $P$ in polar coordinates. $$x = r\cos\alpha, \quad y = r\sin\alpha$$ *Logic:* Any point in the plane can be uniquely described by its distance $r$ from origin and the angle $\alpha$ it makes with the positive X-axis. **Step 2:** Apply the rotation. The new angle becomes $\alpha + \theta$, while $r$ is unchanged. $$x' = r\cos(\alpha + \theta), \quad y' = r\sin(\alpha + \theta)$$ *Logic:* Rotation preserves distance from the center; only the angular coordinate shifts by $+\theta$. **Step 3:** Expand using the angle addition formulas. $$\cos(\alpha + \theta) = \cos\alpha\cos\theta - \sin\alpha\sin\theta$$ $$\sin(\alpha + \theta) = \sin\alpha\cos\theta + \cos\alpha\sin\theta$$ *Logic:* These are the standard trigonometric sum identities — the algebraic bridge from "angle addition" to "linear combination". **Step 4:** Substitute into $x'$ and $y'$. $$x' = r(\cos\alpha\cos\theta - \sin\alpha\sin\theta)$$ $$y' = r(\sin\alpha\cos\theta + \cos\alpha\sin\theta)$$ **Step 5:** Substitute $x = r\cos\alpha$ and $y = r\sin\alpha$ back into the equations. $$x' = (r\cos\alpha)\cos\theta - (r\sin\alpha)\sin\theta = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta$$ $$y' = (r\sin\alpha)\cos\theta + (r\cos\alpha)\sin\theta = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$$ **Step 6:** Write the result in matrix form. $$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$$ $\blacksquare$ **Derivation Complete.** ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — 2D Rotation About an Arbitrary Pivot Point $(x_r, y_r)$ **Given:** Point $P(x, y)$ and an arbitrary pivot $P_r(x_r, y_r)$. **Goal:** Derive the direct coordinate formula for $P'$ after rotation by $\theta$ about $P_r$. **The 3-Stage Strategy:** 1. Translate the pivot to the origin. 2. Rotate about the origin. 3. Translate back. **Step 1 (Translate by $(-x_r, -y_r)$):** Move the pivot to the origin by subtracting $(x_r, y_r)$ from the point. $$x_1 = x - x_r, \quad y_1 = y - y_r$$ **Step 2 (Rotate by $\theta$ about origin):** Apply the rotation matrix derived in 3.1. $$x_2 = x_1\cos\theta - y_1\sin\theta$$ $$y_2 = x_1\sin\theta + y_1\cos\theta$$ **Step 3 (Translate back by $(x_r, y_r)$):** Restore the original pivot position. $$x' = x_2 + x_r, \quad y' = y_2 + y_r$$ **Step 4 — Combine and simplify (substitute Step 1 into Step 2):** $$x' = (x - x_r)\cos\theta - (y - y_r)\sin\theta + x_r$$ $$y' = (x - x_r)\sin\theta + (y - y_r)\cos\theta + y_r$$ **Step 5 — Composite Matrix Form (multiplied right-to-left: first translate, then rotate, then translate back):** $$P' = T(x_r, y_r) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_r, -y_r) \cdot P$$ Written explicitly in homogeneous coordinates: $$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_r \\ 0 & 1 & y_r \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_r \\ 0 & 1 & -y_r \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ $\blacksquare$ **Derivation Complete.** ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — 3D Rotation About the Z-axis (by Analogy) In 3D, rotation about the Z-axis is the natural generalization of 2D rotation. The Z-coordinate is *invariant* ($z' = z$), and the $(x, y)$ sub-matrix matches the 2D case. **Step 1:** Hold $z$ constant: $z' = z$. **Step 2:** Apply the 2D rotation logic to the $(x, y)$ plane viewed along the Z-axis. $$x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta$$ $$y' = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$$ **Step 3:** Assemble the $3 \times 3$ matrix. $$R_z(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4 — Sanity Check:** The determinant is $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$. The matrix is orthogonal. The "left-out" coordinate is the rotation axis. This same logic generates $R_x(\theta)$ (where $x$ is invariant) and $R_y(\theta)$ (where $y$ is invariant), giving the matrices in Table 1 rows 3, 4, 5. $\blacksquare$ **Derivation Complete.** ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A triangle has vertices $A(1, 1)$, $B(4, 1)$, $C(2, 4)$. Rotate the triangle by $90^\circ$ counter-clockwise about the origin. Find the new coordinates. **Solution:** With $\theta = 90^\circ$, $\cos 90^\circ = 0$ and $\sin 90^\circ = 1$. The rotation matrix becomes: $$R(90^\circ) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ Apply to each vertex: **Vertex A(1, 1):** $$x_A' = 0(1) - 1(1) = -1, \quad y_A' = 1(1) + 0(1) = 1$$ $$A' = (-1, 1)$$ **Vertex B(4, 1):** $$x_B' = 0(4) - 1(1) = -1, \quad y_B' = 1(4) + 0(1) = 4$$ $$B' = (-1, 4)$$ **Vertex C(2, 4):** $$x_C' = 0(2) - 1(4) = -4, \quad y_C' = 1(2) + 0(4) = 2$$ $$C' = (-4, 2)$$ **Final Answer:** $A'(-1, 1), B'(-1, 4), C'(-4, 2)$. *Verification:* The original triangle has side $\vert AB \vert = 3$. The rotated side $\vert A'B' \vert = \sqrt{0^2 + 3^2} = 3$. **Rigid-body property confirmed.** ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Geometric Visualization of 2D Rotation The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the operational architecture of the 2D rotation pipeline (input → polar conversion → matrix application → output). ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Point P x comma y] --> B[Convert to Polar Form] B --> C[Polar Form r cos alpha, r sin alpha] C --> D[Add Theta to Angle] D --> E[Reconstruct New Cartesian] E --> F[Output Point P prime x prime comma y prime] G[Reference Pivot] --> D H[Angle Theta User Input] --> D I[Rotation Matrix R Theta] -.Parallel Path.-> F C --> I ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Composite Rotation About Arbitrary Pivot ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Original Point P] --> T1[Stage 1: Translate -xr, -yr] T1 --> R[Stage 2: Rotate by Theta about Origin] R --> T2[Stage 3: Translate +xr, +yr to Restore Pivot] T2 --> Pprime[Output Point P prime] style T1 fill:#e6f3ff style R fill:#fff2cc style T2 fill:#e6f3ff style Pprime fill:#d5e8d4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Color Legend:** Blue blocks represent *translation* stages, Yellow represents the *rotation* stage, Green represents the *final output*. This mirrors the canonical 3-stage composite transformation logic evaluated in KTU board examinations. ### 4.3 Logical Flow of a 3D Rotation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[3D Point x, y, z] --> B{Choose Axis} B -->|X-axis| Cx[Apply Rx matrix, x invariant] B -->|Y-axis| Cy[Apply Ry matrix, y invariant] B -->|Z-axis| Cz[Apply Rz matrix, z invariant] B -->|Arbitrary Axis| Ca[Apply Rodrigues Formula] Cx --> D[Output x prime, y prime, z prime] Cy --> D Cz --> D Ca --> D style A fill:#f9f9f9 style D fill:#d5e8d4 ``` ### 4.4 Conceptual Diagram — Effect of Rotation on Vertices ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Original_Frame A1[Vertex A at x=1, y=1] B1[Vertex B at x=4, y=1] C1[Vertex C at x=2, y=4] end subgraph Transformation_Path M[Rotation by 90 degrees CCW] end subgraph Rotated_Frame A2[Vertex A prime at x=-1, y=1] B2[Vertex B prime at x=-1, y=4] C2[Vertex C prime at x=-4, y=2] end A1 --> M B1 --> M C1 --> M M --> A2 M --> B2 M --> C2 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *What is meant by rotation in computer graphics? Write the 2D rotation transformation matrix for a point rotated counter-clockwise by an angle $\theta$ about the origin.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **(1 Mark)** *Definition:* Rotation is a rigid-body transformation that repositions an object by rotating every point around a fixed pivot (default: origin) by a specified angle $\theta$, preserving shape, size, and distances. - **(1 Mark)** *Sign Convention:* Counter-clockwise (CCW) is positive; follows the right-hand rule / Cartesian convention. - **(1 Mark)** *Matrix:* $$R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}, \quad \begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = R(\theta) \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$$ --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *State any three properties of the 2D rotation matrix.* **Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Orthogonality:** $R(\theta) \cdot R^T(\theta) = I$. The columns and rows are orthonormal. 2. **Unit Determinant:** $\det(R(\theta)) = \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$, hence area and orientation are preserved. 3. **Inverse Property:** $R^{-1}(\theta) = R(-\theta) = R^T(\theta)$. The reverse rotation is by $-\theta$. 4. *Bonus (1 mark):* Composition: $R(\theta_1) \cdot R(\theta_2) = R(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ — rotations about the same pivot commute and add. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** Derive the 2D rotation transformation matrix for rotating a point $P(x, y)$ counter-clockwise by an angle $\theta$ about the origin. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** A triangle has vertices $A(2, 1)$, $B(5, 1)$, $C(3, 5)$. Rotate the triangle by $45^\circ$ CCW about the origin. Compute the new coordinates of each vertex and verify the rigid-body property by checking the length of one side before and after. **\[7 Marks\]** --- **Model Solution — (a) [7 Marks]:** 1. **[1 mark]** Express the point in polar form: $x = r\cos\alpha$, $y = r\sin\alpha$, where $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ and $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$. 2. **[1 mark]** Apply rotation by $\theta$ to get the new angle $\alpha + \theta$, keeping $r$ unchanged. 3. **[1 mark]** Write new coordinates: $x' = r\cos(\alpha + \theta)$, $y' = r\sin(\alpha + \theta)$. 4. **[1 mark]** Expand using the trigonometric sum identities. 5. **[1 mark]** Substitute $r\cos\alpha = x$ and $r\sin\alpha = y$ back into the equations. 6. **[1 mark]** Obtain the linear form: $x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta$, $y' = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$. 7. **[1 mark]** Express in matrix form: $P' = R(\theta) \cdot P$ where $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$. --- **Model Solution — (b) [7 Marks]:** Given: $\theta = 45^\circ$, $\cos 45^\circ = \sin 45^\circ = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071$. The rotation matrix is: $$R(45^\circ) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7071 & -0.7071 \\ 0.7071 & 0.7071 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Vertex A(2, 1):** $$x_A' = (0.7071)(2) - (0.7071)(1) = 0.7071 \approx 0.71$$ $$y_A' = (0.7071)(2) + (0.7071)(1) = 2.1213 \approx 2.12$$ So $A' = (0.71, 2.12)$. **Vertex B(5, 1):** $$x_B' = (0.7071)(5) - (0.7071)(1) = 2.8284 \approx 2.83$$ $$y_B' = (0.7071)(5) + (0.7071)(1) = 4.2426 \approx 4.24$$ So $B' = (2.83, 4.24)$. **Vertex C(3, 5):** $$x_C' = (0.7071)(3) - (0.7071)(5) = -1.4142 \approx -1.41$$ $$y_C' = (0.7071)(3) + (0.7071)(5) = 5.6568 \approx 5.66$$ So $C' = (-1.41, 5.66)$. **Verification of Rigid-Body Property [2 marks]:** - Original side $\vert AB \vert = \sqrt{(5-2)^2 + (1-1)^2} = \sqrt{9} = 3$. - Rotated side $\vert A'B' \vert = \sqrt{(2.83 - 0.71)^2 + (4.24 - 2.12)^2} = \sqrt{(2.12)^2 + (2.12)^2} = \sqrt{4.4944 + 4.4944} = \sqrt{8.9888} \approx 3$. Since $\vert AB \vert = \vert A'B' \vert = 3$, the rigid-body (isometric) property is verified. $\blacksquare$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the 3-stage process of rotating a 2D point $P(x, y)$ about an arbitrary pivot point $(x_r, y_r)$. Write the corresponding composite matrix. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** Given a square with vertices $P_1(2, 2)$, $P_2(6, 2)$, $P_3(6, 6)$, $P_4(2, 6)$, rotate the square by $90^\circ$ clockwise about the pivot point $(4, 4)$. Determine the new coordinates of all vertices. **\[7 Marks\]** --- **Model Solution — (a) [7 Marks]:** **The 3-Stage Composite Process:** When the pivot is *not* the origin, the rotation is decomposed into three elementary steps: **Stage 1 — Translate the pivot to the origin [2 marks]:** Apply translation $T(-x_r, -y_r)$ to move the pivot from $(x_r, y_r)$ to $(0, 0)$. Every point $(x, y)$ becomes $(x - x_r, y - y_r)$. **Stage 2 — Rotate about the origin [2 marks]:** Apply the canonical 2D rotation matrix $R(\theta)$ about the new origin. The point $(x - x_r, y - y_r)$ becomes: $$x'' = (x - x_r)\cos\theta - (y - y_r)\sin\theta$$ $$y'' = (x - x_r)\sin\theta + (y - y_r)\cos\theta$$ **Stage 3 — Translate back to restore the pivot [1 mark]:** Apply translation $T(x_r, y_r)$ to move the system back. The final coordinates are: $$x' = x'' + x_r, \quad y' = y'' + y_r$$ **Composite Matrix [2 marks]:** The composite transformation is $P' = T(x_r, y_r) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_r, -y_r) \cdot P$, where: $$T(x_r, y_r) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_r, -y_r) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x_r(1 - \cos\theta) + y_r\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y_r(1 - \cos\theta) - x_r\sin\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ --- **Model Solution — (b) [7 Marks]:** Given: Square with vertices $P_1(2, 2)$, $P_2(6, 2)$, $P_3(6, 6)$, $P_4(2, 6)$; pivot $(x_r, y_r) = (4, 4)$; $\theta = 90^\circ$ **clockwise** = $-90^\circ$. For CW rotation, use $\theta = -90^\circ$: $\cos(-90^\circ) = 0$ and $\sin(-90^\circ) = -1$. **Direct Formula Application:** $$x' = x_r + (x - x_r)\cos\theta - (y - y_r)\sin\theta$$ $$y' = y_r + (x - x_r)\sin\theta + (y - y_r)\cos\theta$$ **Vertex P₁(2, 2):** $$x' = 4 + (2-4)(0) - (2-4)(-1) = 4 + 0 - (-2) = 6$$ $$y' = 4 + (2-4)(-1) + (2-4)(0) = 4 + 2 + 0 = 6$$ $P_1' = (6, 6)$. **Vertex P₂(6, 2):** $$x' = 4 + (6-4)(0) - (2-4)(-1) = 4 - 2 = 2$$ $$y' = 4 + (6-4)(-1) + (2-4)(0) = 4 - 2 = 2$$ Wait — recalculation: $x' = 4 + 0 - (-1)(2-4) = 4 - (-1)(-2) = 4 - 2 = 2$. $y' = 4 + (-1)(6-4) + 0 = 4 - 2 = 2$. Actually let me recompute carefully: $x' = 4 + (2)(0) - (-2)(-1) = 4 - 2 = 2$. $y' = 4 + (2)(-1) + (-2)(0) = 4 - 2 = 2$. So $P_2' = (2, 2)$. **Vertex P₃(6, 6):** $$x' = 4 + (2)(0) - (2)(-1) = 4 + 2 = 6$$ $$y' = 4 + (2)(-1) + (2)(0) = 4 - 2 = 2$$ $P_3' = (6, 2)$. **Vertex P₄(2, 6):** $$x' = 4 + (-2)(0) - (2)(-1) = 4 + 2 = 6$$ $$y' = 4 + (-2)(-1) + (2)(0) = 4 + 2 = 6$$ $P_4' = (6, 6)$. *Re-evaluation noting pivot is the center of the square (4,4) and the rotation is $90°$ CW about the center: a $90°$ CW rotation of a square about its own center simply swaps vertices in a cycle: $P_1 \to P_2 \to P_3 \to P_4 \to P_1$ becoming $P_4 \to P_1 \to P_2 \to P_3$ respectively. The expected mapping is:* - $P_1(2,2) \to (2, 6) = P_4$ position - $P_2(6,2) \to (2, 2) = P_1$ position - $P_3(6,6) \to (6, 2) = P_2$ position - $P_4(2,6) \to (6, 6) = P_3$ position **Final Rotated Coordinates (corrected):** - $P_1'(2, 6)$ - $P_2'(2, 2)$ - $P_3'(6, 2)$ - $P_4'(6, 6)$ This matches the geometric intuition: a $90°$ CW rotation of a square about its center maps the bottom-left corner to the top-left, etc. $\blacksquare$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign Error (–1 to –2 marks lost):** Forgetting the negative sign in $-\sin\theta$ in the rotation matrix. This is the single most common error. Memorize: $x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta$. > 2. **Wrong Order of Matrix Multiplication (–2 marks):** In composite transformations, matrices multiply **right-to-left** in terms of operation order, but **left-to-right** in the written expression. For pivot rotation: $T_{back} \cdot R \cdot T_{forward}$. > 3. **Degrees vs Radians (–1 mark):** Always check whether $\theta$ is given in degrees. Use $\cos(45^\circ) = 1/\sqrt{2}$, not $\cos(45 \text{ rad})$. > 4. **Confusing Pivot Translation Direction (–2 marks):** To rotate about $(x_r, y_r)$: FIRST translate by $(-x_r, -y_r)$, THEN rotate, THEN translate by $(+x_r, +y_r)$. Students often do the opposite translation first. > 5. **Forgetting the Third Row in Homogeneous Form (–1 mark):** When writing a $3 \times 3$ homogeneous matrix, the bottom row is always $[0 \; 0 \; 1]$. Omission loses a mark. > 6. **Clockwise Rotation Mistake:** CW rotation = negative angle. Either use $\theta = -90^\circ$ explicitly, or replace $\theta$ with $-\theta$ in all formula terms. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Rotation (Module 2):** - **Core Definition:** Rotation is an isometric (rigid-body) transformation that preserves shape, size, and distances while changing orientation. - **Standard 2D Matrix:** $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ (CCW, about origin). - **Sign Convention:** CCW is **positive**; CW is **negative**. This is universal in KTU problems. - **Coordinate Update:** $x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta$; $y' = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$. - **Pivot Rotation Recipe:** Translate → Rotate → Translate back. Always in this order. - **3D Axis Identity:** The coordinate corresponding to the rotation axis is *unchanged* (e.g., $x' = x$ for X-axis rotation). - **Orthogonal Property:** $R^{-1}(\theta) = R^T(\theta) = R(-\theta)$. This is essential for inverse problems and view transformations. - **Determinant = +1:** Guarantees preservation of area (in 2D) or volume (in 3D), and preserves right-handedness. - **Composite Transformations:** Use **homogeneous coordinates** ($3 \times 3$ in 2D, $4 \times 4$ in 3D) to combine rotation with translation and scaling seamlessly. - **Rodrigues' Formula:** For 3D rotation about an arbitrary axis defined by a unit vector $\hat{u}$: $R = \cos\theta \cdot I + (1 - \cos\theta)\hat{u}\hat{u}^T + \sin\theta \cdot [\hat{u}]_\times$. This is a high-yield KTU topic for advanced questions. - **Caveat:** Rotation matrices do **not** commute in 3D (different axes). Order of rotation matters significantly. Use Euler angles carefully. - **Numerical Tip:** Memorize $\cos 30^\circ = \sqrt{3}/2$, $\sin 30^\circ = 1/2$, $\cos 45^\circ = \sin 45^\circ = 1/\sqrt{2}$, $\cos 60^\circ = 1/2$, $\sin 60^\circ = \sqrt{3}/2$, $\cos 90^\circ = 0$, $\sin 90^\circ = 1$. - **Worked Example Sanity Check:** Always verify rigid-body property by computing a side length before and after — this is a frequent 1–2 mark bonus in valuation. - **Negative Angle Substitution:** To convert CW to standard formula, substitute $\theta \to -\theta$. The matrix becomes $\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$. - **Common Mistake Trap:** Students often write $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ — this is **wrong**! The $(1,2)$ entry is $-\sin\theta$, not $+\sin\theta$. - **Homogeneous Form Required:** Whenever a composite transformation is asked (rotate + translate), the $3 \times 3$ homogeneous matrix form is mandatory in KTU answers.

topics.title: Scaling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT416 - GRAPHICS AND MULTIMEDIA PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Scaling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scaling** is a rigid two-dimensional (or three-dimensional) affine geometric transformation that uniformly or differentially enlarges or shrinks the dimensions of a graphical object relative to the coordinate origin (or any arbitrary fixed reference point) by multiplying each vertex coordinate $(x, y)$ by a scalar scaling factor $S_x$ and $S_y$ along the respective $X$ and $Y$ axes.

In matrix form, a point $P = (x, y)$ is transformed to a new point $P' = (x', y')$ using:

$$P' = S \cdot P$$

where $S$ is the **Scaling Matrix** of order $2 \times 2$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you have a **photograph** of a building on your computer screen. If you grab the **corner** of that photo and drag it outward, the building in the image **grows bigger** — this is **enlargement scaling ($S > 1$)**. If you drag the corner **inward**, the building **shrinks** — this is **reduction scaling ($0 < S < 1$)**. If you also have a "mirror" setting and flip the image, that mirror effect is achieved via **negative scaling ($S < 0$)**.

### Key Categorization of Scaling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Uniform Scaling**: $S_x = S_y$. The object expands/shrinks **proportionally** (aspect ratio preserved).
> - **Non-Uniform (Differential) Scaling**: $S_x \neq S_y$. The object gets **stretched or squashed** along an axis (aspect ratio violated).
> - **Scaling Factor $S = 1$**: Identity transformation (no change).
> - **Scaling Factor $S = 0$**: Object collapses to the origin.
> - **Scaling Factor $S < 0$**: Object is reflected across the corresponding axis in addition to scaling.
> - **Magnitude** $\vert S \vert > 1$: Enlargement.
> - **Magnitude** $\vert S \vert < 1$: Reduction.

### Standard Boundary Metrics (in Bold)

- **Scaling Factor ($S_x, S_y, S_z$)** → Dimensionless **real number** multiplier.
- **Fixed Point (Pivot Point)** → $(x_f, y_f)$ — the invariant reference anchor that does **not move** during the transformation.
- **Coordinate Origin** → $(0, 0)$ — the default reference point for basic scaling.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Identify the Geometric Object**: The object is represented as a set of vertices $P_i = (x_i, y_i)$ linked by edges.
2. **Select the Scaling Factors**: Decide the magnitude of $S_x$ and $S_y$ based on the desired dimensional change.
3. **Choose the Reference Point**: Determine whether scaling is about the **origin**, an **arbitrary fixed point $(x_f, y_f)$**, or the **object centroid**.
4. **Apply the Transformation Matrix**: Multiply each coordinate by the scaling matrix.
5. **Reconstruct the Object**: Reconnect the transformed vertices using the same topology to obtain the scaled object $P'$.

### The 'Why' and 'How'

- **Why multiply coordinates?** Multiplication by a constant factor $S$ is the most elementary mathematical operation that **preserves straight lines** while changing length.
- **How does scaling about a fixed point work?** It is decomposed into three atomic sub-operations: **Translate** the fixed point to the origin, **Scale** about the origin, and **Translate** back. This composition is a powerful **affine decomposition** strategy.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Transformation** | **Matrix Representation** | **Resulting Coordinates** |
|---|---|---|
| 2D Scaling (about Origin) | $S = \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 \\ 0 & S_y \end{bmatrix}$ | $x' = S_x \cdot x, \quad y' = S_y \cdot y$ |
| 2D Scaling (about Fixed Point $(x_f, y_f)$) | $T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$ | $x' = x_f + S_x(x - x_f), \quad y' = y_f + S_y(y - y_f)$ |
| 3D Scaling (about Origin) | $S = \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & S_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & S_z \end{bmatrix}$ | $x' = S_x x, \quad y' = S_y y, \quad z' = S_z z$ |
| 3D Scaling (about Fixed Point) | $T \cdot S \cdot T^{-1}$ | $x' = x_f + S_x(x - x_f)$ |
| Homogeneous 2D Scaling | $\begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & S_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Allows composite transformations |
| Inverse Scaling | $S^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/S_x & 0 \\ 0 & 1/S_y \end{bmatrix}$ | Restores original size |
| Reflection (special case $S = -1$) | $\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ or $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Mirroring across $Y$ or $X$ axis |
| Area Scaling Factor | $A' = \vert S_x \cdot S_y \vert \cdot A$ | Ratio of scaled to original area |
| Volume Scaling Factor (3D) | $V' = \vert S_x \cdot S_y \cdot S_z \vert \cdot V$ | Ratio of scaled to original volume |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Area & Volume Engineering Note**: In computer graphics and CAD, the scaling factor multiplies linearly, but **areas scale quadratically** ($A' = S^2 \cdot A$ for uniform scaling) and **volumes scale cubically** ($V' = S^3 \cdot V$). This is critical in **finite element analysis**, **3D printing tolerance**, and **architectural load estimation**.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CAD/CAM Design**: Resizing engineering components on blueprints.
- **Medical Imaging**: Zooming into MRI/CT scan regions of interest.
- **Cartography**: Adjusting map scale for navigation.
- **3D Animation**: Animating objects growing/shrinking (e.g., Ant-Man effect).
- **UI/UX Design**: Responsive layout scaling on different screen resolutions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: 2D Scaling About the Origin

Starting from the definition, the new coordinates after scaling are proportional to the original coordinates. Therefore, let the scaling factors along $X$ and $Y$ axes be $S_x$ and $S_y$ respectively. The transformed coordinates are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= S_x \cdot x \\
y' &= S_y \cdot y
\end{aligned}
$$

Writing this in matrix form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P' &= S \cdot P \\
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 \\ 0 & S_y \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **origin-based scaling matrix** used as a primitive in all graphics pipelines.

---

### Derivation 2: 2D Scaling About an Arbitrary Fixed Point $(x_f, y_f)$

When scaling must occur with respect to a **fixed point** that is not the origin, the point must first be conceptually moved to the origin. This is achieved using the **Composite Transformation** strategy.

**Step 1 — Translate the fixed point to the origin**:
The translation matrix that moves $(x_f, y_f)$ to $(0, 0)$ is $T(-x_f, -y_f)$.

**Step 2 — Apply scaling about the new origin**:
Apply the scaling matrix $S$ at the origin.

**Step 3 — Translate the fixed point back to its original position**:
Apply the inverse translation $T(x_f, y_f)$.

The **combined transformation matrix** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S \cdot T(-x_f, y_f) \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & S_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, compute $S \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$ first:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f) &= \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & S_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & -S_x x_f \\ 0 & S_y & -S_y y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now multiply the result by $T(x_f, y_f)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & -S_x x_f \\ 0 & S_y & -S_y y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & x_f(1 - S_x) \\ 0 & S_y & y_f(1 - S_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying this to point $(x, y)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
