\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & x_f(1 - S_x) \\ 0 & S_y & y_f(1 - S_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
x' &= S_x \cdot x + x_f(1 - S_x) \\
x' &= x_f + S_x(x - x_f) \\
y' &= S_y \cdot y + y_f(1 - S_y) \\
y' &= y_f + S_y(y - y_f)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> These final scalar equations are the **most frequently tested results** in KTU university examinations. Memorize them.

---

### Derivation 3: Inverse Scaling Matrix

For a 2D scaling matrix, the inverse is computed such that $S \cdot S^{-1} = I$. The determinant of $S$ is $\det(S) = S_x \cdot S_y$. Assuming $S_x \neq 0$ and $S_y \neq 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S^{-1} &= \frac{1}{S_x S_y} \begin{bmatrix} S_y & 0 \\ 0 & S_x \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1/S_x & 0 \\ 0 & 1/S_y \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is used for **reverse scaling** (shrinking an enlarged image back to its original size).

---

### Derivation 4: Area Scaling Relationship

Consider a unit square with area $A = 1 \times 1 = 1$. After scaling by $S_x$ and $S_y$, the new square has dimensions $S_x$ by $S_y$, giving new area $A' = S_x \cdot S_y$. Generalizing for any shape with original area $A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A' &= \vert S_x \cdot S_y \vert \cdot A
\end{aligned}
$$

The absolute value ensures area remains positive even if reflection occurs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram A: 2D Scaling Transformation Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT OBJECT Point x y"] --> B["REFERENCE POINT ORIGIN or Fixed Point"]
    B --> C{"SCALING TYPE SELECTION"}
    C --> D1["UNIFORM SCALING Sx equals Sy"]
    C --> D2["NON UNIFORM SCALING Sx not equal Sy"]
    C --> D3["NEGATIVE SCALING S less than zero"]
    D1 --> E["APPLY MATRIX S times P"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F["OUTPUT OBJECT Point x prime y prime"]
    F --> G["RECONSTRUCT GEOMETRY"]
    G --> H["FINAL SCALED OBJECT"]
```

### Diagram B: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fixed-Point Scaling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT VERTEX x y"] --> B["TRANSLATE BLOCK Move to Origin"]
    B --> C["SCALING BLOCK Apply Sx Sy"]
    C --> D["TRANSLATE BLOCK Move Back to Pivot"]
    D --> E["OUTPUT VERTEX x prime y prime"]
    F["FIXED POINT xf yf"] --> B
    F --> D
```

### Diagram C: Effect of Scaling Factors on Object Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Original Object Vertex x y"] --> Q1["Sx greater than 1 Enlarge Horizontally"]
    P --> Q2["Sx less than 1 Reduce Horizontally"]
    P --> Q3["Sx equals 1 No Change"]
    P --> Q4["Sx less than 0 Reflect Across Y Axis"]
    Q1 --> R["Transformed Vertex x prime y prime"]
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    Q4 --> R
```

### Diagram D: Composite Transformation Pipeline (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1 Translate to Origin T neg xf neg yf"] --> S2["Step 2 Apply Scaling S"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3 Translate Back T xf yf"]
    S3 --> S4["Final Composite Matrix M"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define scaling transformation. Differentiate between uniform and non-uniform scaling with an example.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Scaling is an affine transformation that changes the size of an object by multiplying its coordinates with scaling factors $S_x$ and $S_y$. When $S_x = S_y$, it is called **uniform scaling**, preserving the aspect ratio (e.g., resizing a square by 2 in both dimensions). When $S_x \neq S_y$, it is **non-uniform scaling** (e.g., stretching a square into a rectangle by setting $S_x = 2, S_y = 1$).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is a fixed point in scaling? Write the transformation matrix for 2D scaling about a fixed point $(x_f, y_f)$.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A fixed point is a reference point that remains invariant (does not change position) during scaling. The transformation matrix is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & x_f(1 - S_x) \\ 0 & S_y & y_f(1 - S_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Derive the 2D transformation matrix for scaling about an arbitrary fixed point $(x_f, y_f)$. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The derivation proceeds in three stages. First, translate the fixed point to the origin. Second, apply scaling. Third, translate back. The intermediate computation is shown below.

**Stage 1: Translation to origin**

$$
T(-x_f, -y_f) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Stage 2: Scaling at origin**

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & S_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Stage 3: Translation back**

$$
T(x_f, y_f) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Composite matrix computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f) \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & S_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing the rightmost two products first:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f) &= \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & -S_x x_f \\ 0 & S_y & -S_y y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying by the leftmost translation matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & x_f - S_x x_f \\ 0 & S_y & y_f - S_y y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 & x_f(1 - S_x) \\ 0 & S_y & y_f(1 - S_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the required 2D scaling matrix about a fixed point.

---

**(b)** A triangle has vertices $A(1, 1), B(4, 1), C(1, 5)$. Apply a scaling transformation with $S_x = 2$ and $S_y = 3$ about the origin, and find the new coordinates. Also compute the area scaling factor. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The scaling matrix is:

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**For vertex A(1, 1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A'_x &= 2 \times 1 = 2 \\
A'_y &= 3 \times 1 = 3 \\
A' &= (2, 3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**For vertex B(4, 1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B'_x &= 2 \times 4 = 8 \\
B'_y &= 3 \times 1 = 3 \\
B' &= (8, 3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**For vertex C(1, 5):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C'_x &= 2 \times 1 = 2 \\
C'_y &= 3 \times 5 = 15 \\
C' &= (2, 15)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Area of original triangle** (using the determinant formula):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{1}{2} \vert x_A(y_B - y_C) + x_B(y_C - y_A) + x_C(y_A - y_B) \vert \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \vert 1(1 - 5) + 4(5 - 1) + 1(1 - 1) \vert \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \vert -4 + 16 + 0 \vert = 6 \text{ square units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Area of transformed triangle**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A' &= \frac{1}{2} \vert 2(3 - 15) + 8(15 - 3) + 2(3 - 3) \vert \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \vert -24 + 96 + 0 \vert = 36 \text{ square units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Area Scaling Factor**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Factor} &= \frac{A'}{A} = \frac{36}{6} = 6 \\
\text{Verification:} \quad \vert S_x \cdot S_y \vert &= \vert 2 \times 3 \vert = 6 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** A square has vertices $P_1(2, 2), P_2(6, 2), P_3(6, 6), P_4(2, 6)$. Apply a scaling transformation with $S_x = S_y = 0.5$ about the fixed point $(4, 4)$ and find the new coordinates. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The fixed-point scaling formula is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= x_f + S_x(x - x_f) \\
y' &= y_f + S_y(y - y_f)
\end{aligned}
$$

With $x_f = 4, y_f = 4, S_x = S_y = 0.5$:

**For $P_1(2, 2)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= 4 + 0.5(2 - 4) = 4 + 0.5(-2) = 4 - 1 = 3 \\
y' &= 4 + 0.5(2 - 4) = 4 - 1 = 3 \\
P'_1 &= (3, 3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $P_2(6, 2)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= 4 + 0.5(6 - 4) = 4 + 1 = 5 \\
y' &= 4 + 0.5(2 - 4) = 4 - 1 = 3 \\
P'_2 &= (5, 3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $P_3(6, 6)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= 4 + 0.5(6 - 4) = 5 \\
y' &= 4 + 0.5(6 - 4) = 5 \\
P'_3 &= (5, 5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $P_4(2, 6)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= 4 + 0.5(2 - 4) = 3 \\
y' &= 4 + 0.5(6 - 4) = 5 \\
P'_4 &= (3, 5)
\end{aligned}
$$

The scaled square has vertices $(3, 3), (5, 3), (5, 5), (3, 5)$, with the center $(4, 4)$ remaining invariant.

---

**(b)** Explain the difference between scaling and reflection. Show how reflection can be represented as a special case of scaling. Also derive the inverse of a 2D scaling matrix. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Difference between scaling and reflection:**

| **Aspect** | **Scaling** | **Reflection** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Changes the size of an object | Creates a mirror image of the object |
| Scaling factor | $\vert S \vert \neq 1$ typically | $S = -1$ along the reflecting axis |
| Sign of factor | Usually positive (can be negative) | Always $-1$ on the reflecting axis, $+1$ on the other |
| Effect on area | $A' = \vert S_x S_y \vert A$ | $A' = A$ (area preserved) |
| Effect on orientation | Preserved (if $S > 0$) | Reversed (chirality flipped) |

**Reflection as special case of scaling:**
For reflection across the $Y$-axis, set $S_x = -1$ and $S_y = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_Y &= \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

For reflection across the $X$-axis, set $S_x = 1$ and $S_y = -1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_X &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms that **reflection is mathematically a special case of negative scaling**.

**Inverse of 2D scaling matrix derivation:**

Given:

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 \\ 0 & S_y \end{bmatrix}
$$

The determinant is $\det(S) = S_x S_y$. For the inverse to exist, $S_x \neq 0$ and $S_y \neq 0$. The inverse is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S^{-1} &= \frac{1}{\det(S)} \begin{bmatrix} S_y & 0 \\ 0 & S_x \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{S_x S_y} \begin{bmatrix} S_y & 0 \\ 0 & S_x \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1/S_x & 0 \\ 0 & 1/S_y \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \cdot S^{-1} &= \begin{bmatrix} S_x & 0 \\ 0 & S_y \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1/S_x & 0 \\ 0 & 1/S_y \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Forgetting homogeneous coordinates**: When composing scaling with other transformations, students often write $2 \times 2$ matrices, which **cannot** be combined with translation $3 \times 3$ matrices. Always use $3 \times 3$ homogeneous form for 2D work and $4 \times 4$ for 3D.
> 2. **Order of matrix multiplication**: The composite $M = T \cdot S \cdot T^{-1}$ must be evaluated **right to left** when applying to a column vector. Reversing this loses 2 to 3 marks.
> 3. **Sign of fixed-point offset**: Writing $x_f(S_x - 1)$ instead of $x_f(1 - S_x)$ is a very common error worth **2 marks**.
> 4. **Negative scaling confusion**: Students sometimes treat $S = -0.5$ as only "reflection" and forget the magnitude component (reduction). It is **both** reflection and reduction.
> 5. **Area calculation**: The determinant-based area formula requires the **absolute value**; missing it loses marks if the determinant evaluates negative.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Scaling is multiplication**: New coordinate = scale factor × original coordinate.
- **Uniform scaling** keeps aspect ratio ($S_x = S_y$); **non-uniform scaling** stretches it ($S_x \neq S_y$).
- **Magnitude $\vert S \vert > 1$** enlarges; **$\vert S \vert < 1$** reduces; **$S = 0$** collapses; **$S < 0$** reflects.
- **Fixed-point scaling** uses the **three-step composition**: Translate → Scale → Translate Back.
- **Master formula** (memorize!): $x' = x_f + S_x(x - x_f)$ and $y' = y_f + S_y(y - y_f)$.
- **Reflection is special-case scaling** with $S = -1$ along the axis of reflection.
- **Areas scale by $\vert S_x S_y \vert$**; **volumes scale by $\vert S_x S_y S_z \vert$**.
- **Inverse scaling matrix** has diagonal entries $1/S_x$ and $1/S_y$ (provided $S_x, S_y \neq 0$).
- **Always use homogeneous coordinates** ($3 \times 3$ for 2D, $4 \times 4$ for 3D) when composing with translation or rotation.
- **3D scaling extends 2D scaling** with an additional $S_z$ factor along the $Z$ axis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reflection and Shearing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT416 - GRAPHICS AND MULTIMEDIA PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reflection and Shearing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 REFLECTION — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reflection** is a rigid-body geometric transformation that produces a **mirror image** of a 2D or 3D object about a specified line (the **axis of reflection** or **mirror line**). The reflected image is equidistant from the mirror line as the original object, but lies on the opposite side. In matrix terms, it is represented by a transformation matrix whose **determinant = -1**, indicating an orientation reversal (a change in handedness — from right-handed to left-handed coordinate system).

In computer graphics, reflection is fundamental for:
- Symmetric object modeling (human faces, automobiles, architectural floor plans)
- Generating mirror views in 3D rendering pipelines
- Creating texture patterns and procedural art

**Reflection preserves:**
- ✅ Distances (it's a *rigid* / *isometric* transformation)
- ✅ Angles
- ✅ Shape and size (no scaling involved)

**Reflection reverses:**
- ❌ Orientation (clockwise ↔ counter-clockwise winding order)
- ❌ Handedness (chirality is flipped)

## 1.2 SHEARING — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shearing** is a non-rigid affine transformation that distorts the shape of an object such that points are displaced **parallel to a given axis** by an amount proportional to their **perpendicular distance** from that axis. The transformation matrix has a **determinant = 1**, meaning area (in 2D) and volume (in 3D) are conserved, but the shape becomes slanted.

Shearing is widely used in:
- **3D graphics**: To simulate oblique projections and camera-lens distortions
- **Animation**: To produce "italic" or "skew" text effects
- **Image processing**: To create perspective illusions in 2D art
- **CAD systems**: To model shear stress in mechanical parts

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction**: Reflection is an *orthogonal* (rigid) transformation, while shearing is an *affine* (non-rigid) transformation. The mirror line is unaffected in reflection, while the **shear reference axis** remains fixed but all parallel lines get displaced in shearing.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

### Reflection — The Pond Analogy 🪞
> Imagine standing by a perfectly still lake. Your reflection appears *upside down* along the water's surface (the mirror line). If you raise your right hand, the reflection raises its **left** hand. The image is **equidistant from the surface**, but on the opposite side. This is **reflection about the x-axis**.

### Shearing — The Stack of Cards Analogy 🃏
> Take a deck of playing cards held vertically, with the bottom edge fixed on a table. Now push the top edge sideways. The cards **slant** — they are sheared. Each card slides parallel to the next, but the entire stack transforms into a **parallelogram**. This is **x-shear**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Reflections in 2D

| S.No. | Type of Reflection | Axis of Reflection | Transformation Matrix $M_R$ | Effect on Point $(x, y)$ |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | About **x-axis** | $y = 0$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(x, -y)$ |
| 2 | About **y-axis** | $x = 0$ | $\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(-x, y)$ |
| 3 | About **origin** | Both axes | $\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(-x, -y)$ |
| 4 | About line **$y = x$** | $y = x$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(y, x)$ |
| 5 | About line **$y = -x$** | $y = -x$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(-y, -x)$ |
| 6 | About line **$x = y \tan(\theta)$** | Through origin at angle $\theta$ | $\begin{bmatrix} \cos 2\theta & \sin 2\theta \\ \sin 2\theta & -\cos 2\theta \end{bmatrix}$ | See derivation |

## 2.2 The Master Reflection Formula (About a Line Through Origin at Angle θ)

For reflection about a line passing through the origin making an angle $\theta$ with the positive x-axis, the transformation matrix is:

$$M_R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 2\theta & \sin 2\theta \\ \sin 2\theta & -\cos 2\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

The transformed coordinates are obtained as:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 2\theta & \sin 2\theta \\ \sin 2\theta & -\cos 2\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$$

This gives:
- $x' = x \cos 2\theta + y \sin 2\theta$
- $y' = x \sin 2\theta - y \cos 2\theta$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Special Cases** (Substitute $\theta$ values):
> - $\theta = 0°$ → Reflection about **x-axis** ✓
> - $\theta = 90°$ → Reflection about **y-axis** ✓
> - $\theta = 45°$ → Reflection about **line $y = x$** ✓
> - $\theta = -45°$ (or $135°$) → Reflection about **line $y = -x$** ✓

## 2.3 Types of Shear in 2D

| S.No. | Type of Shear | Fixed Axis | Transformation Matrix $M_{Sh}$ | Effect on Point $(x, y)$ |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **X-Shear** ($Sh_x \neq 0$) | x-axis | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ Sh_x & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(x, y + x \cdot Sh_x)$ |
| 2 | **Y-Shear** ($Sh_y \neq 0$) | y-axis | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & Sh_y \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(x + y \cdot Sh_y, y)$ |

### Where:
- $Sh_x$ = **X-shear coefficient** (slants vertical lines; displaces y-coordinate proportional to x)
- $Sh_y$ = **Y-shear coefficient** (slants horizontal lines; displaces x-coordinate proportional to y)

## 2.4 Properties Comparison Table

| Property | Reflection | Shearing |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Determinant of matrix | $\det(M_R) = -1$ | $\det(M_{Sh}) = 1$ |
| Area conservation | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Distance conservation | ✅ Yes (Rigid) | ❌ No (Non-rigid) |
| Angle conservation | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (except along axes) |
| Orientation reversal | ✅ Yes (Indirect) | ❌ No (Direct) |
| Handedness | Reversed | Preserved |
| Classification | Orthogonal / Rigid | Affine / Non-rigid |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **CAD/CAM Systems**: Reflection is used for generating symmetric jigs, fixtures, and molds. Shearing is used to model **shear stress** in mechanical elements like beams and rivets.
- **Image Processing & CG**: Reflection matrices are embedded in OpenGL's `glScalef(-1,1,1)` for mirror effects. Shear matrices generate oblique projection views.
- **Crystallography**: Reflection symmetry operations describe crystal lattice planes (Miller indices).
- **Animation**: Skewed text (italic fonts) is created by applying Y-shear.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reflection About a Line y = x tan(θ) Through Origin

### Setup
Let $P(x, y)$ be the original point and $P'(x', y')$ be its reflection about a line $L$ passing through the origin and making angle $\theta$ with the positive x-axis.

### Step 1 — Compute the position vector of P
The position vector of $P$ from origin is:
$$\vec{r} = x\hat{i} + y\hat{j}$$

### Step 2 — Decompose the vector into components parallel and perpendicular to L
**Component parallel to L:**
$$\vec{r}_{\parallel} = (x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta)[\cos\theta \, \hat{i} + \sin\theta \, \hat{j}]$$

**Component perpendicular to L:**
$$\vec{r}_{\perp} = (-x \sin\theta + y \cos\theta)[-\sin\theta \, \hat{i} + \cos\theta \, \hat{j}]$$

### Step 3 — Apply the reflection rule
Reflection **reverses** the perpendicular component and **preserves** the parallel component:
$$\vec{r}' = \vec{r}_{\parallel} - \vec{r}_{\perp}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify
$$\begin{aligned}
x' &= (x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta)\cos\theta - (-x \sin\theta + y \cos\theta)(-\sin\theta) \\
x' &= x \cos^2\theta + y \sin\theta \cos\theta - x \sin^2\theta - y \sin\theta \cos\theta \\
x' &= x(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta) + y(2 \sin\theta \cos\theta) \\
x' &= x \cos 2\theta + y \sin 2\theta
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
y' &= (x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta)\sin\theta - (-x \sin\theta + y \cos\theta)(\cos\theta) \\
y' &= x \cos\theta \sin\theta + y \sin^2\theta + x \sin\theta \cos\theta - y \cos^2\theta \\
y' &= x(2 \sin\theta \cos\theta) + y(\sin^2\theta - \cos^2\theta) \\
y' &= x \sin 2\theta - y \cos 2\theta
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Write in Matrix Form
$$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 2\theta & \sin 2\theta \\ \sin 2\theta & -\cos 2\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$$

✅ **Verified**: The general reflection formula.

## 3.2 Special Case Verification: Reflection About x-axis ($\theta = 0$)

Substituting $\theta = 0°$ (so $\cos 0 = 1$, $\sin 0 = 0$):
$$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ -y \end{bmatrix}$$

✅ Point $(3, 4) \to (3, -4)$ — correctly reflected about x-axis.

## 3.3 Special Case Verification: Reflection About y = x ($\theta = 45°$)

Substituting $\theta = 45°$ (so $\cos 90° = 0$, $\sin 90° = 1$):
$$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} y \\ x \end{bmatrix}$$

✅ Point $(3, 4) \to (4, 3)$ — correctly reflected about line $y = x$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Reflection

**Problem:** Reflect the triangle with vertices $A(2, 1)$, $B(4, 1)$, $C(3, 4)$ about the **line $y = x$**, and find the area of the reflected triangle.

### Step 1 — Apply the matrix
$$M_R = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Transform each vertex
For $A(2, 1)$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} \implies A'(1, 2)$$

For $B(4, 1)$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} \implies B'(1, 4)$$

For $C(3, 4)$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} \implies C'(4, 3)$$

### Step 3 — Area Calculation using the Shoelace Formula
$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \vert x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2) \vert$$
$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \vert 1(4 - 3) + 1(3 - 2) + 4(2 - 4) \vert$$
$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \vert 1 + 1 - 8 \vert = \frac{1}{2} \times 6 = 3 \text{ sq. units}$$

✅ Area is preserved as expected (reflection is rigid).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Shearing

**Problem:** Apply a **y-shear with shear factor $Sh_y = 2$** to a square with vertices $A(1, 1)$, $B(4, 1)$, $C(4, 4)$, $D(1, 4)$, and find the area of the resulting figure.

### Step 1 — Apply the y-shear matrix
$$M_{Sh} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Transform each vertex
For $A(1, 1)$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \implies A'(3, 1)$$

For $B(4, 1)$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \implies B'(6, 1)$$

For $C(4, 4)$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 12 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} \implies C'(12, 4)$$

For $D(1, 4)$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 9 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} \implies D'(9, 4)$$

### Step 3 — Area Calculation (Shoelace Formula)
$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \vert x_1(y_2 - y_4) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_4 - y_2) + x_4(y_1 - y_3) \vert$$
$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \vert 3(1 - 4) + 6(4 - 1) + 12(4 - 1) + 9(1 - 4) \vert$$
$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \vert -9 + 18 + 36 - 27 \vert = \frac{1}{2} \times 18 = 9 \text{ sq. units}$$

### Step 4 — Verify with Determinant
$$\text{Original Area} = 3 \times 3 = 9 \text{ sq. units}$$
$$\text{New Area} = \vert \det(M_{Sh}) \vert \times \text{Original Area} = 1 \times 9 = 9 \text{ sq. units}$$ ✅

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reflection Architecture — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputPoint[Original Point P x y]
    angleInput[Mirror Line Angle theta]
    matrixGen[Matrix Generator Module]
    reflectMatrix[Reflection Matrix M_R cos 2theta sin 2theta sin 2theta neg cos 2theta]
    matrixMult[Matrix Multiplication Engine]
    outputPoint[Reflected Point P dash x dash y dash]
    determinentCheck[Determinant Check equals neg 1]
    rigidVerdict[Output: Rigid Transformation Handedness Flipped]
    
    inputPoint --> matrixMult
    angleInput --> matrixGen
    matrixGen --> reflectMatrix
    reflectMatrix --> matrixMult
    matrixMult --> outputPoint
    reflectMatrix --> determinentCheck
    determinentCheck --> rigidVerdict
```

## 4.2 Shearing Architecture — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputPoly[Original Polygon Vertex List]
    shearX[Shear Factor Shx]
    shearY[Shear Factor Shy]
    axisSelector[Axis Selector X or Y]
    xShearMatrix[X Shear Matrix 1 0 Shx 1]
    yShearMatrix[Y Shear Matrix 1 Shy 0 1]
    matrixMult[Matrix Multiplication Engine]
    outputPoly[Transformed Polygon Vertex List]
    areaCheck[Area Verification det equals 1]
    slantedVerdict[Output: Affine Transformation Parallelogram]
    
    inputPoly --> matrixMult
    axisSelector --> xShearMatrix
    axisSelector --> yShearMatrix
    shearX --> xShearMatrix
    shearY --> yShearMatrix
    xShearMatrix --> matrixMult
    yShearMatrix --> matrixMult
    matrixMult --> outputPoly
    xShearMatrix --> areaCheck
    yShearMatrix --> areaCheck
    areaCheck --> slantedVerdict
```

## 4.3 Transformation Comparison — Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[2D Geometric Transformation] --> B{Is the shape preserved?}
    B -- Yes Rigid --> C[Reflection Matrix]
    B -- No Non Rigid --> D[Shear Matrix]
    C --> C1[About x axis]
    C --> C2[About y axis]
    C --> C3[About y equals x]
    C --> C4[About origin]
    C --> C5[General angle theta]
    D --> D1[X Shear Shx]
    D --> D2[Y Shear Shy]
    D1 --> E1[Determinant equals 1]
    D2 --> E2[Determinant equals 1]
    C1 --> F1[Determinant equals neg 1]
    C2 --> F2[Determinant equals neg 1]
    C3 --> F3[Determinant equals neg 1]
    C4 --> F4[Determinant equals neg 1]
    C5 --> F5[Determinant equals neg 1]
```

## 4.4 Coordinate Mapping Reference Chart (Textual Schematic)

| Original Point $(x, y)$ | Reflection about x-axis | Reflection about y-axis | Reflection about y=x | X-Shear $(Sh_x)$ | Y-Shear $(Sh_y)$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $(a, b)$ | $(a, -b)$ | $(-a, b)$ | $(b, a)$ | $(a, b + a \cdot Sh_x)$ | $(a + b \cdot Sh_y, b)$ |
| $(2, 3)$ | $(2, -3)$ | $(-2, 3)$ | $(3, 2)$ | $(2, 3 + 2 Sh_x)$ | $(2 + 3 Sh_y, 3)$ |
| $(5, -4)$ | $(5, 4)$ | $(-5, -4)$ | $(-4, 5)$ | $(5, -4 + 5 Sh_x)$ | $(5 - 4 Sh_y, -4)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Memory Aid**: For reflection, the coordinate **perpendicular** to the mirror axis is negated. For shearing, the coordinate **parallel** to the shear axis is shifted by an amount proportional to the **perpendicular** distance.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between reflection and shearing transformations with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Reflection** is a *rigid body* transformation producing a mirror image of an object. The transformation matrix has a determinant of $-1$. Example: Reflecting the letter "b" about the y-axis gives "d".
- **Shearing** is a *non-rigid* affine transformation that distorts the shape of an object. The matrix has a determinant of $+1$, meaning area is preserved. Example: Converting regular text into italic text by applying Y-shear.
- Key difference: Reflection preserves distances and angles; shearing preserves area only.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Find the transformation matrix for reflecting a point about the line y = x.**

**Model Answer:**
The line $y = x$ makes an angle $\theta = 45°$ with the x-axis. Using the general reflection formula:
$$M_R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 2\theta & \sin 2\theta \\ \sin 2\theta & -\cos 2\theta \end{bmatrix}$$
Substituting $2\theta = 90°$, so $\cos 90° = 0$ and $\sin 90° = 1$:
$$M_R = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
The transformed point becomes $(y, x)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the 2D transformation matrix for reflection about a line passing through the origin making an angle $\theta$ with the positive x-axis. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

The reflection matrix about a line through origin at angle $\theta$ is derived as:
$$M_R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 2\theta & \sin 2\theta \\ \sin 2\theta & -\cos 2\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Detailed derivation:**
1. Let $P(x, y)$ be the original point. Its position vector is $\vec{r} = x\hat{i} + y\hat{j}$.
2. The line $L$ has unit direction vector $\hat{u} = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$.
3. The component of $\vec{r}$ along $L$ (parallel component):
$$\vec{r}_{\parallel} = (\vec{r} \cdot \hat{u})\hat{u} = (x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta)(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$$
4. The component perpendicular to $L$:
$$\vec{r}_{\perp} = \vec{r} - \vec{r}_{\parallel}$$
5. After reflection, the perpendicular component is negated:
$$\vec{r}' = \vec{r}_{\parallel} - \vec{r}_{\perp} = 2\vec{r}_{\parallel} - \vec{r}$$
6. Substituting and simplifying using $\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta = \cos 2\theta$ and $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$:
$$x' = x \cos 2\theta + y \sin 2\theta$$
$$y' = x \sin 2\theta - y \cos 2\theta$$
7. In matrix form:
$$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 2\theta & \sin 2\theta \\ \sin 2\theta & -\cos 2\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$$

**(b)** A triangle has vertices $A(1, 2)$, $B(5, 2)$, $C(3, 6)$. Apply a **reflection about the line $y = x$** followed by a **scaling by factor 2 about the origin**. Find the final coordinates of the transformed triangle and its area. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply reflection about $y = x$:**

The reflection matrix about $y = x$ is:
$$M_R = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Transforming each vertex:
- $A(1, 2) \to A'(2, 1)$
- $B(5, 2) \to B'(2, 5)$
- $C(3, 6) \to C'(6, 3)$

**Step 2 — Apply scaling by factor 2:**

The scaling matrix is:
$$M_S = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

The combined transformation matrix is:
$$M_{combined} = M_S \times M_R = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Apply combined matrix to original vertices:**

- $A(1, 2)$: $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} \implies A''(4, 2)$
- $B(5, 2)$: $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix} \implies B''(4, 10)$
- $C(3, 6)$: $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 12 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix} \implies C''(12, 6)$

**Step 4 — Compute final area using the Shoelace Formula:**
$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \vert x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2) \vert$$
$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \vert 4(10 - 6) + 4(6 - 2) + 12(2 - 10) \vert$$
$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \vert 16 + 16 - 96 \vert = \frac{1}{2} \times 64 = 32 \text{ sq. units}$$

✅ **Verification via determinants**: $\vert \det(M_R) \vert \times \vert \det(M_S) \vert = 1 \times 4 = 4$. Original triangle area $= 8$ sq. units. Final area $= 8 \times 4 = 32$ sq. units. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Modified]`

**(a)** What is shearing? Derive the transformation matrix for **x-shear** and **y-shear** with the fixed reference axis. Explain the effect of shear coefficients on a unit square. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Definition**: Shearing is an affine transformation that displaces each point parallel to a given axis by an amount proportional to its signed distance from that axis.

**X-Shear (Fixed x-axis):**
In x-shear, the x-axis remains fixed. The y-coordinate of each point is shifted by an amount proportional to x:
$$x' = x, \quad y' = y + Sh_x \cdot x$$

In matrix form:
$$M_{Sh_x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ Sh_x & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Y-Shear (Fixed y-axis):**
In y-shear, the y-axis remains fixed. The x-coordinate of each point is shifted by an amount proportional to y:
$$x' = x + Sh_y \cdot y, \quad y' = y$$

In matrix form:
$$M_{Sh_y} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & Sh_y \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Effect on a Unit Square** (vertices: $(0,0), (1,0), (1,1), (0,1)$):

For X-shear with $Sh_x = 2$:
- $(0, 0) \to (0, 0)$
- $(1, 0) \to (1, 2)$
- $(1, 1) \to (1, 3)$
- $(0, 1) \to (0, 1)$

The unit square becomes a **parallelogram** with area still 1 (since $\det(M) = 1$).

For Y-shear with $Sh_y = 2$:
- $(0, 0) \to (0, 0)$
- $(1, 0) \to (1, 0)$
- $(1, 1) \to (3, 1)$
- $(0, 1) \to (2, 1)$

Again, the unit square becomes a parallelogram with area 1.

**(b)** A square with vertices $P(0, 0)$, $Q(4, 0)$, $R(4, 4)$, $S(0, 4)$ is to be transformed using a **y-shear with shear factor $Sh_y = 3$**. Determine the coordinates of the transformed square and the new area. Verify the area using the determinant method. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Y-shear matrix:**
$$M_{Sh_y} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Transform each vertex:**
- $P(0, 0)$: $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \implies P'(0, 0)$
- $Q(4, 0)$: $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \implies Q'(4, 0)$
- $R(4, 4)$: $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 16 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} \implies R'(16, 4)$
- $S(0, 4)$: $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 12 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} \implies S'(12, 4)$

**Step 3 — Area calculation using the Shoelace Formula:**
$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \vert x_P(y_Q - y_S) + x_Q(y_R - y_P) + x_R(y_S - y_Q) + x_S(y_P - y_R) \vert$$
$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \vert 0(0 - 4) + 4(4 - 0) + 16(4 - 0) + 12(0 - 4) \vert$$
$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \vert 0 + 16 + 64 - 48 \vert = \frac{1}{2} \times 32 = 16 \text{ sq. units}$$

**Step 4 — Verify via determinant method:**
$$\det(M_{Sh_y}) = (1)(1) - (3)(0) = 1$$
$$\text{New Area} = \vert \det(M_{Sh_y}) \vert \times \text{Original Area} = 1 \times 16 = 16 \text{ sq. units}$$

✅ **Verified**: Both methods yield 16 sq. units, confirming that y-shear preserves area.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Callouts:**
> 1. **Matrix Multiplication Order**: When combining transformations, the order is **critical**. $M_B \cdot M_A$ means "apply $M_A$ first, then $M_B$". Reversing the order gives a different result.
> 2. **Sign Errors in Reflection Matrices**: Students often confuse $(x, -y)$ vs $(-x, y)$ for x-axis vs y-axis reflections. **Memory tip**: The coordinate *perpendicular* to the mirror axis gets negated.
> 3. **Shear Coefficient Placement**: In x-shear matrix $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ Sh_x & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $Sh_x$ is placed in the **bottom-left** position, not bottom-right. Misplacement changes the transformation entirely.
> 4. **Angle Conversion**: When $\theta$ is in degrees, remember to compute $2\theta$ and then use trigonometric values. Forgetting the factor of 2 is a common error in the general reflection formula.
> 5. **Composite Transformations**: Always multiply matrices in the correct sequence. KTU typically asks for composite transformations involving 2-3 operations in sequence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- ✅ **Reflection** is a **rigid-body** transformation with $\det(M_R) = -1$. It preserves distances and angles but reverses orientation.
- ✅ **Shearing** is an **affine** transformation with $\det(M_{Sh}) = 1$. It preserves area (in 2D) but distorts shape.
- ✅ The **general reflection matrix** about a line through origin at angle $\theta$ uses $\cos 2\theta$ and $\sin 2\theta$ — remember the **factor of 2**.
- ✅ Special cases: $\theta = 0°$ → x-axis reflection; $\theta = 45°$ → $y = x$ reflection; $\theta = 90°$ → y-axis reflection.
- ✅ **X-shear** fixes the x-axis and shifts y-coordinates; matrix is $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ Sh_x & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
- ✅ **Y-shear** fixes the y-axis and shifts x-coordinates; matrix is $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & Sh_y \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
- ✅ For **composite transformations**, multiply matrices in **reverse order** of application: $M_{final} = M_{last} \times \cdots \times M_{second} \times M_{first}$.
- ✅ **Area change rule**: $\text{New Area} = \vert \det(M) \vert \times \text{Original Area}$.
- ✅ Reflection matrices are **involutory**: $M_R^2 = I$ (applying reflection twice returns the original).
- ✅ Shear matrices are **NOT involutory**: $M_{Sh}^2 \neq I$ (applying shear twice does not return the original).
- ✅ In KTU 2024 Scheme, **Part A** focuses on definitions, types, and simple 3-mark problems. **Part B** typically involves composite transformations with 2-3 sub-operations requiring full matrix calculations.
- ✅ Always show **step-by-step working** in KTU exams — partial marks are awarded for each correct transformation step.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composite Transformations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT416 - GRAPHICS AND MULTIMEDIA PRINCIPLES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Composite Transformations ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Composite Transformation** is a sequence of two or more elementary geometric transformations (such as translation, rotation, scaling, reflection, and shearing) that are combined into a **single equivalent matrix** by means of matrix multiplication. The result is one consolidated transformation matrix that, when applied to a point or object, produces the same final position as applying the individual transformations one after another. In **homogeneous coordinate representation**, every 2D point $(x, y)$ is represented as $(x, y, 1)$ and a 2D transformation is represented by a $3 \times 3$ matrix. The composite transformation matrix for a sequence of $n$ transformations $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n$ is computed as: $$P' = T_n \cdot T_{n-1} \cdot \ldots \cdot T_2 \cdot T_1 \cdot P$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 scheme, composite transformations are evaluated as a high-weightage topic. Students MUST be fluent in deriving pivot-point rotation, fixed-point scaling, and reflection about arbitrary lines using homogeneous matrix multiplication. The order of matrix multiplication is **NOT commutative**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a robot arm picking up a box from one shelf and placing it on another: 1. First, the arm **translates** to the box. 2. Then it **rotates** to align its gripper. 3. Then it **scales** (orients) to the right angle. 4. Finally, it **translates** the box to the new shelf. The final position of the box is the result of **all four motions combined**. Instead of calculating each motion step-by-step at runtime, we can mathematically "fold" all four motions into a **single super-instruction** — a composite transformation matrix. Just like you can pre-compute the total displacement, a composite matrix is a **pre-calculated shortcut** that bundles an entire animation pipeline into one elegant mathematical object. ### 1.3 Why Composite Transformations Matter > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** In **OpenGL, DirectX, and WebGL**, every frame your GPU renders involves thousands of composite transformations — the Model-View-Projection matrix in your shader is itself a composite of camera, perspective, and object transformations. Animation systems in **Unreal Engine** and **Unity** build skeletal hierarchies purely through composited rotation, translation, and scale matrices. **CAD/CAM software** like AutoCAD relies on composite transformations to allow users to mirror, rotate, and align engineering drawings with millimetre precision. ### 1.4 Key Terminology | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Homogeneous Coordinates** | A coordinate system extending 2D $(x, y)$ to 3D $(x, y, 1)$ so that translation becomes a linear operation. | | **Pivot Point** | A reference point about which a rotation or scaling is performed (instead of the origin). | | **Pre-multiplication** | Multiplying the current matrix on the **left** by a new transformation matrix $T$. | | **Post-multiplication** | Multiplying the current matrix on the **right** by a new transformation matrix $T$. | | **Column Vector Convention** | Points are written as column vectors; transformations are applied as $P' = M \cdot P$. | | **Row Vector Convention** | Points are written as row vectors; transformations are applied as $P' = P \cdot M$. | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The General Structure of a 2D Composite Transformation Every 2D affine transformation in homogeneous form can be written as a $3 \times 3$ matrix with the structure: $$ T_{\text{composite}} = \begin{bmatrix} a & b & t_x \\ c & d & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ The **upper-left $2 \times 2$ sub-matrix** $\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$ handles **linear operations** (rotation, scaling, shearing, reflection). The **upper-right $2 \times 1$ sub-matrix** $\begin{bmatrix} t_x \\ t_y \end{bmatrix}$ handles **translation**. The **bottom row is always** $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ to preserve the homogeneous 1 in the third coordinate. ### 2.2 The Three Building-Block Transformations **Translation** by $(t_x, t_y)$: $$ T(t_x, t_y) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Rotation** about the origin by angle $\theta$ (counter-clockwise positive): $$ R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Scaling** about the origin by factors $(s_x, s_y)$: $$ S(s_x, s_y) = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### 2.3 Critical Order of Matrix Multiplication > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Matrix multiplication is **NOT commutative**, i.e., $A \cdot B \neq B \cdot A$ in general. In the **column vector convention** ($P' = M \cdot P$), the **last transformation to be applied to the object is written on the LEFT-most side** of the matrix product, and the **first transformation is on the RIGHT-most side**. This is a frequent source of marks lost in KTU exams. The general pipeline is: $$ P' = T_n \cdot T_{n-1} \cdot \ldots \cdot T_1 \cdot P $$ where $T_1$ is applied first to $P$, then $T_2$, ..., and finally $T_n$ produces $P'$. ### 2.4 Composite Transformations Built From Pivot-Point Operations #### A. Rotation About an Arbitrary Point $(x_p, y_p)$ by Angle $\theta$ This is constructed as a **3-step sandwich**: 1. Translate the pivot to the origin: $T(-x_p, -y_p)$. 2. Rotate about the origin: $R(\theta)$. 3. Translate back: $T(x_p, y_p)$. $$ T_{\text{rot}} = T(x_p, y_p) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_p, -y_p) $$ #### B. Scaling About a Fixed Point $(x_f, y_f)$ by Factors $(s_x, s_y)$ Same **3-step sandwich** structure: 1. Translate fixed point to origin: $T(-x_f, -y_f)$. 2. Scale about origin: $S(s_x, s_y)$. 3. Translate back: $T(x_f, y_f)$. $$ T_{\text{scale}} = T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S(s_x, s_y) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f) $$ #### C. Reflection About an Arbitrary Line $y = mx + c$ The full derivation involves: 1. Translate so the line passes through the origin: $T(0, -c)$. 2. Rotate so the line coincides with the X-axis: $R(-\alpha)$ where $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(m)$. 3. Reflect about the X-axis: $M_x$. 4. Rotate back: $R(\alpha)$. 5. Translate back: $T(0, c)$. $$ T_{\text{ref}} = T(0, c) \cdot R(\alpha) \cdot M_x \cdot R(-\alpha) \cdot T(0, -c) $$ ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Operation | Composite Matrix Formula | |---|---|---| | 1 | Rotation about origin | $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | | 2 | Scaling about origin | $S(s_x, s_y) = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | | 3 | Translation | $T(t_x, t_y) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | | 4 | Rotation about pivot $(x_p, y_p)$ | $T(x_p, y_p) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_p, -y_p)$ | | 5 | Scaling about fixed point $(x_f, y_f)$ | $T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S(s_x, s_y) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$ | | 6 | Reflection about X-axis | $M_x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | | 7 | Reflection about Y-axis | $M_y = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | | 8 | Reflection about line $y=x$ | $M_{y=x} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | | 9 | Reflection about line $y=-x$ | $M_{y=-x} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | | 10 | Reflection about origin | $M_{o} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | | 11 | X-shear | $Sh_x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & sh_x & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | | 12 | Y-shear | $Sh_y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ sh_y & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | | 13 | General pivot rotation | $x' = x_p + (x - x_p)\cos\theta - (y - y_p)\sin\theta$ | | 14 | General pivot rotation | $y' = y_p + (x - x_p)\sin\theta + (y - y_p)\cos\theta$ | | 15 | General fixed-point scaling | $x' = x_f + (x - x_f) \cdot s_x$ | | 16 | General fixed-point scaling | $y' = y_f + (y - y_f) \cdot s_y$ | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Composite transformations power **medical imaging** (CT/MRI scan alignment), **robotics inverse kinematics** (multi-joint arm positioning), **satellite attitude control**, and **digital animation** (character rigging in Pixar/Disney pipelines). Every smooth rotation you see in a 3D game is a chain of composited quaternions and matrices. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Rotation About an Arbitrary Pivot Point $(x_p, y_p)$ **Goal:** Derive the composite $3 \times 3$ matrix for rotating a point $(x, y)$ about an arbitrary pivot $(x_p, y_p)$ by angle $\theta$. **Step 1:** Translate the pivot to the origin so that the new coordinates become: $$x' = x - x_p, \quad y' = y - y_p$$ In homogeneous form, the translation matrix is $T(-x_p, -y_p)$. **Step 2:** Apply rotation about the origin by angle $\theta$ using $R(\theta)$. **Step 3:** Translate back by $(x_p, y_p)$ to restore the original pivot position. The composite matrix is: $$ T_{\text{rot}} = T(x_p, y_p) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_p, -y_p) $$ **Step 4 (Explicit Matrix Multiplication):** Compute $R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_p, -y_p)$ first. $$ R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_p, -y_p) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_p \\ 0 & 1 & -y_p \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & -x_p\cos\theta + y_p\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & -x_p\sin\theta - y_p\cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ This represents the matrix after the **rotation** and the **back-shift of the pivot** in a single step. **Step 5:** Now pre-multiply by $T(x_p, y_p)$: $$ T(x_p, y_p) \cdot \left[R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_p, -y_p)\right] = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_p \\ 0 & 1 & y_p \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & -x_p\cos\theta + y_p\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & -x_p\sin\theta - y_p\cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x_p(1 - \cos\theta) + y_p\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y_p(1 - \cos\theta) - x_p\sin\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 6:** The final composite rotation matrix is therefore: $$ T_{\text{rot}}(x_p, y_p, \theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x_p(1 - \cos\theta) + y_p\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y_p(1 - \cos\theta) - x_p\sin\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 7 (Verification via direct coordinate equations):** Multiplying the above matrix by the homogeneous point $\begin{bmatrix} x & y & 1 \end{bmatrix}^T$ yields: $$ x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta + x_p(1 - \cos\theta) + y_p\sin\theta $$ $$ y' = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta + y_p(1 - \cos\theta) - x_p\sin\theta $$ Rearranging by grouping the original coordinates: $$ x' = x_p + (x - x_p)\cos\theta - (y - y_p)\sin\theta $$ $$ y' = y_p + (x - x_p)\sin\theta + (y - y_p)\cos\theta $$ These match the **classical KTU textbook pivot-rotation equations** — derivation verified. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Scaling About a Fixed Point $(x_f, y_f)$ **Goal:** Derive the composite matrix for scaling a point about a fixed point by factors $(s_x, s_y)$. **Step 1:** Translate fixed point to origin: $T(-x_f, -y_f)$. **Step 2:** Scale about the origin: $S(s_x, s_y)$. **Step 3:** Translate back: $T(x_f, y_f)$. $$ T_{\text{scale}} = T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S(s_x, s_y) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f) $$ **Step 4:** Compute $S(s_x, s_y) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$: $$ = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & -s_x x_f \\ 0 & s_y & -s_y y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 5:** Pre-multiply by $T(x_f, y_f)$: $$ = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & -s_x x_f \\ 0 & s_y & -s_y y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & x_f(1 - s_x) \\ 0 & s_y & y_f(1 - s_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 6 (Verification):** Applying to point $(x, y, 1)$: $$ x' = s_x \cdot x + x_f(1 - s_x) = x_f + s_x(x - x_f) $$ $$ y' = s_y \cdot y + y_f(1 - s_y) = y_f + s_y(y - y_f) $$ These are the standard KTU fixed-point scaling equations — derivation verified. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: General Reflection About the Line $y = mx + c$ **Goal:** Reflect a point $(x, y)$ about an arbitrary line $y = mx + c$. **Step 1:** Translate the line vertically by $-c$ so it passes through the origin (now it is $y = mx$): $$ T(0, -c) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & -c \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2:** Rotate by $-\alpha$ where $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(m)$ so the line $y = mx$ coincides with the X-axis: $$ R(-\alpha) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha & \sin\alpha & 0 \\ -\sin\alpha & \cos\alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3:** Reflect about the X-axis (negate the Y-coordinate): $$ M_x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 4:** Rotate back by $\alpha$: $$ R(\alpha) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha & 0 \\ \sin\alpha & \cos\alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 5:** Translate back by $+c$: $$ T(0, c) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & c \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 6:** The composite matrix is: $$ T_{\text{ref}} = T(0, c) \cdot R(\alpha) \cdot M_x \cdot R(-\alpha) \cdot T(0, -c) $$ This is the **master equation** for any reflection problem in the KTU syllabus. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does this 5-step sandwich work?** Geometrically, we "tame" the arbitrary line $y = mx + c$ by moving it to a known reference (the X-axis), perform the easy reflection there, then undo the taming. This same trick — translate-rotate-do-rotate⁻¹-translate⁻¹ — works for **any** transformation that is easy about the origin but hard about a general reference. ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) **Problem:** A triangle has vertices $A(1, 1)$, $B(5, 1)$, $C(3, 4)$. Rotate the triangle by $90°$ counter-clockwise about the pivot point $P(2, 2)$. Find the new coordinates. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify the parameters: $x_p = 2$, $y_p = 2$, $\theta = 90°$. For $\theta = 90°$: $\cos 90° = 0$ and $\sin 90° = 1$. **Step 2:** Plug into the general pivot-rotation equations: $$ x' = x_p + (x - x_p)\cos\theta - (y - y_p)\sin\theta $$ $$ y' = y_p + (x - x_p)\sin\theta + (y - y_p)\cos\theta $$ **Step 3:** Apply to $A(1, 1)$: $$ x'_A = 2 + (1 - 2)(0) - (1 - 2)(1) = 2 + 0 - (-1) = 2 + 1 = 3 $$ $$ y'_A = 2 + (1 - 2)(1) + (1 - 2)(0) = 2 + (-1) + 0 = 1 $$ So $A' = (3, 1)$. **Step 4:** Apply to $B(5, 1)$: $$ x'_B = 2 + (5 - 2)(0) - (1 - 2)(1) = 2 + 0 - (-1) = 3 $$ $$ y'_B = 2 + (5 - 2)(1) + (1 - 2)(0) = 2 + 3 + 0 = 5 $$ So $B' = (3, 5)$. **Step 5:** Apply to $C(3, 4)$: $$ x'_C = 2 + (3 - 2)(0) - (4 - 2)(1) = 2 + 0 - 2 = 0 $$ $$ y'_C = 2 + (3 - 2)(1) + (4 - 2)(0) = 2 + 1 + 0 = 3 $$ So $C' = (0, 3)$. **Final Answer:** The new triangle has vertices $A'(3, 1)$, $B'(3, 5)$, $C'(0, 3)$. ## 4.
