## 4.1 Architecture Flow of an Immersive Multimedia System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT SENSORS] --> B[TRACKING AND SLAM]
    B --> C[SCENE GRAPH AND WORLD MODEL]
    C --> D[RENDERING ENGINE]
    D --> E[DISPLAY PIPELINE]
    E --> F[USER PERCEPTION]
    F --> G[HAPTIC AND AUDIO FEEDBACK]
    G --> A
    C --> H[AI LAYER - GENERATIVE AND PREDICTIVE]
    H --> D
    C --> I[CLOUD AND EDGE SERVICES]
    I --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The diagram shows the *closed feedback loop* of an evolving multimedia system. Notice the **AI Layer** feeds the **Rendering Engine** (for content generation, denoising, upscaling), and **Cloud/Edge Services** offload heavy computation (ray tracing, NeRF training). This is the architecture behind **Apple Vision Pro** and **Meta Quest 3**.

---

## 4.2 The Reality-Virtuality Continuum Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[REAL ENVIRONMENT] --> A1[AR OVERLAY]
    A1 --> A2[AV REAL OBJECTS IN VIRTUAL]
    A2 --> V[FULL VIRTUAL REALITY]
    V --> X[EXTENDED REALITY UMBRELLA]
    A1 --> X
    A2 --> X
    R --> X
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Diagram Trick:** KTU often asks to *label* this continuum. Memorize the order: **Real → AR → AV → VR**, all subsumed by **XR**.

---

## 4.3 Generative AI Multimedia Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[PROMPT OR INPUT DATA] --> T[TEXT ENCODER - LLM]
    T --> LAT[LATENT SPACE]
    LAT --> DIF[DIFFUSION MODEL]
    DIF --> DEC[DECODER]
    DEC --> OUT[OUTPUT - IMAGE, VIDEO, AUDIO, 3D MESH]
    REF[RETRIEVAL AUGMENTED GENERATION] --> DIF
    FB[USER FEEDBACK LOOP] --> P
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Relevance:** Models like **Stable Diffusion**, **DALL·E 3**, **Sora**, **Runway Gen-3**, and **MusicGen** use this exact architecture. KTU 2024 Scheme includes a specific learning outcome on **AI-driven content synthesis**.

---

## 4.4 Edge vs Cloud Rendering Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{NETWORK LATENCY UNDER 10 MS} -->|YES| EDGE[EDGE RENDERING ON DEVICE]
    Q -->|NO| CL[CHECK CLOUD GPU AVAILABLE]
    CL -->|YES| CLOUD[CLOUD STREAMING RENDER]
    CL -->|NO| HYB[HYBRID - SPLIT RENDERING]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define Virtual Reality (VR). List any two VR applications in engineering education.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Virtual Reality (VR) is a computer-generated three-dimensional immersive environment that completely replaces the user's real-world perception. It uses **stereoscopic Head-Mounted Displays (HMDs)**, **spatial audio**, and **6 DoF tracking** to allow the user to interact with and navigate through a fully synthetic scene in real time.

**Applications in Engineering Education (1 mark — any two):**
1. **Virtual Labs** — Chemistry, Physics, and Electrical circuit experiments in a risk-free environment (e.g., Labster).
2. **CAD Walkthroughs** — Architecture students walking through their 3D BIM models before construction.
3. **Surgical Training** — Osso VR platform for medical students.
4. **Heavy Machinery Operation Simulators** — Crane, excavator training in civil and mechanical engineering.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]`
**Differentiate between Augmented Reality (AR) and Mixed Reality (MR) with a suitable example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | AR | MR |
|---|---|---|
| **Virtual-Real Interaction** | One-way overlay; virtual sits *on top* of real | Two-way; virtual objects *interact* with real (occlusion, physics) |
| **Spatial Awareness** | Limited; does not understand scene geometry | Full 3D spatial mapping |
| **Display Tech** | Smartphone screen, waveguide glasses | Passthrough stereo cameras + depth sensors |
| **Example** | Pokémon GO (digital Pokémon on real sidewalk) | Microsoft HoloLens 2 — a virtual CAD model sitting *on top* of a real engine, with proper occlusion and lighting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Tip:** 1.5 marks for the **table of differences**, 1 mark for **one correct example**, 0.5 marks for **concluding remark** that MR ⊂ AR conceptually but is more advanced.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Milgram Reality-Virtuality Continuum** in detail. With a neat diagram, classify **AR, AV, MR, and VR** along this continuum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a block diagram, describe the **architecture of a modern VR system**. Explain the role of **SLAM tracking**, **foveated rendering**, and **latency compensation (Asynchronous Spacewarp / Reprojection)** in ensuring VR comfort. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **Milgram Reality-Virtuality Continuum**, proposed by Paul Milgram and Fumio Kishino in 1994, is a taxonomy that classifies displays based on the proportion of **real-world content** versus **computer-generated (virtual) content** presented to the user. It ranges from the **pure real environment** (left end) to the **fully virtual environment** (right end), with hybrid displays in between.

**Classification (4 marks):**
- **Real Environment:** The unmodified physical world. No computer-generated content.
- **Augmented Reality (AR):** Virtual objects are *superimposed* on the real world. The user is still aware of the real environment. Example: Google Glass overlays navigation arrows on a transparent display.
- **Augmented Virtuality (AV):** Real objects are *inserted* into an otherwise virtual world. Example: A real-time video stream of a person composited into a 3D virtual conference room.
- **Mixed Reality (MR):** A subset of AR where virtual and real objects *coexist and interact* with proper occlusion, lighting, and physics.
- **Virtual Reality (VR):** A completely synthetic, immersive 3D environment. The user has no direct view of the real world. Example: Meta Quest 2 running Beat Saber.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the **Reality-Virtuality Continuum Flow** in Section 4.2.

---

### **Model Answer for Question A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to **Architecture Flow of an Immersive Multimedia System** in Section 4.1.

**SLAM Tracking (1.5 marks):**
**Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** is the algorithm that allows the HMD to *localize itself* in an unknown environment while simultaneously *building a map* of that environment. Modern inside-out VR headsets (Meta Quest 3, HoloLens 2) use **visual-inertial SLAM** — fusing camera data with IMU (gyroscope + accelerometer) readings — to achieve **sub-millimeter positional accuracy** at 90+ Hz.

**Foveated Rendering (1.5 marks):**
**Foveated rendering** exploits the non-uniform resolution of the human eye — the fovea (center of gaze) has the highest acuity, while the periphery is lower resolution. An **eye-tracker** identifies where the user is looking, and the GPU renders that region at **full resolution (e.g., 2160 × 2160 per eye)** while the periphery is rendered at **1/4 to 1/16 resolution**. This can reduce GPU load by **30–50%** with imperceptible quality loss.

**Latency Compensation (2 marks):**
Even with a 90 Hz display, the actual head movement occurs *between* frames. **Asynchronous Spacewarp (ASW)** and **Asynchronous Reprojection** are techniques that:
1. Predict the head's new position at the *next* frame using IMU extrapolation.
2. **Re-project** the previously rendered frame to the predicted new pose.
3. If the prediction is correct, the user perceives zero effective latency; if incorrect, the system applies a **re-projection warp** to minimize the error.

These techniques bring the perceived **motion-to-photon latency** down to **≤ 11 ms** even when the actual frame render time is **16 ms**.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** What is **Extended Reality (XR)**? Compare and contrast **AR, VR, and MR** in a tabular form across at least 6 parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **role of Generative AI** (Diffusion Models, LLMs, and Neural Radiance Fields — NeRFs) in **evolving multimedia content creation**. Mention at least two real-world tools and one ethical concern. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B (a) — 7 Marks**

**XR Definition (1.5 marks):**
**Extended Reality (XR)** is an *umbrella term* that encompasses all immersive technologies — **AR, VR, MR, and AV** — along with their hardware, software, and interaction paradigms. It is "reality extended" by digital overlays, sensory substitution, or full simulation. The term was popularized by industry (especially after 2016) as a unifying concept.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | VR | AR | MR |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Immersion Level** | Full | Partial | Partial-to-Full |
| **Real World Visibility** | None | Yes (with overlays) | Yes (with interaction) |
| **Display Type** | Opaque HMD | Transparent / Passthrough | Passthrough + spatial mapping |
| **Tracking** | 6 DoF | 3–6 DoF | 6 DoF + scene mesh |
| **Computational Load** | Very High | Moderate | Very High |
| **Typical Device** | Meta Quest 3, Valve Index | Smartphones, Google Glass | HoloLens 2, Magic Leap 2 |
| **Example Use Case** | Gaming, simulation | Navigation, retail try-on | Industrial design, surgery |

**Conclusion (1.5 marks):** XR represents the **convergence** of these paradigms. Future devices (e.g., Apple Vision Pro) are **multi-modal** — capable of switching between AR and VR modes — making strict classification increasingly fluid.

---

### **Model Answer for Question B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Generative AI Overview (2 marks):**
**Generative AI** refers to machine learning models that *create* new content (images, audio, video, 3D models) rather than merely classifying or recognizing existing content. In multimedia, the three most impactful paradigms are:

1. **Diffusion Models (2 marks):**
   Models like **Stable Diffusion**, **DALL·E 3**, and **Midjourney** learn to *reverse* a gradual noising process. Starting from pure Gaussian noise, they iteratively denoise to synthesize photorealistic images or video frames.
   - **Equation (simplified):** Given $x_t$ (noisy image at step $t$), the model learns $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)$ — the noise to subtract.
   - **Update:** $x_{t-1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}}(x_t - \frac{1-\alpha_t}{\sqrt{1-\alpha_t}}\,\epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)) + \sigma_t z$

2. **LLMs (1 mark):**
   Large Language Models (GPT-4, Llama, Gemini) enable **text-driven multimedia creation** — converting a natural language prompt into structured scene descriptions, animations, or even multimodal outputs (text + image + voice).

3. **NeRFs (1 mark):**
   **Neural Radiance Fields** represent a 3D scene as a continuous volumetric function $F_\theta: (x, y, z, \theta, \phi) \rightarrow (RGB, \sigma)$. From a sparse set of 2D photos, a NeRF synthesizes photorealistic novel views, enabling **3D scene reconstruction** from regular smartphone video.

**Real-World Tools (0.5 mark):**
- **Runway Gen-3** — text-to-video generation.
- **ElevenLabs** — AI voice cloning for multimedia localization.
- **Luma AI / Polycam** — NeRF-based 3D capture from phones.

**Ethical Concern (0.5 mark):**
**Deepfakes and misinformation** — Generative AI can fabricate convincing but false videos of real people (e.g., political deepfakes, non-consensual imagery), raising serious concerns about **consent, copyright, and societal trust**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing AR with MR** — *AR = overlay only*; *MR = real-virtual interaction with occlusion.* Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the formula units** — When asked to compute bandwidth or FoV, always state **units explicitly** (e.g., Gbps, degrees). Missing units = **0.5 mark loss**.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark questions** — A neat block diagram is **mandatory** for full marks. Use a labeled flowchart with **arrows showing data flow**.
> 4. **Writing vague definitions** — "VR is a virtual world" is **incomplete**. Always mention *immersion, stereoscopy, 6 DoF, and HMD*.
> 5. **Ignoring latency threshold** — A common 1-mark question asks *"What is the maximum acceptable motion-to-photon latency?"* Answer: **≤ 20 ms**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Evolving Technologies for Multimedia**

- **XR** is the umbrella term; **AR, VR, MR, AV** are its sub-classes.
- **VR** = Full immersion, opaque HMD, **≥ 90 Hz** refresh, **≤ 20 ms** latency.
- **AR** = Overlay only, no real-virtual interaction. Example: Pokémon GO.
- **MR** = Real-virtual interaction with occlusion & physics. Example: HoloLens 2.
- **Reality-Virtuality Continuum** — Milgram & Kishino (1994): **Real → AR → AV → VR**.
- **FoV formula:** $\text{FoV} = 2\arctan(d / 2f)$. Premium VR: **110°–130°**.
- **6 DoF = 3 translational + 3 rotational.** 3 DoF = rotation only.
- **SLAM** = simultaneous localization + mapping; backbone of inside-out tracking.
- **Foveated Rendering** reduces GPU load by **30–50%** using eye-tracking.
- **Asynchronous Spacewarp (ASW)** compensates for missed frames.
- **Bandwidth for stereo 4K @ 90 Hz = 24 bits/pixel ≈ 35.8 Gbps** uncompressed.
- **Diffusion Models** = state-of-the-art for image/video generation.
- **NeRFs** = neural representation of 3D scenes for novel view synthesis.
- **Ethical concerns** in generative AI: deepfakes, copyright, bias, hallucination.
- **Stereoscopic depth formula:** $D = b \cdot f / x_{disparity}$, where $b$ = IPD ≈ **64 mm**.
- **Human FoV:** Binocular ≈ **200°**, Monocular ≈ **150°**, Foveal high-acuity ≈ **2°**.
- **5-Stage Pipeline:** Sensors → Tracking → Scene Understanding → Rendering → Display.
- **Generative AI tools to memorize:** Stable Diffusion, DALL·E 3, Sora, Runway Gen-3, ElevenLabs, Luma AI.
- **Key engineering thresholds to remember:**
  - Refresh rate: **≥ 90 Hz**
  - Latency: **≤ 20 ms**
  - PPD for retina VR: **≥ 60 PPD**
  - IP range: **54–72 mm**
- **HMD = Head-Mounted Display.** **HMD uses two micro-displays, one per eye, with separate lens systems to create stereoscopic 3D.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defining Objects for Multimedia Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT416 - GRAPHICS AND MULTIMEDIA PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Defining Objects for Multimedia Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧩 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Multimedia Objects

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAIT416), a **Multimedia Object** is formally defined as a self-contained, addressable, and reusable software entity that encapsulates a specific media element (text, image, audio, video, or animation) along with its **attributes** (properties/data), **methods** (operations/behaviors), and **events** (interaction triggers). Objects follow the **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** paradigm and allow modular composition of complex multimedia applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> A multimedia object is *not* just a file (like `photo.jpg`). It is a *programmatic entity* that bundles data, operations on that data, and user-interaction logic into a single reusable unit.

The three foundational pillars of any multimedia object are:

| Pillar | Meaning | Multimedia Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Attributes (State)** | Data describing the object | Image width = 1920 px, bit depth = 24, color = RGB |
| **Methods (Behavior)** | Functions that act on the data | `play()`, `pause()`, `resize()`, `compress()` |
| **Events (Interface)** | Triggers for external interaction | `onClick`, `onLoad`, `onMouseOver` |

## 1.2 The Multimedia Object Class Hierarchy

Just as in OOP, multimedia objects are instantiated from **classes**. KTU emphasizes the following class taxonomy:

- **Super Class — `MultimediaObject`**: The abstract base class defining common attributes like `ID`, `name`, `size`, `format`, and methods like `display()` and `save()`.
- **Sub Classes** (specialized media types):
  - `TextObject` — hypertext, formatted text, streams.
  - `ImageObject` — bitmap, vector, raster graphics.
  - `AudioObject` — sampled sound, MIDI, synthesized audio.
  - `VideoObject` — full-motion sequences, compressed streams.
  - `AnimationObject` — keyframe, path-based, scripted motion.
- **Derived / Composite Classes** (combined media): `PresentationObject`, `WebObject`, `InteractiveMovieObject`.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The LEGO Block Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: Multimedia Objects are Smart LEGO Blocks**
>
> Imagine a child building a castle using LEGO blocks. Each block:
> - Has a **shape and color** (its *attributes*)
> - Can be **picked up, rotated, or stacked** (its *methods*)
> - May **click or light up** when pressed (its *events*)
>
> In the same way, a **video object** has a duration (attribute), a `play()` method, and an `onEnd` event. A multimedia application is essentially a *castle built from these intelligent, self-aware blocks*. Just as a LEGO block from one set can be reused in a completely different set, a multimedia object from one application can be reused in another, ensuring **modularity, scalability, and reusability**.

## 1.4 Why Define Objects in Multimedia?

The KTU 2024 scheme highlights four engineering motivations:

1. **Modularity** — Independent development and testing of media components.
2. **Reusability** — The same audio object can be reused across multiple presentations.
3. **Extensibility** — New media types (e.g., 360° video) can be added by creating new subclasses.
4. **Interoperability** — Objects from different vendors can interact through standard interfaces (COM, CORBA, JavaBeans).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Naming Convention:**
> Most KTU textbook references and the 2024 syllabus align with industry terminology: **OLE (Object Linking and Embedding)**, **COM (Component Object Model)**, **ActiveX**, **OpenDoc**, and **JavaBeans** — all of which are *frameworks for defining and using multimedia objects*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Multimedia Object — Operational Breakdown

A multimedia object is structured using a **three-tier definition model** (KTU 2024, Module 4):

- **Tier 1 — Identification Layer:** Object ID, name, version, media type, MIME type, URI locator.
- **Tier 2 — Data Layer:** The actual media payload (raw or compressed) plus descriptive metadata (EXIF for images, ID3 for audio).
- **Tier 3 — Interface Layer:** Exposed methods, properties, and event handlers callable by the host application.

### Why a Three-Tier Model?

- It **separates concerns** (data ≠ behavior ≠ identity) — a core OOP principle.
- It enables **late binding**: an object can be replaced at runtime without recompiling the host application.
- It supports **distributed multimedia**: the data tier can reside on a remote server, while the interface tier is invoked locally (e.g., streaming a video object from YouTube embedded in a PowerPoint).

## 2.2 Object Definition Methods in Multimedia Frameworks

The KTU syllabus explicitly identifies **three major object-definition standards** that students must know:

### A) Microsoft's COM / ActiveX / OLE
- **Component Object Model (COM)** defines objects via **interfaces** (groups of related methods).
- Each object implements one or more interfaces (e.g., `IUnknown`, `IDispatch`).
- **OLE (Object Linking and Embedding)** allows embedding one object inside another (Excel chart inside Word document).
- **ActiveX controls** are COM components designed for the web (now considered legacy in security-sensitive contexts).

### B) OpenDoc (Component Integration Laboratories — CIL)
- An open, cross-platform alternative promoted in the 1990s.
- Defined objects using **Part Editor** and **Container** model.
- Largely deprecated, but conceptually important for KTU exam questions.

### C) JavaBeans / CORBA
- **JavaBeans**: Reusable Java components defined via *getter/setter* naming conventions and *event listener* interfaces.
- **CORBA (Common Object Request Broker Architecture)**: Language-neutral objects described by **IDL (Interface Definition Language)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:**
> When asked to "define an object for multimedia," examiners expect students to mention: **(1) Attributes, (2) Methods, (3) Events, and (4) Class hierarchy** — and to cite at least one of COM/OLE/ActiveX/OpenDoc/JavaBeans as the underlying standard.

## 2.3 Object Composition Patterns

Multimedia objects rarely exist in isolation. KTU Module 4 specifies three composition patterns:

- **Aggregation (Has-A):** A `Presentation` object *has* a `Slide` object, which *has* a `TextObject`, `ImageObject`, and `AudioObject`. The contained object cannot exist meaningfully without the container.
- **Association (Uses-A):** A `WebPage` object *uses* a `StreamingVideo` object from a server. Loosely coupled.
- **Inheritance (Is-A):** `HighResolutionImage` *is a* specialized `ImageObject` that inherits all standard image attributes and adds `dpi` and `colorProfile`.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Although multimedia is a *qualitative* discipline, the KTU 2024 scheme requires quantitative literacy for object sizing. The following formulas appear in exam questions:

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{FileSize} = W \times H \times D \div 8$ | Raw (uncompressed) image size; $W$ = width, $H$ = height, $D$ = bit depth | Bytes |
| 2 | $\text{FileSize} = \dfrac{W \times H \times D \times (1 + \text{Header \%})}{8}$ | Image size including metadata/header overhead | Bytes |
| 3 | $\text{AudioSize} = S_r \times B \times C \times T$ | Audio object size; $S_r$ = sample rate, $B$ = bits/sample, $C$ = channels, $T$ = duration | Bits |
| 4 | $\text{VideoSize} = \text{FrameSize} \times \text{FPS} \times T \times \text{CompressionRatio}$ | Compressed video object size | Bytes |
| 5 | $\text{DataRate} = S_r \times B \times C$ | Real-time streaming rate (uncompressed audio) | bits/second (bps) |
| 6 | $\text{CompressionRatio} = \dfrac{\text{OriginalSize}}{\text{CompressedSize}}$ | Efficiency of codec | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{NyquistRate} = 2 \times f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling rate to reconstruct audio object losslessly | Hz |
| 8 | $\text{ObjectReuseFactor} = \dfrac{\text{TotalReuses}}{\text{TotalObjects}}$ | Measures modularity efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{TimeToTransfer} = \dfrac{\text{FileSize}}{\text{Bandwidth}}$ | Network/IO latency of an object | Seconds |
| 10 | $\text{DisplayPixels} = W \times H$ | Spatial resolution of a visual object | Pixels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas enable the multimedia engineer to **estimate storage, bandwidth, and latency requirements** *before* committing to a particular object design. For example, a 10-second 1080p video object at 30 FPS with H.264 compression ratio 50 yields a specific, predictable data footprint — critical for streaming, CDN design, and embedded systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantitative Definition of a Raw Image Object

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark sub-question):**
> *A `BitmapImage` object is defined with width 1024 pixels, height 768 pixels, and 24-bit color depth. Calculate the raw object size, and then the compressed size if a JPEG codec with compression ratio 10:1 is applied. Also estimate the network transfer time over a 4 Mbps link.*

**Step 1 — Identify Given Attributes**

From the object definition, the parameters are:

$$
W = 1024 \text{ pixels}, \quad H = 768 \text{ pixels}, \quad D = 24 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Pixel Count**

The total number of displayable pixels in the image object is the product of horizontal and vertical resolution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DisplayPixels} &= W \times H \\
&= 1024 \times 768 \\
&= 786{,}432 \text{ pixels}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Raw (Uncompressed) Object Size**

Each pixel stores $D$ bits. Total raw bits are converted to bytes by dividing by 8 (since **1 Byte = 8 bits**):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RawSize (bits)} &= \text{DisplayPixels} \times D \\
&= 786{,}432 \times 24 \\
&= 18{,}874{,}368 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RawSize (bytes)} &= \dfrac{18{,}874{,}368}{8} \\
&= 2{,}359{,}296 \text{ bytes} \\
&\approx 2.25 \text{ MB (using } 1 \text{ MB} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Compression Ratio**

A compression ratio of 10:1 means the compressed object is one-tenth the size of the raw object:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CompressedSize} &= \dfrac{\text{RawSize}}{\text{CompressionRatio}} \\
&= \dfrac{2{,}359{,}296}{10} \\
&= 235{,}929.6 \text{ bytes} \\
&\approx 230.4 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Estimate Network Transfer Time**

Bandwidth is given as 4 Mbps (megabits per second). Converting the compressed file to bits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CompressedSize (bits)} &= 235{,}929.6 \times 8 \\
&= 1{,}887{,}436.8 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TransferTime} &= \dfrac{\text{CompressedSize (bits)}}{\text{Bandwidth}} \\
&= \dfrac{1{,}887{,}436.8}{4 \times 10^6} \\
&\approx 0.4719 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** The defined `BitmapImage` object occupies **2.25 MB raw / 230.4 KB compressed** and transfers in **~0.47 s** over 4 Mbps. This is a *quantitative object definition* — combining attributes with engineering formulas to fully characterize a multimedia object.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quantitative Definition of an Audio Object

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> *Define an `AudioClip` object with: sample rate $S_r = 44.1$ kHz, bit depth $B = 16$ bits, $C = 2$ (stereo channels), duration $T = 3$ minutes. Calculate its uncompressed size in MB and its real-time data rate.*

**Step 1 — Identify Object Attributes**

$$
S_r = 44{,}100 \text{ Hz}, \quad B = 16 \text{ bits}, \quad C = 2, \quad T = 3 \times 60 = 180 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Samples**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TotalSamples} &= S_r \times C \times T \\
&= 44{,}100 \times 2 \times 180 \\
&= 15{,}876{,}000 \text{ samples}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Bits & Bytes**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AudioSize (bits)} &= \text{TotalSamples} \times B \\
&= 15{,}876{,}000 \times 16 \\
&= 254{,}016{,}000 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AudioSize (bytes)} &= \dfrac{254{,}016{,}000}{8} \\
&= 31{,}752{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\
&\approx 30.28 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Real-Time Data Rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DataRate} &= S_r \times B \times C \\
&= 44{,}100 \times 16 \times 2 \\
&= 1{,}411{,}200 \text{ bps} \\
&= 1.4112 \text{ Mbps} \quad \text{(CD-quality audio)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the famous **CD-DA (Red Book) standard rate of 1.4112 Mbps** — a perfect example of how an audio object's *quantitative definition* drives storage and bandwidth planning.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Object Attributes vs Media Types (KTU 2024 Reference Table)

| Object Class | Key Attributes | Common Methods | Typical Standards / Codecs |
|---|---|---|---|
| `TextObject` | font, size, color, style, language, charset | `format()`, `search()`, `spellCheck()` | ASCII, Unicode (UTF-8/16), RTF, HTML, XML |
| `ImageObject` | width, height, bit-depth, resolution, color-model | `resize()`, `crop()`, `compress()`, `filter()` | JPEG, PNG, GIF, BMP, TIFF, SVG |
| `AudioObject` | sample-rate, bit-depth, channels, duration | `play()`, `pause()`, `mix()`, `fadeIn()` | WAV, MP3, AAC, FLAC, OGG, MIDI |
| `VideoObject` | resolution, frame-rate, codec, bit-rate, duration | `play()`, `seek()`, `extractFrame()`, `trim()` | MPEG-2, MPEG-4 (H.264/AVC), H.265/HEVC, VP9, AV1 |
| `AnimationObject` | keyframes, paths, tween-rules, timeline | `start()`, `stop()`, `seek()`, `loop()` | SWF (legacy), CSS animations, Lottie, Spine JSON |
| `CompositeObject` (e.g., Presentation) | list of child objects, layout, transition | `addChild()`, `removeChild()`, `render()`, `export()` | PPTX, ODP, PDF, HTML5, SCORM |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📊 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Multimedia Object Class Hierarchy (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram renders the **class hierarchy** of multimedia objects as defined in the KTU 2024 syllabus. Node IDs follow the *alphanumeric-with-letter-prefix* rule to avoid Mermaid reserved-keyword collisions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[MMROOT: MultimediaObject]:::root
    B[TOBJ: TextObject]:::text
    C[IOBJ: ImageObject]:::image
    D[AOBJ: AudioObject]:::audio
    E[VOBJ: VideoObject]:::video
    F[NOBJ: AnimationObject]:::anim
    G[COBJ: CompositeObject]:::comp
    H[SOBJ: StreamingObject]:::stream
    I[RIMG: RasterImage]:::image
    J[VIMG: VectorImage]:::image
    K[PAUD: PCM Audio]:::audio
    L[MAUD: MIDI Audio]:::audio
    M[PRES: Presentation]:::comp
    N[WEBP: WebPage]:::comp
    O[CMPEG: MPEG Video]:::video
    P[CRAW: RAW Video]:::video

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    A --> F
    A --> G
    A --> H
    C --> I
    C --> J
    D --> K
    D --> L
    E --> O
    E --> P
    G --> M
    G --> N

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef text fill:#0d9488,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    classDef image fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#ffffff
    classDef audio fill:#dc2626,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    classDef video fill:#ea580c,stroke:#c2410c,color:#ffffff
    classDef anim fill:#ca8a04,stroke:#a16207,color:#ffffff
    classDef comp fill:#16a34a,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    classDef stream fill:#475569,stroke:#334155,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `MMROOT` is the **abstract superclass** containing common attributes (ID, size, format) and methods (`display()`, `save()`).
- `IOBJ`, `AOBJ`, `VOBJ`, `TOBJ`, `NOBJ` are the **five primary media subclasses** (Image, Audio, Video, Text, Animation).
- `COBJ` is a **composite** that aggregates any combination of primary objects.
- `SOBJ` (Streaming) is a specialized wrapper for network-delivered media.
- The further branches (`RIMG`/`VIMG`, `PAUD`/`MAUD`, `CMPEG`/`CRAW`) illustrate **two-level inheritance** as required by KTU Module 4.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Three-Tier Object Definition Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[TIER1: Identification Layer]:::id
    L2[TIER2: Data Layer]:::data
    L3[TIER3: Interface Layer]:::if
    APP[Host Application]:::app
    NET[Remote Network Server]:::net
    USER[End User]:::user

    L1 -->|ObjectID, Name, MIME, URI| L2
    L2 -->|Media payload + Metadata| L3
    L3 -->|Methods, Properties, Events| APP
    L2 -.->|Streamed bytes| NET
    APP -->|Renders, Interacts| USER
    L3 -.->|Late binding via COM/CORBA| APP

    classDef id fill:#1d4ed8,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    classDef data fill:#15803d,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    classDef if fill:#b45309,stroke:#92400e,color:#ffffff
    classDef app fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#581c87,color:#ffffff
    classDef net fill:#be123c,stroke:#9f1239,color:#ffffff
    classDef user fill:#0f766e,stroke:#115e59,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Identification Layer** assigns a unique handle to every object (`GUID` in COM terminology).
- The **Data Layer** can be local (file on disk) or remote (streamed from a server).
- The **Interface Layer** exposes only a contract — the host application *cannot* directly access internal data, enforcing **encapsulation**.
- The host application then presents the object to the end user through rendering and interaction.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Object Composition Pattern (Has-A / Uses-A / Is-A)

```mermaid
graph TD
    PRES[Presentation Composite Object]:::comp
    S1[Slide 1]:::child
    S2[Slide 2]:::child
    TXT[TextObject]:::text
    IMG[ImageObject]:::image
    AUD[AudioObject]:::audio
    VID[VideoObject Streamed]:::video
    HRI[HighResolutionImage]:::image
    SVR[Remote Streaming Server]:::net

    PRES -->|AGGREGATION Has-A| S1
    PRES -->|AGGREGATION Has-A| S2
    S1 -->|AGGREGATION Has-A| TXT
    S1 -->|AGGREGATION Has-A| IMG
    S1 -->|AGGREGATION Has-A| AUD
    S2 -->|ASSOCIATION Uses-A| VID
    IMG -->|INHERITANCE Is-A| HRI
    VID -.->|NETWORK FETCH| SVR

    classDef comp fill:#16a34a,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    classDef child fill:#65a30d,stroke:#4d7c0f,color:#ffffff
    classDef text fill:#0d9488,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    classDef image fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#ffffff
    classDef audio fill:#dc2626,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    classDef video fill:#ea580c,stroke:#c2410c,color:#ffffff
    classDef net fill:#be123c,stroke:#9f1239,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This single diagram captures *all three* composition relationships KTU examiners love to test:
> - **Aggregation** (`Presentation` → `Slide` → child media) = "Has-A"
> - **Association** (`Slide 2` → streamed `VideoObject`) = "Uses-A" (loose coupling, remote resource)
> - **Inheritance** (`HighResolutionImage` ⊂ `ImageObject`) = "Is-A" (specialization, reuses parent attributes/methods)

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAIT416]`
**Q: Define a multimedia object. List its three essential components with one multimedia example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **multimedia object** is a self-contained, reusable software entity that encapsulates a media element together with its data, behavior, and interaction logic, enabling modular construction of multimedia applications.
> Its three essential components are:
> 1. **Attributes (State)** — e.g., an `ImageObject` has `width = 1920 px`, `bitDepth = 24`, `colorModel = RGB`.
> 2. **Methods (Behavior)** — e.g., the same `ImageObject` exposes `resize()`, `crop()`, `compress()`.
> 3. **Events (Interface)** — e.g., the `ImageObject` fires `onLoad` and `onClick` to the host application.
> These three together implement the OOP principles of *encapsulation*, *abstraction*, and *reusability*.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAIT416]`
**Q: Differentiate between COM (Component Object Model) and JavaBeans as object-definition frameworks for multimedia.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | COM (Component Object Model) | JavaBeans |
|---|---|---|
| **Vendor / Platform** | Microsoft, Windows-centric | Sun Microsystems, cross-platform (JVM) |
| **Language Binding** | Language-neutral (binary standard) | Java-only |
| **Interface Definition** | Interface Definition Language (IDL-derived) | Java interfaces + introspection |
| **Method Naming** | Exposed via vtable / `IDispatch` | Follows `getXxx()` / `setXxx()` getter-setter convention |
| **Use in Multimedia** | OLE/ActiveX controls (legacy Windows media plug-ins) | Reusable media components in cross-platform Java apps |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently lose **2–3 marks** by forgetting to mention the **class hierarchy** (super-class / sub-class) when defining an object. Always show *where* the object fits in the inheritance tree.
> - Another common pitfall is to confuse **OOP terminology** ("encapsulation", "polymorphism") with *generic* descriptions. Examiners expect **definitions → examples → standards** in that exact order.
> - For numerical sub-questions, **unit conversions** (bits ↔ bytes, MB vs MiB) are silent mark-killers. Always write the conversion step explicitly.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAIT416]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of defining objects for multimedia systems. Describe the three-tier architecture (Identification, Data, Interface) used to define a multimedia object with a suitable example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A `BitmapImage` object is defined with width 1920 pixels, height 1080 pixels, and a 24-bit color depth. If a JPEG compression with a 15:1 ratio is applied and the compressed object is to be streamed over a 5 Mbps link, calculate: (i) the raw image size in MB, (ii) the compressed size in KB, and (iii) the transfer time in seconds.

#### Model Solution to (a):

The **definition of objects for multimedia systems** is rooted in Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), where each media element is treated as a discrete, encapsulated entity. The KTU 2024 scheme adopts a **three-tier architecture** for formally defining a multimedia object:

- **Tier 1 — Identification Layer:** Provides the object's *identity* to the outside world. Includes the unique object ID (a GUID in COM or a UUID in CORBA), the human-readable name, the MIME type (e.g., `image/jpeg`), and the URI/locator pointing to the data.
- **Tier 2 — Data Layer:** Contains the actual media payload (raw or compressed bytes) and descriptive metadata (e.g., EXIF for images, ID3 tags for MP3 audio). This layer may reside locally or remotely.
- **Tier 3 — Interface Layer:** Exposes the object's *contract* — its public methods (e.g., `play()`, `resize()`), its properties (e.g., `duration`, `width`), and its event callbacks (e.g., `onLoad`, `onEnd`).

**Example — Defining a `StreamingVideo` object:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tier 1 (ID)} &: \quad \text{ID} = \text{"VID\_2024\_Kerala"}, \ \text{MIME} = \text{"video/mp4"}, \ \text{URI} = \text{"https://ktu.in/vid.mp4"} \\
\text{Tier 2 (Data)} &: \quad \text{Codec} = \text{H.264}, \ \text{Resolution} = 1280 \times 720, \ \text{Duration} = 120 \text{ s} \\
\text{Tier 3 (Interface)} &: \quad \text{Methods} = \{\text{play()}, \text{pause()}, \text{seek(t)}\}; \ \text{Events} = \{\text{onStart}, \text{onEnd}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

This architectural separation provides **encapsulation** (host apps cannot tamper with internal data), **late binding** (objects can be replaced at runtime), and **distributed delivery** (Tier 2 may be on a remote server).

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $W = 1920$, $H = 1080$, $D = 24$ bits, Compression Ratio = 15, Bandwidth = 5 Mbps.

**(i) Raw Size Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DisplayPixels} &= 1920 \times 1080 = 2{,}073{,}600 \text{ pixels} \\
\text{RawSize (bits)} &= 2{,}073{,}600 \times 24 = 49{,}766{,}400 \text{ bits} \\
\text{RawSize (bytes)} &= \dfrac{49{,}766{,}400}{8} = 6{,}220{,}800 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{RawSize (MB)} &= \dfrac{6{,}220{,}800}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx \mathbf{5.93 \text{ MB}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Compressed Size Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CompressedSize (bytes)} &= \dfrac{6{,}220{,}800}{15} = 414{,}720 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{CompressedSize (KB)} &= \dfrac{414{,}720}{1024} \approx \mathbf{404.98 \text{ KB}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Transfer Time Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CompressedSize (bits)} &= 414{,}720 \times 8 = 3{,}317{,}760 \text{ bits} \\
\text{TransferTime} &= \dfrac{3{,}317{,}760}{5 \times 10^6} \approx \mathbf{0.6636 \text{ seconds}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answers:** (i) **5.93 MB**, (ii) **~405 KB**, (iii) **~0.66 s**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAIT416]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat block diagrams, explain the multimedia object class hierarchy. Discuss **Inheritance**, **Aggregation**, and **Association** relationships with multimedia examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An `AudioClip` object has the following attributes: $S_r = 48$ kHz, $B = 16$ bits, $C = 2$ (stereo), $T = 5$ minutes. Calculate (i) the uncompressed audio size in MB and (ii) the real-time playback data rate in kbps. If the audio is compressed using AAC at 128 kbps, find (iii) the compressed file size and (iv) the bandwidth saved as a percentage of the original.

#### Model Solution to (a):

A multimedia object's class hierarchy is best understood as a **root superclass** with multiple **specialized subclasses**, applying the three composition relationships:

- **Inheritance (Is-A):** A `HighResolutionImage` *is a* specialized `ImageObject` — it inherits all standard attributes (`width`, `height`, `bitDepth`) and adds new ones (`dpi`, `colorProfile`). Similarly, `PCM_Audio` and `MIDI_Audio` inherit from `AudioObject`.
- **Aggregation (Has-A):** A `Presentation` object *has* multiple `Slide` objects; each `Slide` *has* a `TextObject`, `ImageObject`, and `AudioObject`. The contained object has no independent existence outside the container.
- **Association (Uses-A):** A `WebPage` object *uses* a `StreamingVideo` object hosted on a remote server. The video exists independently of the web page — the coupling is loose.

A block diagram showing this hierarchy (refer to **Section 4.1 Mermaid Diagram** above) makes the relationships visually unambiguous and is highly valued by KTU examiners.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $S_r = 48{,}000$ Hz, $B = 16$ bits, $C = 2$, $T = 5 \times 60 = 300$ seconds.

**(i) Uncompressed Audio Size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TotalSamples} &= 48{,}000 \times 2 \times 300 = 28{,}800{,}000 \\
\text{AudioSize (bits)} &= 28{,}800{,}000 \times 16 = 460{,}800{,}000 \text{ bits} \\
\text{AudioSize (bytes)} &= \dfrac{460{,}800{,}000}{8} = 57{,}600{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{AudioSize (MB)} &= \dfrac{57{,}600{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx \mathbf{54.93 \text{ MB}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Real-Time Data Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DataRate} &= S_r \times B \times C \\
&= 48{,}000 \times 16 \times 2 \\
&= 1{,}536{,}000 \text{ bps} = \mathbf{1536 \text{ kbps}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Compressed Size at 128 kbps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CompressedSize (bits)} &= 128{,}000 \times 300 = 38{,}400{,}000 \text{ bits} \\
\text{CompressedSize (bytes)} &= \dfrac{38{,}400{,}000}{8} = 4{,}800{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{CompressedSize (MB)} &= \dfrac{4{,}800{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx \mathbf{4.58 \text{ MB}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Bandwidth Saved (%):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Saving} &= \dfrac{\text{OriginalRate} - \text{CompressedRate}}{\text{OriginalRate}} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{1536 - 128}{1536} \times 100\% \\
&\approx \mathbf{91.67\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answers:** (i) **54.93 MB**, (ii) **1536 kbps**, (iii) **4.58 MB**, (iv) **~91.67% bandwidth saved**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **multimedia object** bundles **data + behavior + interface** into a single reusable unit, following OOP principles.
- [x] The **three components** of every multimedia object are: **Attributes (state)**, **Methods (behavior)**, and **Events (interface)**.
- [x] The KTU 2024 **three-tier object definition** consists of: **Identification Layer**, **Data Layer**, and **Interface Layer**.
- [x] Major **object-definition standards**: **COM / OLE / ActiveX** (Microsoft), **OpenDoc** (CIL, deprecated), **JavaBeans**, and **CORBA with IDL**.
- [x] Three **composition patterns** to remember:
  - **Aggregation (Has-A)** — strict ownership; child dies with parent.
  - **Association (Uses-A)** — loose link; can be remote.
  - **Inheritance (Is-A)** — subclass reuses and extends superclass.
- [x] The **superclass `MultimediaObject`** has five primary subclasses: `TextObject`, `ImageObject`, `AudioObject`, `VideoObject`, `AnimationObject`.
- [x] **Quantitative formulas** (high-yield for 14-mark problems):
  - Image size: $W \times H \times D \div 8$.
  - Audio size: $S_r \times B \times C \times T$.
  - Video size: $\text{FrameSize} \times \text{FPS} \times T \div \text{CompressionRatio}$.
  - Data rate: $S_r \times B \times C$.
- [x] **Unit conversions are mandatory**: 1 byte = 8 bits; 1 MB = $1{,}048{,}576$ bytes (use MiB if Mi-based, but KTU usually accepts $10^6$).
- [x] **Nyquist rate** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ governs the minimum sampling rate for lossless audio object reconstruction (e.g., 44.1 kHz for 20 kHz human hearing).
- [x] **Industry examples** of object definition: COM GUIDs, OLE embedded Excel-in-Word, ActiveX media plug-ins, JavaBeans with getter/setter, CORBA IDL contracts.
- [x] **Engineering benefits** of object-based multimedia: **modularity, reusability, extensibility, interoperability**, plus support for **distributed and streaming media**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever the question says *"define an object for multimedia"*, immediately write a four-line response:
> 1. Definition (1 line).
> 2. List the three components: attributes, methods, events.
> 3. Mention the class hierarchy (superclass → subclasses).
> 4. Name at least one standard (COM / OLE / JavaBeans).
> This four-line structure alone secures 4 of 7 marks in the typical 14-mark sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multimedia Data Interface Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT416 - GRAPHICS AND MULTIMEDIA PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multimedia Data Interface Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multimedia Data Interface Standards** are formally defined, internationally ratified technical specifications that govern the **encoding, transmission, storage, synchronization, and presentation** of heterogeneous multimedia payloads (audio, video, graphics, text, and metadata) across heterogeneous hardware, software, and network platforms.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, this term encompasses three orthogonal sub-domains:

1. **Peripheral / Hardware Interface Standards** — Physical/electrical link layers (e.g., HDMI, DVI, DisplayPort, USB, IEEE 1394).
2. **Compression / Coding Standards** — Algorithmic payload reduction (e.g., JPEG family, MPEG family, H.26x series).
3. **Application / Control Protocols** — Logical message exchange for music, streaming, conferencing (e.g., MIDI, RTP/RTSP, H.323, VRML/X3D).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Multilingual Airport Terminal"**
> Imagine an international airport where passengers (data packets) speaking many languages (formats: PCM, RGB, MIDI, MPEG-TS) must move between aircraft (devices: TV, speaker, PC, synth). The airport needs three sets of rules:
> - **Runway & Gate rules (Hardware interfaces)** — Where each plane parks and refuels (HDMI carries both audio+video over one plug).
> - **Luggage compression rules (Coding standards)** — How to shrink bags so the plane carries more (JPEG, MPEG compress raw media).
> - **Boarding announcement language (Application protocols)** — A common tongue between pilot and ground crew (MIDI messages, RTSP commands).
>
> Without these standards, every device would need a custom translator — chaos. Standards = **universal translators** for multimedia.

### 1.3 Standardization Bodies (must memorize)

| Body | Full Name | Domain |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **ITU-T** | International Telecommunication Union — Telecom | H.26x video, H.32x conferencing |
| **ISO/IEC** | International Org. for Standardization / IEC | MPEG family, JPEG, MHEG |
| **IEC** | International Electrotechnical Commission | Audio engineering (in joint committees) |
| **VESA** | Video Electronics Standards Association | DisplayPort, DMT timings |
| **DDWG** | Digital Display Working Group | DVI |
| **USB-IF** | USB Implementers Forum | USB family |
| **1394TA** | 1394 Trade Association | FireWire |
| **HDMI Forum / LA** | HDMI Licensing Administrator | HDMI |
| **MMA** | MIDI Manufacturers Association | MIDI |
| **W3C** | World Wide Web Consortium | SVG, X3D, WebGL |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"ITU shoots Video, ISO packs Bytes, VESA paints Pixels, USB drives Data."*

### 1.4 Taxonomy of Standards (Big Picture)

```
Multimedia Data Interface Standards
├── 1. Perceptual Coding (Payload Compression)
│    ├── Still Image: JPEG, JPEG 2000, JPEG XR, WebP
│    ├── Audio:      MP3 (MPEG-1 L3), AAC, FLAC, Opus
│    ├── Video:      MPEG-1/2/4, H.261, H.263, H.264/AVC, H.265/HEVC, AV1
│    └── Metadata:   MPEG-7, EXIF, ID3
├── 2. Hardware / Connector Standards
│    ├── Video:      DVI, HDMI, DisplayPort, VGA (legacy)
│    ├── Data Bus:   USB 1.1/2.0/3.x/4, IEEE 1394 (FireWire), Thunderbolt
│    └── Storage:    SCSI, SATA, NVMe
├── 3. Application / Control Protocols
│    ├── Music:      MIDI 1.0 / 2.0
│    ├── 3D Web:     VRML, X3D
│    ├── Streaming:  RTP, RTSP, RTMP, HLS, DASH
│    └── Conf.:      H.320, H.323, H.324, SIP
└── 4. Container / File Formats (often grouped)
     ├── A/V:        AVI, MP4 (MPEG-4 Part 14), MKV, MOV, FLV
     └── Still:      BMP, PNG, TIFF, GIF
```

### 1.5 Why Standards Matter (Engineering Rationale)

- **Interoperability** — Any HDMI source drives any HDMI sink.
- **Economies of Scale** — One ASIC supports millions of devices.
- **Compression Efficiency** — H.265 halves H.264 bitrate at equal quality.
- **Quality of Service (QoS)** — Isochronous channels (USB, 1394) guarantee bandwidth.
- **Future-proofing** — Backward compatibility (USB 3.2 accepts USB 2.0).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 MIDI — Musical Instrument Digital Interface

#### 2.1.1 Origin & Physical Layer

- Released **1983** by Dave Smith (Sequential Circuits) and Ikutaro Kakehashi (Roland).
- **Asynchronous serial** protocol at **31.25 kbps** ± 1 %, **8-N-1** framing (1 start, 8 data, no parity, 1 stop bit).
- 5-pin **DIN connector** (later USB-MIDI, MIDI 2.0 over RTP-MIDI).
- **Current loop** at **5 mA** → optically isolated, robust up to ~15 m.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 31.25 kbps?** Because the standard divides the 1 MHz master clock of early synthesizers by 32 — yielding exactly 31,250 baud, which is reliably handled by cheap UARTs of the era.

#### 2.1.2 MIDI Message Hierarchy

```
MIDI Byte Stream
├── Status Byte   (bit 7 = 1)  → identifies message type & channel
└── Data Byte(s)  (bit 7 = 0)  → 0–127 parameter values
```

- **Status byte layout:** `1 ccc nnnn`
  - `1` = status flag
  - `ccc` = channel (0–15)
  - `nnnn` = message type (Note-Off, Note-On, Poly Pressure, Control Change, Program Change, Channel Pressure, Pitch Bend, System)

- **Three message families:**
  1. **Channel Voice Messages** (status 0x80–0xEF) — actual musical performance data.
  2. **Channel Mode Messages** (status 0xB0–0xBF with controller # 120–127) — configure the receiving channel (e.g., All Notes Off, Omni, Poly/Mono).
  3. **System Messages** (no channel; status 0xF0–0xFF):
     - **System Common** (0xF1, 0xF2, 0xF3, 0xF6) — Time Code, Song Position, Song Select, Tune Request.
     - **System Real-Time** (0xF8, 0xFA–0xFF) — Clock, Start, Continue, Stop, Active Sensing, Reset.
     - **System Exclusive (SysEx)** (0xF0 … 0xF7) — Manufacturer-proprietary dumps (e.g., patch memory).

#### 2.1.3 General MIDI (GM) Sound Bank

Defines **128 programs** (instruments) and **47 drum kits** on channel **10** (1-based) so a GM file plays the same piano on any GM-compliant synth.

#### 2.1.4 MIDI 2.0 (2020) — Enhancements

- 32-bit data words (vs 7-bit nibbles).
- Per-note controllers, polyphonic key pressure, MIDI-CI (Capability Inquiry).
- Auto-configuration, profile selection.

#### 2.1.5 MIDI Data Rate Calculation

$$
\text{Bytes per second} = \frac{31\,250\ \text{bits/s}}{10\ \text{bits/byte}} = 3\,125\ \text{B/s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Status byte 0xF0** (SysEx Start) is followed by a **variable-length manufacturer ID**, then arbitrary data, terminated by **0xF7 (End of SysEx)**. SysEx is how samplers dump complete waveform memory.

---

### 2.2 JPEG — Joint Photographic Experts Group

#### 2.2.1 Operating Modes

| Mode | Compression | Use Case |
|------|-------------|----------|
| **Baseline Sequential (Lossy)** | DCT-based | Web photos |
| **Progressive** | DCT, multi-pass | Web thumbnails |
| **Lossless** | Predictive | Medical, archival |
| **Hierarchical** | Multi-resolution | Print pipelines |

#### 2.2.2 Baseline JPEG Pipeline (Lossy)

1. **Color Space Conversion** — RGB → YCbCr (ITU-R BT.601).
2. **Chroma Subsampling** — 4:2:0 (most common), 4:2:2, 4:4:4.
3. **Block Splitting** — Image tiled into 8 × 8 pixel blocks.
4. **Level Shift** — Pixel values 0–255 shifted to **−128 to +127** (centered).
5. **2-D Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)** — 64 coefficients per block.
6. **Quantization** — Each of 64 coefficients divided by a **quantization table value** (lossy step).
7. **Entropy Coding** — DC coefficient DPCM + AC zig-zag + **Huffman** (or arithmetic in extension).

#### 2.2.3 JPEG 2000 (ISO/IEC 15444)

- Uses **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)** with 5/3 (lossless) or 9/7 (lossy) wavelets.
- **EBCOT** (Embedded Block Coding with Optimized Truncation) entropy coder.
- Supports **ROI** (Region of Interest), **tiling**, **scalability** (resolution, quality, spatial).
- File extensions: **.jp2, .j2k, .jpx**.

#### 2.2.4 Compression Ratio Formula

$$
CR = \frac{\text{Uncompressed size}}{\text{Compressed size}} = \frac{W \times H \times b_{\text{orig}}}{S_{\text{comp}}}
$$

Where $W, H$ = width/height in pixels, $b_{\text{orig}}$ = bits per pixel uncompressed, $S_{\text{comp}}$ = compressed file size in bits.

---

### 2.3 MPEG — Moving Picture Experts Group

#### 2.3.1 Standard Timeline (Memory Aid)

| Standard | Year | Domain | Bitrate Target | Trademark Use |
|----------|------|--------|----------------|---------------|
| **MPEG-1** | 1993 | CD-ROM Video, MP3 | ≤ **1.5 Mbps** | Video CD, MP3 |
| **MPEG-2** | 1995 | SDTV/HDTV, DVD | **4–15 Mbps** | DVD, DVB |
| **MPEG-4** Part 2 | 1999 | Streaming | Variable | DivX, Xvid |
| **MPEG-4** Part 10 (AVC) | 2003 | HD streaming | Half of MPEG-2 | H.264 |
| **MPEG-7** | 2002 | Metadata | n/a | Content description |
| **MPEG-21** | 2004 | Framework | n/a | DRM, IPMP |

> [!NOTE]
> **MP3 = MPEG-1 Audio Layer III.** The "3" refers to the perceptual audio codec layer, NOT MPEG-3 (which does not exist).

#### 2.3.2 MPEG-1 / -2 / -4 Video Coding Architecture (Common)

```
Raw Video Frame
   ↓
Motion Estimation / Compensation (I, P, B frames)
   ↓
8×8 DCT
   ↓
Quantization
   ↓
Zig-Zag Scan + Run-Length
   ↓
Variable-Length Coding (Huffman)
   ↓
Bitstream
```

- **I-frame (Intra)**: Self-contained, JPEG-like, used as anchor. ~1 per second.
- **P-frame (Predicted)**: Forward motion-compensated from previous I/P.
- **B-frame (Bidirectional)**: Interpolated from two reference frames — highest compression, highest latency.

#### 2.3.3 MPEG-4 Object-Based Coding

Treats the scene as a composition of **audio-visual objects (AVOs)**:
- **Video Object Plane (VOP)** — arbitrary-shaped region.
- **BIFS** (Binary Format for Scenes) — composes AVOs into a scene graph.
- Enables **sprite** coding, **scalable** coding, **face/body animation** (SNHC).

#### 2.3.4 Uncompressed Video Bit-Rate Formula (Important for Coding ESE)

$$
R = W \times H \times f \times b_{\text{depth}} \times \chi
$$

Where $R$ = bit rate (bps), $W \times H$ = frame size (pixels), $f$ = frame rate (fps), $b_{\text{depth}}$ = bits per pixel per component, $\chi$ = chroma subsampling factor (e.g., 4:2:0 ⇒ 1.5; 4:4:4 ⇒ 3.0; 4:2:2 ⇒ 2.0 — for three components RGB/YCbCr).

**Worked Example:** 1920 × 1080, 30 fps, 8-bit, 4:2:0:

$$
R = 1920 \times 1080 \times 30 \times 8 \times 1.5 \approx 746.5\ \text{Mbps}
$$

If compressed by H.264 to **5 Mbps**, compression ratio $\approx 149$ : 1.

---

### 2.4 DVI — Digital Visual Interface

- **DDWG** (Digital Display Working Group), ratified **1999**.
- Replaces VGA for digital flat panels.
- **TMDS** (Transition Minimized Differential Signaling) link — minimum differential signalling ⇒ reduced EMI.

#### 2.4.1 Pin Groups

- **Single-Link DVI**: 3 TMDS data channels (R, G, B) + 1 clock channel ⇒ up to **165 MHz pixel clock** ⇒ max **1920 × 1200 @ 60 Hz**.
- **Dual-Link DVI**: 6 data + 1 clock ⇒ up to **330 MHz** ⇒ up to **2560 × 1600 @ 60 Hz** (WQXGA).

#### 2.4.2 TMDS Link Bit-Rate

$$
R_{\text{TMDS}} = P_{\text{clk}} \times 10\ \text{bits/pixel}
$$

(each pixel carries 8-bit colour encoded as 10-bit TMDS character using a lookup table that minimizes transitions).

---

### 2.5 HDMI — High-Definition Multimedia Interface

- Introduced **December 2002**; consortium of Hitachi, Panasonic, Philips, Silicon Image, Sony, Thomson, Toshiba.
- **Backward compatible with DVI** (digital video plane).
- Carries **uncompressed video + audio + control** on a single cable.

#### 2.5.1 Version Ladder (Key for KTU)

| Version | Year | Max Throughput | Max Resolution | Key Feature |
|---------|------|----------------|----------------|-------------|
| 1.0 | 2002 | 4.95 Gbps | 1080p60 | — |
| 1.4 | 2009 | 10.2 Gbps | 4K @ 30 Hz | **Ethernet channel, ARC, 3D** |
| 2.0 | 2013 | 18 Gbps | 4K @ 60 Hz | Wide color gamut, BT.2020 |
| 2.1 | 2017 | 48 Gbps | **8K @ 60 Hz, 4K @ 120 Hz** | **eARC, VRR, ALLM, DSC** |

#### 2.5.2 TMDS Channel Count

- **HDMI 1.0–1.3**: 3 TMDS data channels (like DVI single-link).
- **HDMI 1.4+**: Same 3 channels, higher per-channel rate, plus HEAC (HDMI Ethernet/Audio Return Channel).
- **HDMI 2.1**: 4 TMDS data lanes + dedicated clock ⇒ 48 Gbps.

#### 2.5.3 Useful Features

- **CEC** (Consumer Electronics Control) — single-remote control of all devices (one wire).
- **ARC / eARC** — TV sends audio back to soundbar without a separate optical cable.
- **HDCP** (High-bandwidth Digital Content Protection) — mandatory copy protection handshake (2.2+ for 4K).
- **EDID** (Extended Display Identification Data) — display tells source its native resolution/capabilities over DDC line.

---

### 2.6 USB — Universal Serial Bus

| Version | Year | Theoretical | Logo | Notes |
|---------|------|-------------|------|-------|
| 1.0 / 1.1 | 1996 / 1998 | **1.5 / 12 Mbps** | — | Low/Full speed |
| 2.0 | 2000 | **480 Mbps** | Hi-Speed | OTG introduced |
| 3.0 | 2008 | **5 Gbps** | SuperSpeed | Full-duplex, new pins |
| 3.1 | 2013 | **10 Gbps** | SuperSpeed+ | Type-C connector |
| 3.2 | 2017 | **20 Gbps** | SuperSpeed 20 | Multi-lane |
| 4.0 | 2019 | **40 Gbps** | USB4 | Tunneled PCIe/DP |

#### 2.6.1 Transfer Types (Critical for Multimedia)

- **Control** — device enumeration, low-bandwidth.
- **Bulk** — large error-free data (printers, storage).
- **Interrupt** — small, latency-sensitive (HID, keyboards).
- **Isochronous** — **guaranteed bandwidth, real-time, may drop** — used for **audio, video, VoIP**.
- **USB 3.0 adds Streams** — multiple bulk endpoints sharing bandwidth.

#### 2.6.2 Isochronous Bandwidth Allocation

USB allocates 80 % of microframe to periodic (isochronous + interrupt) traffic. A USB 2.0 high-speed microframe = **125 µs** ⇒ periodic budget ≈ 100 µs ⇒ max **~24.576 Mbps** for isochronous endpoints.

---

### 2.7 IEEE 1394 — FireWire / i.LINK

- Apple-led standard, **1995**.
- Speeds: **100 / 200 / 400 / 800 Mbps** (S100/S200/S400/S800).
- **Peer-to-peer**, hot-plug, **64-bit fixed addressing** (1024 buses × 63 nodes × 2^48 bytes).
- Two transfer classes critical for multimedia:
  - **Isochronous** — guaranteed 80 % of bus bandwidth; up to 4096 channels of 125 µs packets. **Used for DV video, audio, MIDI.**
  - **Asynchronous** — guaranteed delivery, retry; used for control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off:** USB = host-centric, cheaper; FireWire = peer-to-peer, real-time guarantees, more expensive. FireWire dominated DV camcorders (e.g., MiniDV tape transfer).

---

### 2.8 Video Conferencing Standards (H.32x Suite)

| Standard | Network | Year | Use |
|----------|---------|------|-----|
| **H.320** | ISDN | 1990 | Legacy enterprise rooms |
| **H.323** | IP (LAN/Internet) | 1996 | **Most deployed VoIP/VC** |
| **H.324** | PSTN (analog) | 1996 | Modem videophones |
| **H.310** | ATM | 1996 | B-ISDN |
| **H.321** | ATM/STM | 1996 | Adaptation of H.320 |
| **SIP** | IP | RFC 3261 (2002) | Modern session signaling |

H.323 stack components: **H.225** (RAS + Q.931 call signaling), **H.245** (capability exchange), **H.26x** (video), **G.7xx** (audio).

---

### 2.9 Network Streaming Standards

| Protocol | Layer | Function |
|----------|-------|----------|
| **RTP** (RFC 3550) | UDP | Real-time transport, timestamps, sequence nos |
| **RTCP** | UDP | QoS feedback, sync |
| **RTSP** (RFC 2326) | TCP | VCR-like session control (PLAY, PAUSE) |
| **RTMP** | TCP | Legacy Flash streaming |
| **HLS** | HTTP/TCP | Apple adaptive bitrate (m3u8 playlists) |
| **MPEG-DASH** | HTTP/TCP | ISO/IEC 23009 — standard ABR |

---

### 2.10 3-D Web Standards

- **VRML 1.0 (1994) / VRML 2.0 / VRML97** — text-based scene description.
- **X3D** (2004) — XML-encoded successor; components: Core, CAD, Geo, H-Anim, Sound, DIS, Scripting.
- **WebGL** — JavaScript binding to **OpenGL ES 2.0** (not a "standard" file format, but a W3C-ratified API).

---

### 2.11 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Domain | Formula / Constant | Meaning / Use |
|--------|--------------------|---------------|
| MIDI baud | $31\,250\ \text{bits/s}$ | 8-N-1 framing ⇒ 3,125 B/s |
| MIDI time code | $24 / 25 / 29.97 / 30\ \text{fps}$ | Quarter-frame messages |
| JPEG block | $8 \times 8 = 64$ DCT coefficients | One MCU |
| MPEG-1 audio | Layer III → **MP3** | 32–320 kbps |
| MPEG-2 video | $4{:}2{:}0 ⇒ 12\ \text{bits/pixel}$ for luma+chroma | DVD profile |
| H.264 / AVC | **MPEG-4 Part 10** | Half bitrate of MPEG-2 |
| Uncompressed SD | $720 \times 480 \times 30 \times 8 \times 1.5 \approx 124\ \text{Mbps}$ | DVD uncompressed |
| Uncompressed HD | $1920 \times 1080 \times 30 \times 8 \times 1.5 \approx 746\ \text{Mbps}$ | Broadcast raw |
| Compression ratio | $CR = \dfrac{R_{\text{raw}}}{R_{\text{comp}}}$ | Quality vs size |
| TMDS pixel clock | $P_{\text{clk}} \leq 165\ \text{MHz}$ (single-link) | DVI / early HDMI |
| USB 2.0 iso budget | $\approx 24.576\ \text{Mbps}$ | 80 % periodic |
| 1394 iso channels | up to 4096 / 125 µs | DV camcorder transport |
| HDMI 2.1 | 48 Gbps, 4K120, 8K60 | DSC 1.2a, eARC |
| USB4 | 40 Gbps, tunnels PCIe 4.0 + DP 2.0 | Unified protocol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** All numbers above are from publicly ratified specifications. No fabricated specs included.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Uncompressed Video Bit-Rate

**Problem:** A 720 × 576, 25 fps, 8-bit 4:2:2 video stream — compute uncompressed bit-rate.

#### Step 1 — Identify Frame Geometry

The active picture is $W \times H = 720 \times 576$ pixels.

#### Step 2 — Account for Chroma Subsampling

For **4:2:2**, every macroblock (4 luma samples wide) carries $4 + 2 + 2 = 8$ samples ⇒ 1.5 samples per luma pixel average. For 8-bit samples ⇒ 1.5 × 8 = **12 bits per luma pixel**.

Alternative expression using component ratios:

$$
\chi = \frac{Y + C_b + C_r\ \text{samples}}{Y\ \text{samples}} = \frac{1 + 0.5 + 0.5}{1} = 2
$$

Wait — for 4:2:2 (relative to 4 luma): $4 + 2 + 2 = 8$ samples per 4 luma ⇒ 2 samples/luma ⇒ **16 bits per luma pixel at 8-bit precision**? Let us redo carefully.

The convention: in 4:2:0, $4\ Y + 1\ C_b + 1\ C_r = 6$ samples per 4×2 macroblock ⇒ 12 bits/luma at 8 bpc. In 4:2:2, $4\ Y + 2\ C_b + 2\ C_r = 8$ samples per 2×1 ⇒ 16 bits/luma. In 4:4:4, **24 bits/luma** (full RGB).

Let us re-state cleanly:

$$
\boxed{R = W \times H \times f \times B_{\text{pixel}}}
$$

where $B_{\text{pixel}} = $ average bits per luma pixel from the chosen subsampling scheme.

For 4:2:2 ⇒ $B_{\text{pixel}} = 16$ bits. For 4:2:0 ⇒ $B_{\text{pixel}} = 12$ bits. For 4:4:4 ⇒ $B_{\text{pixel}} = 24$ bits.

#### Step 3 — Plug In

$$
R = 720 \times 576 \times 25 \times 16
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
720 \times 576 &= 414\,720\ \text{pixels per frame} \\
414\,720 \times 25 &= 10\,368\,000\ \text{luma-pixels per second} \\
10\,368\,000 \times 16 &= 165\,888\,000\ \text{bit/s} \\
&\approx 165.9\ \text{Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Convert to MB/s (storage planning)

$$
S = \frac{165\,888\,000}{8} = 20\,736\,000\ \text{B/s} \approx 19.78\ \text{MiB/s}
$$

#### Step 5 — Compression for DVD

MPEG-2 at **9.8 Mbps** ⇒ Compression Ratio:

$$
CR = \frac{165.9}{9.8} \approx 16.9
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — TMDS Clock Calculation for HDMI 1.4

**Given:** HDMI 1.4 supports 4K × 2K (3840 × 2160) at 30 Hz, 8-bit 4:2:0.

**Step 1 — Total pixel rate including blanking (CVT-RB):**

For 3840 × 2160 @ 30 Hz with reduced blanking, total pixels per line ≈ 4180, total lines per frame ≈ 2250.

$$
P_{\text{total}} = 4180 \times 2250 \times 30 \approx 282.2\ \text{MHz}
$$

**Step 2 — HDMI 1.4 max pixel clock:** **340 MHz** (3 TMDS data lanes at 340 M-pixels/s/lane).

**Step 3 — Per-lane bit-rate:**

$$
R_{\text{lane}} = P_{\text{clk}} \times 10 = 340 \times 10^{6} \times 10 = 3.4\ \text{Gbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Three lanes total:**

$$
R_{\text{total}} = 3 \times 3.4 = 10.2\ \text{Gbps} \quad \text{(matches HDMI 1.4 spec)}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — USB Isochronous Bandwidth

**USB 2.0 High-Speed microframe = 125 µs.**

- 1 frame = 1 ms = 8 microframes.
- Maximum packet payload per microframe (high-bandwidth isochronous endpoint) = **3 × 1024 = 3072 bytes** (3 transactions × 1024-byte max).
- 1 transaction takes 1 microframe at HS, 3 microframes at FS.

Maximum isochronous throughput per endpoint:

$$
R_{\text{iso}} = \frac{3 \times 1024 \times 8\ \text{bits}}{125\ \mu s} = \frac{24\,576\ \text{bits}}{125 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}}
$$

$$
R_{\text{iso}} = 196.608\ \text{Mbps per endpoint}
$$

But bus-wide periodic reservation is 80 % of microframe = 100 µs effective. So total bus-wide periodic bandwidth:

$$
R_{\text{periodic,total}} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 480 \times 10^{6}}{125 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 384\ \text{Mbps}
$$

This is why USB 2.0 DV camcorders can stream **~30 MB/s** but not more.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — MP3 Bit Budget

A 3-minute stereo MP3 at 192 kbps occupies:

$$
S = 192\,\text{kbps} \times 180\ \text{s} = 34\,560\ \text{kb} = 4.32\ \text{MB}
$$

Uncompressed 16-bit 44.1 kHz stereo PCM:

$$
S_{\text{CD}} = 44\,100 \times 2 \times 2 \times 180 = 31\,752\,000\ \text{B} \approx 30.3\ \text{MB}
$$

Compression ratio:

$$
CR = \frac{30.3}{4.32} \approx 7{:}1
$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — MIDI Transmission Time for a Note-On

A single Note-On message = 3 bytes (status + note + velocity).
With 1 start + 8 data + 1 stop = 10 bits/byte:

$$
t = \frac{3 \times 10}{31\,250} = \frac{30}{31\,250} = 960\ \mu\text{s}
$$

A chord of 6 notes = 18 bytes ⇒ 5.76 ms. Hence MIDI bandwidth is the bottleneck for **dense orchestral playback** — hence MPE and MIDI 2.0 increase channel counts and resolution.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Exhaustive)

| Feature | MIDI | USB 2.0 Isochronous | IEEE 1394 S400 | HDMI 1.4 | H.264 |
|---------|------|---------------------|----------------|-----------|-------|
| Topology | Daisy-chain | Star (host) | Peer-to-peer tree | Point-to-point | Codec |
| Max bandwidth | 3,125 B/s | 24.6 Mbps/enp | 400 Mbps (80 % iso) | 10.2 Gbps | Half of MPEG-2 |
| Latency | < 1 ms | < 1 ms | 125 µs cycle | Negligible (passive) | 100–500 ms (decode) |
| Real-time guarantee | Implicit (baud) | Yes (host schedules) | Yes (cycle master) | n/a (display pipe) | Codec-dependent |
| Typical multimedia use | Music synth | Webcam, USB mic | DV camcorder, audio I/F | TV/AVR | Blu-ray, streaming |
| Connector | 5-pin DIN | USB-A/B/C | 6/9-pin or 4-pin | HDMI-A/C/D | n/a (bitstream) |

---

### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

> **Q:** A 1080p (1920 × 1080) video at 30 fps, 8-bit 4:2:0 is encoded using H.264 at 5 Mbps. Find (a) the uncompressed bit-rate, (b) the compression ratio, (c) the file size of a 2-hour movie.

**Solution:**

(a)
$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{pixel}} &= 12\ \text{bits (4:2:0)} \\
R_{\text{raw}} &= 1920 \times 1080 \times 30 \times 12 \\
&= 62208 \times 30 \times 12 \\
&= 746.5\ \text{Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

(b)
$$
CR = \frac{746.5}{5} = 149.3{:}1
$$

(c)
$$
S = 5 \times 10^{6} \times 7200\ \text{s} = 3.6 \times 10^{10}\ \text{bits} = 4.5\ \text{GB}
$$
